AT 9:03 A.M., WITH CHAIR ALICE L LEE PRESIDING.

order.

DEPUTY COUNTY CLERK; Chair, proceeding with the presentationof testimony on the
ceremonial resolution.Members of the publicmay testifyin the Council Chamber, at
courtesy testimony sites,or remotely through the information listedon the meeting
agenda.
Individualsinthe Chamber may sign up to testifywith the staffmember located at the
desk in the 8th floorlobby. Individualsat courtesy testimony sitesmay sign up with
staffat the site.Individualsparticipatingremotely may sign up inthe meeting chat. If
you are connected to the meeting by phone, you willautomatically be added to the
testifierlist.Individualsconnected by phone are currentlyon mute and willbe unmuted
when itistheirtime to testify.Individualsconnected by video conference should keep
both theirvideo and microphones offand should turnthem on only when itistheirtime
to testify.
Testimony islimitedto the items listedon the agenda. Individualsmay testifyforthree
minutes with up to one minute to conclude per item.When testifying,please stateyour
name and the name of any organization you represent, as well as the item on which
you willbe testifying.Please also identifyto the Council ifyou are a paid lobbyist.
Individualsmay be disconnected from the meeting at any time forbreaking decorum.
At thistime, we willbe taking testimony on the ceremonial resolutiononly. The first
person signed up to testifyon the ceremonial resolutionis Lucienne de Naie. To be
followed by BridgetMowat.
PRESENTATION OF ORAL TESTIMONY
MS. LUCIENNE DE NAIE:
Aloha, Councilmembers. Nice to see allof you today. This isLucienne de Naie from
Huelo. Idon’t,Idon'thave the privilegeof knowing Colette well,but Ihave worked with
her on variousprojectsand issuesformany, many years and she willbe deeply missed.
She had a dedicationto things and a focus that reallystood out among those who
deeply care about our aina and our people. And I am very sad to know thatwe have
one less voice to speak forallthe important things thatshe stood forallofthese years.
So mahalo forthisopportunity.

DEPUTY COUNTY CLERK: The next individualsigned up to testifyisBridget Mowat. To be
followed by Juanita Reyher Colon.
MS. BRIDGET MOWAT:
Good morning. Good morning, Councilmembers. And Ijustwant to speak on behalf
of my family,the Mowat's, we liveon Molokai. My name is Bridget Mowat and Ijust
wanted to share my relationshipwith Colette has been a long one. Iremember when
she firstcame to Molokai. We were ata meeting, or we were atan officeplace upstairs
from, Ithinkitwas American Savings, but she taught me how to use a thesaurus. She
told me, showed me the book, and told me what itwas used for,and Ithought thatwas
fabulous. I was older than her, married with children,and I didn'teven know what a
thesaurus was. So, I down, I don't know what I did, but she showed itto me, and I
thought, no wonder she know plenty of words and no wonder she can write so well.
So, from then on, I'm a user of the thesaurus.
She was a mana wahine, very articulate.She, when she had business to be done, she
went rightat it.She was always, she did her homework. Whenever we were attending
meetings, she would always question these tons ofdocuments that,thatshould've,that
we allshould have read thatwe, that Iwould get bored inthe middle of it.But she read
everything,and she knew justwhat to ask. She was smart. She reallyloved Molokai.
The lasttime Ispoke with her,she was inthe hospital,and she was coming home. So,
I know she loved Molokai very much. So, Ijustwanted to share that. She willbe
missed. Ihave been to many meetings with her,and boy, when she startedpounding
tables,and making her point clear,I didn'tknow whether I should run, or what, man.
She was, she's a tough cookie, and she was always thatfront,frontline,she was on
the frontlineof every battlewe fought, so Ireallyhave a lotof respect forher, and she
willbe missed. And we love the family. And Isend my condolences. So, thank you,
Keani and Councilmembers forallowing us this,thistime to share our mana'o, and God
bless you all.Mahalo.

Mahalo. Aloha, Chairwoman Lee and Members of the County Council. My name is
Juanita Reyher-Colon. I'm a residentof Mapulehu, Molokai. Mahalo for allowing me
the time to speak today regarding item E. on the agenda. I want to extend my
condolences to the family of Aunty Colette Machado. I'vehad the privilegeto have
known Aunty notjustas a member ofthe Molokai community, but alsoas a fellowboard
member on the Molokai Land Trust Board.
As we allknow. Aunty Colette was very passionate about Native Hawaiian issues and
rights,as well as our aina. She leaves behind a legacy that transcends our pae ‘aina.
We should allbe proud of the work and advocacy Aunty Colette has done over the
years. Mahalo Aunty Colletteforbeing a mana wahine, a mentor to myself and others,
and a champion of aloha aina. She willbe missed. Mahalo piha.

MS. CHERYL CORBIELL:
Mahalo, Honorable Maui County representatives. And mahalo for the resolution
regarding the contributionsof Colette Machado. I support the resolutionwith much
aloha. Iwas privilegedto work alongside Colette and her husband Myron forseveral
decades. Colette isknown for her work in the County and State areas as an elected
representative,but she was a teacher, a coach, a mentor, volunteer to many
organizations.For example, several non-profitswould not existtoday withoutColette's
guidance, such as the Molokai Land Trust. The renewable energy non-profitson
Molokai, both were started initiallywith the seeds by ColetteMachado.
Whether fightingunsuitableprojectsor forsuitableprojectsand planninga renewable
future, as well as the awareness and activism . . . invasive . . and implementing
something thatthe government said we could not do, and that isto do a research-
oriented 28-mile survey of the Molokai south shore, which is used today by many
entities.She was involvedindozens and dozens of groups and projects.The ... did
become a gathering place as per itsnamesake, with teaching keikiabout ogo and
restoration work at the site.
She helped familiesin need, eitherusing her own resources, or who, gettingthem in
touch withotherorganizations.Istillhear her voice,even though she'snothere,telling
me what to do, which ispono regardless of who isin opposition,to do my homework
and research, and never give up. Colettewas ...because she always spoke so frankly
and honestly. But she gave of her time,selflessly,expertiseand energy tohelpHawaii,
and especiallyMolokai. Mahalo forrecognizing thiswonderful Hawaiian woman and
leader. Mahalo.

Iwas born in a generation where when Iwas coming up in community service,itwas
reallyjust me. And Aunty them had been in thisdig for a long, long time. And so, I
came with allmy anxiousness and zeal, and limitedexperience and thought, and she
was quick to put me in my place. Iremember her making me cry at meetings and stuff
afterItestified.And, and where some might take offense,what that did for me was
show me that aloha was never meant to be weak, that when 1come to stand in defense
of my community, and my family,Ibettercome knowing what Italkingabout. When I
come with passion, and allthese great ideas, 1bettermake sure Idid my homework.
And so, thatset me on a course to have integrity,and to be sure that Iwas working for
the betterment of the whole, no matter how hard or alone sometimes you are,thatthe
benefitto thiscommunity was more than justone person. And she taught me allthose
things.
Later on in life,when Igrew up, and she and Ichose to follow Ke Akua ina dedicated

allthe lines. She said so many of us in our Hawaiian culture,and in our effortto help
the lahuihave created unnecessary linesand divisions.
And Iam so blessed to have known her and Uncle Myron, to have been loved by them,

MR. WALTER RITTE:
Aloha. I'mtryingto see ifIcould get me on the screen, but Iguess not. The firstthing
that comes to my mind when you talkabout Colette Machado isthat she was a great
lady. There's no doubt inmy mind thatthisisgoing to be a huge loss to allof us on
Molokai. Idon'tknow how we're going to fillthatvoid. But Iknow there'sa lotof young
people coming up, and they can emulate her and fillthat void.
She was a leader. She was a leader that no matter who was in the room, you knew
she was one of the leaders in that room. No nonsense, that'sforsure. And yet,she
had a very spiritualside. Iwas inmany, many, many, many, many battleswith Colette
Machado over the years. Imean, a lotof times we were together and there was a few
times we were againsteach other,but we always stayed together atthe end, no matter
whether we agreed with each other or not.
I'dliketo give you one example of how diverse thislady was. When we were doing
battlesbecause we didn'twant the cruiseships to come to Molokai, she got everybody
together before sunrise and said to everybody that we were gathered there to give
voice for the things that had no voice. And letus know thatwe were fightingcruise
ships not just because of problems on Molokai, that we only have one bathroom for
2,000 people at the wharf, but thatwe were there to protectthe reefand the thingsthat
Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 12
going be damaged by thishuge ship. And then we went intoJoe Souki's office. He
was incharge of transportationforthe State,and we triedto convince him not to allow
these ships to come to Molokai. And she and Iwalked in the door, and we talked really
nicelyto Mr. Souki, and itdidn'ttake more than three minutes before we had a swearing
match going on. And of course, she won the swearing match. And there was no ships
coming to Molokai. That was Colette Machado. She willbe missed. And mahalo to
allof you forallowingus to share some of the storiesof thisgreat lady. Aloha.

MS. STACY CRIVELLO:
Aloha. Aloha, Chair Lee, Vice-Chair Rawlins-Fernandez, and Councilmembers, and
of course, Myron Akutagawa. Aloha mai kakou. On behalf of my personal friendship
with Colletteand my Helm Ohana, we would liketo mahalo, mahalo you foryour explicit
words of aloha and honor forColetteYvette Pi'ipi'iMachado Akutagawa. Molokai and
Hawaii Nei were blessed to be benefactors of Colette's unselfish works. With her
politicalexperience, she led us through many bumpy and stressfulpaths, but her
leadershiphas managed to weather the frequent storms by focusing on the needs and
rightsof kanaka maoli.
Colette'saccomplishments are endless, and time does not allow me to listher works.
And knowing Colette,she would shy away from thiskind of attention.However, the
well-being of her Hawaiian familiesand communities were always at the top of her
serving ladder. I know Colette as one who has, firstof ail,her love forour heavenly
father,her husband Myron, her ohana. These are allsynonymous of her life'sjourney.
Iwillalways cherish her friendshipand appreciate how we always talkedabout meeting
the needs of our island home.
I'dliketo share a quote. Colette would say, "Molokai is my ‘ainahanau, my land of
birth.It'salso my land of my kupuna kahiko, or ancestors. And lastly,her place of. ..
iwi,where Iwillremain when Ihala". These are quotes from our ColetteMachado. Her
journey has led her unto a glow of unearthly splendor, where she continues her works
as our prayerfulwarrior. Today, she leaves us a canopy of works with love and aloha.

Mahalo, Colette. Mahalo, Myron. Mahalo, Councilmember Keani, for this special

MS. HANNAH BERNARD:
Oh, kala mai. Aloha kakou and salut, Councilmembers and Chair. I'm just hoping to
speak on behalfof Colette Machado, because of my experience with her so long ago
when the whale sanctuary was firstestablishedby an act of Congress, and very,very
hotlycontested. And many people were concerned about the Federal overlayaddition
and allthe various potentialfor problems. And knowing allof her work, and how
balanced she was inthe way thatshe thought and felt,Isought her counsel intryingto
findout from community members and leaderswhat they thought about the sanctuary,
and is itthe rightthing,and could itwork, and how could we move forward and have
an extra layerof support forour humpback whales in Hawaii?
And I'llnever forget,she said a lotof things,but one of the things she said to me, I
carrywith me always is,Ithinkabout my sister,you know, do 1want protectionformy
sister? Do Iwant protectionfor my own family? And itreminds me so much of the
importance and the beauty and the value of that connectiveness, that allthings,
especiallyas documented inthe creation chant, the kumulipo, the,all,allof Kanaloa,
all of the creatures in the sea are manifestations of Kanaloa and how her bringing it
back to our connectedness and our abilityto take care of each other,that’swhat we
should be lookingat. And so, Ijustreally.I'm so gratefulthatyou're honoring her this
way, because she was an extraordinarybeing and meant a lotto allof us. Mahalo.

VICE-CHAIR RAWLINS-FERNANDEZ: Mahalo, Chair. Mahalo, Deputy Clerk. Your
pronunciations of the olelo,pua oleloHawaii were perfect.
Aunty Colettewas our champion, as you heard, our warrior,our aloha aina. Aunty Lori,
see, you made me startcrying. She never minced words. As one friendKevin Chang
shared, she was more directthan most of humanity, and didn'tseem to fear much, if
anyone. And as Uncle Walter stated,she was just no nonsense. Colette Machado
was always thinkingabout the kua aina and protectingour aina. She loved Molokai,of
which she was so protective.She gave voice to those who had no, you know, voice,
our aina, our wai, our reef,kohola.
We often hear community organizers encourage those they mobilizeto letthe anger
and rage in response to injusticeto fueltheirfight,and that'show I remember Aunty
Colette. She was a fighterforour people, especiallythe oppressed and downtrodden,
something she herselffeltas a child.She was a strong administratorof the Officeof
Hawaiian Affairs,and the leader who mentored and upliftedour lahui.Whether itwas
using a thesaurus, or likemyself, in supporting me to serve as the president of our
Hoolehua Hawaiian Civic Club.
She came from a time and a place where you had to fight.She was also no victim.
She was rough and tumble. She was bullheaded and charged likea bull.As you heard
from allthe testifiers,you had to come ready. As Zhan reflected,itwas likesteel
sharpening steel. As a wahine myself, growing up with her as a role-model, also
shaped me intoa stronger person.
As Mr. Chang beautifullycaptured,she was not afraidto dress-down. She could walk
intoany room with a baseball cap, blue mu'umu'u, and localslippers,and dominate
that room. While in her more feistieryears, people may have feared gettinga knuckle
sandwich when she startedpounding on desks. She gained more patience as she got
older. And instead,she might justlower her glasses and gaze out and add something.

the path thatshe was layingand where she was going. There were people who disliked

MR. DAVID DORN Testifying on Committee Report No. 22-61):
Hello,Councilmembers. My name isDavid Dorn. I'velivednext to 16 East Waipu'ilani
for over twenty years, and I oppose the Hale Waipu'ilaniProject. I'veexperienced
many floods while livingin thisarea and I believe that thisprojectwillmake flooding
worse. We shouldn'tbe buildingmore houses in Kihei’sworst floodzone. There are
betterplaces to buildhousing away from flood plainsand coastal hazards. Kihei has
an aging infrastructure,bad traffic,and lack of area drainage. This localarea has no
storm water drainage at alland isknown to flood.
As you know, thisdeveloper has been offered a land swap. The County has begun
discussing a land swap ina more suitablelocation.There are 1200 home sitesjustup
the hillinthe R&T Park, land by the Police Station,Maui Lani, and Puunene. These
are places where the new housing should be located. Itisup to the County to decide
who and what you want to protect. Sites such as 16 East Waipu'ilanido not have to
meet the specific.. .Army Corps of Engineers wetland criteriato be important to the
watershed.
Iask thatthispropertybe put intoconservation and saved from development. Please
keep thispropertyas is. Preserving thislothas many benefitsfrom area drainage,will
not increase flooding and will not overburden the local community or its aging
infrastructure.
And as a reminder, thisCouncil has no obligationto approve thisproject,but you do
have an obligationto protectthe current residentsand tryto make our community safe
and free from risk. There are 2500 affordable homes in the pipeline,so disapproving
this project willhave a negligibleimpact on affordable housing. Disapproving this
28 unitprojectwillhave about a one percent reduction of houses inthe pipeline.
The decision you make today must consider how you can do the leastharm and do the
most good? This Council has a legalduty and moral obligationto protectthe safetyof
the community and the responsibilityto ensure that future homes are only located in
safe places. Irespectfullyask thatyou disapprove thisprojectand set us on a path to
a better,safer future. Thank you.

MS. JILL WIRT (testifying on Committee Report No. 22-61):
Good morning. Council Chair Lee, Vice-Chair Rawlins-Fernandez, and
Councilmembers. I'm JillWirt, and I am a projectmanager for Maui Nui Marine
Resource Council.
Like many in our community, the Maui Nui Marine Resource Council recognizes the
need for workforce housing. However, the proposed Hale Waip'uilani housing
development is in an ecological significantarea that serves an important role in
protectingcoral reefs,ocean water qualityinthe coastalareas of North Kihei.
Regular Meeting ofthe Councilofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 34
As you know the Kihei reefhelps protectSouth Maui's shores from storm surges and
erosion. The Kihei reef tract has also been identified as an important source of coral
larvaeforthe reefsofMaui County, especiallythose of Molokai. Inother words, Kihei’s
reef help seed reefsthroughout Maui Nui so they can grow and be replenished. What
you decide about Hale Waipu’ilaniwillhave a significantimpact on the futureof corals
in Kihei and Maui Nui.
Let'stalkabout Waipu'ilantGulch, with originatesin the moku of Kula. When itrains
Upcountry, stormwater flows from this extremely large watershed area through
Waipu'ilaniGulch pickingup speed and sediment along the way. Fortunately,when
thisfast-moving storm water hitsthe floodplain,that'smakai of the highway, itslows
down, loses energy, and spreads out inthe widening partofthe stream bed. Tons of
soilcarriedby the water can then settleonto the ground. This protectsthe coralreef
by keeping sediment out of ocean. Excess sediment isvery harmful to coralreefs,
interferingwiththe coral'sabilityto generate food forthemselves and to reproduce. If
Hale Waipu'ilaniisbuilt,we would lose the floodplainand the filteringand the flood
preventionitprovidesto the detrimentofthe community and the corals.
The proposed housing should not be builtinthisenvironmentallysignificantarea. We
support a land swap with the developer, through which the County would trade some
of itsown lands thatare more suitableforhousing inexchange forthe Waipu'ilaniGulch
land.Thiswillallowthe County toenhance the Waipu'ilanifloodplaintofurtherimprove
itsabilityto hold and filterstormwater run-offto protectcoralsinKihei'scoastalareas.
There's potentiallya large-scalefinancialsupportfortheseenhancements, as well.As
a startMaui Nui Marine Resource Council is in receipt of a $225,000 grant from the
National Fish and Wildlife Foundation for a site assessments and preliminarydesigns
for nature-based solutionsfor the floodplainof Waipu'ilaniGulch, as well as the
Kulanihakoi and Keokea Gulches to prevent coastal flooding and sediment runoff.
These, thisgrant isa pipelinegrant,meaning there willbe futureopportunitiestoapply
for next steps,includingpermitting,design, and buildingor installingthe nature-based
solutions,which could equal millionsof dollarscoming intoMaui County.
However, some of thispossiblefloodpreventionwork would become impossibleifthe
Waipu'ilanifloodplainis developed. The coral reefs,ocean water qualityand the
community of North Kiheiwould suffer.Please expedite the effortto createthisland
swap and allowthe County to keep thissectionofWaipu'ilaniGulch as open space as
a step towards protectingthe coralreefsof the Kiheireeftract.Mahalo.

MR. JIM HAYES [testifyinaon BillNo. 21 CD1 EDI (2022)1:
Thank you. Council. My name is Jim Hayes, l‘m a principalplanner with Planning
Solutionsin Honolulu. I‘mtestifyingon Bill21.
I have years of experience assessing impacts to threatened endangered seabirds in
Hawaii and I led a team that recently completed a Draft Environmental Assessment of
the proposed Maui County StreetlightConversion Project. The Draft Environmental
Assessment willappear inthe July 23 editionof The Environmental Notice.
The conversion projectinvolvesthe replacement of the approximately4,900 County-
owned streetlightfixtures.That would involvethe removal ofthe existinghigh pressure
sodium lightsand the installationof fully-shielded,energy-saving lightemittingdiode,
or LED streetlights.The LEDs would have a corralatedcolortemperature or CCT of
2700K, and itcould be wirelessly controlled,and they comply with the existing
administrativerules. The projectincludes the implementation of adaptive lightingto
avoid and minimize impacts to wildlife;those measures would includedimming when
and where appropriate.
In the Draft Enviromental Assessment we consider an alternative that would utilize a
lower blue lightcontent streetlight,such as the one thatwould be requiredunder Bill21.
That alternative was eliminated, and Bill21 should be reconsidered for the following
reasons:
A lower blue lightcontent alternativewould not provide bettershieldingof the lightand
that isthe most criticalelement when avoiding and minimizing impacts to streetlights.
The potentialwildlifeimpact associated with an alternativethat used low blue light
fixturescountywide would likelybe similarto the impacts associatedwiththe proposed
conversion project.And there are two main reasons forthis.First,there'sno evidence
that seabird lightattractionwould be meaningfully reduced using a low blue light
content fixture. Itis important to note that the U.S. Fish and WildlifeService and
Hawaii's Department of Land and Natural Resources do not mention using a low blue
lightproduct to avoid or minimize impacts to Hawaii's seabirds. Second, the proposed
conversion projectincludes adaptive lightingmeasures, such as the use of low blue
Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 61
lightcontent fixturesincertainshorelinelocations.This isimportant because itissea
turtles,not seabirds that are adversely affected by blue light.
Additionalreasons fordiscardingthe blue lightcontent alternativeincludethatthe 2016
demonstration projecton Maui Lani Parkway showed us that residentsand agency
representativespreferstreetlightsthathave more than 15 percent blue light.The public
and safetyagencies would finda low blue lightalternativeunappealing and potentially.
CHAIR LEE: Mr. Hayes. Mr. Hayes, could you conclude your testimony,please.

MR. HAYES: The publicand safetyagencies would findlow blue lightalternativeunappealing
and potentiallydetrimentalto theirsafety,as EricMiyasato demonstrated.
Further considering safety, the County of Maui Police Department told us as we
prepared the draftEA, that the LED lightingis recommended in a policytitled"Crime
Prevention Through Environmental Design." The LEDs are recommended in that
policybecause they allow color and objects to be more visibleand appear sharper.
However, those benefits are only realizedwhen the LED utilizedproduces a broad
spectrum of light.A low blue lightLED would not produce a low,would not produce a
broad spectrum of lightand, therefore,be inconsistentwith the desires of the Maui
PoliceDepartment and inconsistentwiththe Crime PreventionThrough Environmental
Design policies.

Igrew up around inSanta Clara Valley in California,inan area calledPsomas. We

successful. It'snot going to happen up there on the side of Haleakala.

MS. GINA CHAMBERLAIN (testifyingon Committee Report No. 22-62):
Hi. I am Gina Chamberlain, and I am the single-familyprogram manager for Rural

also to the Affordable Housing Committee. So, Iwanted to speak reallyquicklyon, I
have itrighthere, 22-62, on the,to change the,to a new method of determining the
sales priceforaffordablehousing unit.
We are reallyinsupport of thisinthat itbrings the, the kind of the underwritingcriteria,
the way the program is designed in alignment with some of the main affordable
financing options for low- and moderate-income households. It,some of the main
sources would be FHA and also USDA 502 Direct and 502 Guaranteed. So to
eliminate,currentlythose mortgage financingoptionsdon't reallyfitwith the way the
program is designed so you're eliminatingone of the, some of the largestpools of
affordablefinancing.
And then when you put thatin lightof the reallyhigh rentcosts,the idea of folkswho
are already cost burdened coming down with the down payment requirements thatare
required to make conventional financingwork isunrealistic.And then by making this
change, you are then adding the key normal components when you'relookingatthat,
what we callthe front-endratioor the housing ratio.And by adding that,that means
you are helpingfolksget intoa house, and not startoffbeing extremely cost burdened,
but startingoffin a way that'saffordableso thatthe likelihoodof them being able to
succeed on the long-term is,ismuch greater.
So, Ithinkby making thischange you are opening up those huge mortgage pools of
funds that folks can access, and also then enables other partnershipsto get additional
resources that often have the same criteria that are attached. Thank you.

Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 91
This is the fourth time I'm coming to you to say this. This Council paid Hawaii
Community Assets $300,000 to develop a housing plan. We engaged over
1,600 County residentsand had 28 affordablehousing experts who over eightmonths
did the hard work of defininga comprehensive approach thatwould work in creating
certaintyand lead to more affordablehomes forlocalresidentsas fastas possible.
I'veheard multipletimes thatfunding isavailableto subsidizebuildersand thatisgreat,
but we need to subsidize the buyers directly.Here's why. Subsidizingthe buildersand
not our localpeople leads to additionalState regulatoryrequirements which could
extend the projectby up to three years. The substance provided by the County gets
eaten up in that time as the builder pays intereston the loan. For 100 percent
affordablehousing builders,they can'taffordthree years indelay.
The use of bonds as well,the use of bonds isa great toolforaffordablehousing, but
inan interestrateenvironment likeyou have today, bonds are not as attractiveas they
were when you'reat historicallylow interestrates.The bonds can'tbe soldtosubsidize
the builderor even the buyer forthatmatter,a projectwillbe dead.
So. I'm hoping thisfourthtime that we put forward some language that can starta
conversation with you folksto help us get the language rightthe firsttime;right?Create
the certaintywe need, givethe flexibilityto address the shiftsinthe economy, and most
importantly,allow our locallow-income people to buy the homes they can affordand
ensure our builders can continue to build the affordable homes. So thank you so much.

MS. NAPUA HUEU (testifyingon Resolution No. 22-119 CD1 FDD:
Aloha, Chair and County Councilmembers. My name isNapua Hueu and I'mtestifying
in fullsupport of Resolution 22-119. The County of Maui speaks a lotabout food
securityand transitioningaway from our relianceon tourism. This resolutionisone
measure to tangiblyensure that happens.
My father'ssideofthe familyhas been farming kaloforseven generationsinEast Maui.
In 1983 with evident riseintourism, my grandmother startedone ofthe firstfruitstands
up on the Hana Highway. Twelve years later,she sold her portionof the company to
her partner at the time hoping to focus more on our family'starofarming operations.
After years of limitedfarming capabilitieslargelydue to the lack of stream water
resources, but also partiallydue to the lackof manpower. As the lackof stream water
dissolved interestin farming, drivingour generationalfarmers out of the region,and
forcingmost ofthem intoconstructionlaboring.Given these dynamics oflackofstream
water and lack of manpower, my family had no other choice but to returnto tourism
with my grandmother buildinganother fruitstand down on the peninsulawhere itstands
today.
The water diversion construct as is disables East Maui's farming potentialand forces
us to relyon tourism to subsidize our needs. Itshould not be likethismoving forward.
In2014, Imyself had to take a career intourism despitemy preferenceand longingto
be a full-time taro farmer. The water diversion conditions under the current corporate
management structuredoes not allowus to transitionout oftourism.Itisthe County's
duty and the best interestof our civiliansto acquire the water deliverysystem and
ensure thatitismanaged by the people and forthe people.
Resolution 22-119, would harness the place-specificintelligenceof East Maui farmers
as itrelatesto best managing our water resources. Ifeelthe resolutionwas carefully
craftedand intricatelyreflectsthe years and years of testimonyby East Maui farmers
who have the utmost potentialto feed Hawaii nutriousfoods. I appreciatethe
schematics of the resolution that ensures for a diverse representationto avoid the
smaller farmers being leftout of decisionmaking.
As a volunteer coordinator of Honomanu lo'irestorationproject,my cousin, Jesse
Nakaoka and myself are currentlythe only generationalEast Maui tarofarmers growing
food inthe boundaries of Honomanu ahupua'a at thistime. And yet under the current
Regular Meeting of the Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 101
water diversion construct, we are not consulted or accurately informed on how much
water isbeing taken, and we are not given the platformto communicate what the day
to day effectsof that water take are on our currectfarming activitiesand futurefarming
capabilities.Honomanu Stream is one of the largestin the East Maui watershed
contributingan astronomical amount of water forthe largerisland'suse.
Isupportthisresolutionthatinreturnforour specificcontributionand sacrificeto serve
the water needs ofmutlipleahupua'a milesaway, would give the smallertarofarmers,
likeourselves a means to inform the County on what exactly isgoing on inthe fieldin
order to maximize our agriculturalpotentialand output.
Please vote in favor of Resolution 22-119, which would substantiate and provide
evidence of your comprehension on the fact that the continued corporate
mismanagement of water compromises alleconomic industrieson this island. No
water,no Upcountry or CentralMaui ag and ranching.No water, no tourism. No water,
no development. We are farbeyond the thresholdof getting a legitimatehandle on
sound water managment to sustainably enable allof our other island's industries.
Please show the people of Maui thatyou support a responsibleresource management
by puttingResolution 22-119 on the ballot.Mahalo.

have seen this project numerous times. I'm also an architectand a planner so I
understand what I'm lookingat when Isee it.
I think the affordable condominium project proposed is an example of haste makes
waste. Yes, we need affordable homes to be available for purchase by our local
residents. My own daughter and her husband and childcannot affordto buy a home,
even though they both work. Itisdebatable as to how affordablethisprojectreallyis?
Most people seeking affordable housing fallinto income levels below what these
condos willbe offered at. Interestrates are risingmaking loans even harder to get.
These unitscould easilyfallintomarket ratesales as we saw the projectinNorth Kihei
at Kawahine did.
Regular Meeting of the Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 103
Affordable homes need to be good qualityand must have a healthyand sustainable
environment thatsupports good community development. There must be a long view
in the planning of affordable homes. This projectisvery shortsighted. Itislocated in
an area that isknown to flood regularly,sometimes catastrophicallycausing ruinto our
nearshore waters and damage to existinghomes. These problems willbe exacerbated
by the project.
Two, itis in an area that has no sidewalks, curbs, guttersor drainage infrastructure.
The developer istryingto exempt themselves from supplying these basic needs.
Three, the densityof housing and parking inthissmall sitewillbe very uncomfortable
for unsuspecting new as well as existingresidents. Itwillbe a heat island. Good
community lifewillnot be promoted.
Four, the sitedrainage proposals willnot work and they would be very unaffordableto
try to maintain. Pervious concrete willnot remain pervious due to dust and mud
infiltration.Grass pavers cannot be watered when cars are parked on them. Water
storage tanks beneath the buildingsare not effectiveinfloods.
Itwould be far less damaging to allow the existingR-3 zoning to prevailinthiscase.
The impact on floodingand congestion would likelybe much less. There would be
fewer than halfthe number of parking spaces requiredeven ifthe sitewere to be, able
to be developed to maximum capacity:which itprobably would not be.
Ideallythe siteshould be an example of wetland restroration,where the most good can
be done to recreate balance inthe floodingissues that plague thispartof Kihei. The
County would be wise to consider purchasing thislotor tradingthe owners fora more
appropriate site,where they can develop trulyaffordablehousing that would not be
saddled with these issues.

which urges the Mayor to authorizea comprehensive feasibilitystudy forthe EMI water
system in East Maui. A feasibilitystudy willaddress many of the concerns thatthe
Department has, and in my opinion,concerns which allof you, Councilmembers and
the general publicshould have before making a huge decision inthe formation of a
regionalcommunity board and the Maui Nui Community Water Systems. How can a
decision of this magnitude be made without having accurate cost of acquiring,
operating and maintaining these regional water systems and without knowing an
accurate number of the futurerevenue generated by these water systems?
I'llgive you an example. So, when you go to purchase a new car and saying thatnew
car islikethiswater system, do you justsay "Iwillbuy the car because Ilove it."Or
do most of us ask what the purchase pricewillbe, what the cost of maintenance will
be, does the car have everything you need, or you need to purchase accessories in

July 15, 2022
Page 133
the future? Another thingmost of us thinkabout once we know the totalcost of buying
thiscar is,am Iable to make the monthly payments with the amount Imake at my job,
my revenue. Ibelieve allof us willnot sign on the dotted lineto purchase thatcar until
we know allthe answers to these questions.
So, similarto making a decision on these water systems, there'sa lotof outstanding
questions, and I think we need to think a littlebitmore and have those questions
answered. DWS feelsthatthisresolutionisan importantfirststep in consideringthe
County's best interactionwith the East Maui water system, whichever agency takes the
lead in the State water lease process. We stillneed importantquestions answered
before we make huge decisions on water management issues, and we believethis
resolutionwould get us those answers.

So, firstone. Department requests that more claritybe given when the terms "water
collectionand deliverysystems" are used, thatthose terms are nottobe confusedwith
water collectionand deliverysystems under the jurisdictionofthe Department ofWater
Supply. We also request that Section 8-11.2 be clarifiedto acknowledge our
Department's ongoing abilityto acquire and manage water sources.
Second overallcomment, the Department regularlydevelops new sources and also
accepts sources such as privatewells or other infrastructure,ifitisinthe best interest
of the Department and our customers, and we want to be sure thatthe Department
continues to have the abilityto do so.

Page 134
So the comment is,"Wholesale regionalwater authoritysystems generally own or
operate allmajor water supply facilitiesincludingthe reservoirs,the treatment plants,
and transmission lines. The related costs of service are then billedto municipalities
that own and operate transmission facilitiesand the linesin service areas. Then they
issue end users the billings,and provide serviceconnections to end user customers.
The eighth "Whereas", reso page 2. The Department of Water Supply is an existing
government agency with the abilityto provide clean and safe drinking water at
affordablerateswhile managing revenue, operating expenses, capitalimprovements,
and debt service. The Maui Nui Community Water Systems willrelyupon ratecharges
to the Department. The administrativeoverhead and operationalcosts of running the
regionalwater systems willbe passed on to the Department of Water Supply in the
form of high wholesale water rates. The Department of Water Supply customers will
ultimatelyabsorb these rateincreases.
The 21st "Whereas", reso page 3. The Department of Water Supply has a long
standing practiceof supporting agriculture.Water costs forlocalfarmers and ranchers
continue to be subsidized by general use customers. The FiscalYear '22 Water Rate
Study prepared by Harris& Associates, indicatesthatagriculturalcustomers pay about
30 percent of the totalcost required to provide the water they use. Maui Nui Water
Systems "additionalrevenue" cannot translateto reduced agriculturalwater rates as
system costs would be funded by increased wholesale ratesto DWS. The Department
of Water Supply would then be requiredto pass on thisadditionalcostto allcustomers.
That goes hand in hand with thatfirstcomment on the "Whereas", where we pass, we
need to pass on the rateincreases to the ranchers and the farmers.
Now the following comments are made directlyto the amended Chapter 19.
Section 8-19.1, Organization. The comment that we have is hiring staff with
appropriate licenses necessary to manage water collectionand deliverysystem poses
significantstaffingchallenges due to a limitedselectionof qualifiedapplicants. For
example, engineers, and personnel with water system experience are inshort supply.
Having agencies with similarduties willmean that there willbe competition for the
limitednumber of qualifiedcandidates to fillvacancies. Providingstaffwith competitive
salaries, benefit packages, training,and necessary equipment along with the
acquisition,operation, and maintenance for water collectionand delivery systems
would be costly.

And I’m Albert Perez with Maui Tomorrow, and we support this Charter amendment
proposal. Allof our communities in Maui County deserve to have decisions that are
made about theircommunities ina way that issensitiveto the actual conditions on the
ground. Your neighbors are more likelyto make decisionsthatare appropriate foryour
neighborhood. Maui County isgrowing and it'stime to make sure thatwe're allequally
represented, and regional planning commissions willfurtherthat goal. With regard to
the claim that overlapping jurisdictionswillcreate problems, the jurisdictionsdo not
overlap. They're defined by the community plan region boundaries. Ifthere are issues
that affectmore than one jurisdiction,than those issues can be referredto more than
one planning commission just likethey are now. So, I urge you to fon/vardthisto the
people of Maui County fora decision in November.
Meanwhile, my next item in Kihei we have another housing projectbeing proposed in
wetlands. Of course, I'm speaking of Hale Waipu'ilani which translates to the
"waterspout." And Ithinkwe should pay attentionto thattraditionalculturalknowledge.
We are not doing any favors to people who are economically vulnerable and barely
Regular Meeting ofthe Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15. 2022
Page 157
able to afforda house by givingthem a chance to finallyhave a home inan area that
floods.
And the logic that there will be five homes there anyway escapes me, it'snot
guaranteed. They stillneed an SMA permit and even ifitdid happen there wouldn't be
as many people affectedby the inevitableflooding.People who can affordone ofthose
fivehomes would also be able to betterrecover financially.
Then according to the committee reportthe developer intends to receive a Federal
Emergency Management Agency letterof map amendment which would remove the
floodinsurance requirementforhomeowners. But accordingto the FEMA website, the
developer can request a letterof map revision,but there'sno guarantee that itwould
be granted. The LOMR must be, itmust consider other communities affected,property
owners nearby need to be notified,and it'ssubjectto appeal. So, it'snot guaranteed.
Also from the FEMA website, very importantly,issuance of a LOMR does not mean
that there'sno riskof flooding. They stress that not having a flood insurance policy
could have disastrousconsequences leavingyou with no financialprotectionfrom flood
losses. So, they recommend that you have flood insurance anyway, even with a
LOMR. And thiswilladd to the expense of livingthere and make itless affordable.
Ifwe keep approving projectslikethis,there willbe no wetlands leftto protectwith the
wetland's ordinance. And the reason thatthese projectskeep coming up isbecause
the land islessexpensive. Ifyou improve thisone, you willbe encouraging developers
to keep doing thison wetlands, in flood zones. Your disapproval would discourage
them.
Also, thiskind of projectwhere the deed restrictionisonly forten years, we're basically
chasing our tailsanyway. They willnot be affordableafterthat. In addition,as I
understand itifpeople don'tqualifyat lower income levelsand the County doesn't buy
the units,then they can be sold at market price.That isa windfallfordevelopers, and
Ibelieve that’sone reason thistype of projectisso attractive.Instead of the good kind
of projectlikeIkaikaOhana, Doug Bigley,those people.
Anyway, thisCouncil isalready moving inthe rightdirectionwhich isto help developers
be able to off-setthe higherpriceofmore suitableland by providing infrastructure.That
willprovide housing that isclearlyinthe publicinterest.Putting houses inflood zones
on wetlands is not.
Moving on. Let me take a sip,sorry. East Maui Community Water Authority. We
support this Charter amendment. It'stime for Maui County to take controlof it'sown
water resources. Having public water supply depend on privatewater systems ispoor
Regular Meeting of the Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 158
publicpolicyand itplaces the publicatthe mercy ofdecisionsthatare based on what's
best forthe company, not the publicinterest.It'salso risky.
Climate change and invasive species are making watersheds more susceptible to
landslides and watershed health will be criticalto maintainingany flow at all.Itmay or
may not be worth itfor a privatecompany to repairlandslidedamage to the ditches.
We don'twant that decision to be leftto a company's bottom-line when we're talking
about publictrustresources.
I'dalso liketo stressonce again to ensure thatthe EMI employees, who know how to
run thatsystem be retained,because they know itbest. And the water authoritywill
also need to work closelywith invasive species experts and others who can help
restorethe watershed. Water forseriousagriculturalusers can continue to be provided
balanced with instream uses such as kalo growing thathave priority.
With a publicwater management entity,decisions would be transparent, consistent
with the public trust,and an authoritymay be eligiblefor grant funding that'snot
availableto privatecorporations. This willoffsetthe costs that are of concern to the
Department of Water Supply and others. There has already been many investigations
regarding the feasibilityof this idea, not the least of which was the temporary
investigativegroup ofthe Board ofWater Supply. And Iwould justliketo compliment
Councilmember Sinenci,the Board of Water Supply, and allthe citizenswho have put
so much time intothoroughly vettingthisidea already.
Finally,I'dliketo pointout as Mr. Mayer did thatthe currentratesthatare being charged
to the County are not guaranteed. They're also subjectto corporate decision-making
that we're tryingto get out from under. So, thisCharter amendment would allow the
people of the County of Maui to take charge of theirown water future. And Iurge you
to approve itand send itto the people of Maui County fortheirdecision. Mahalo.

MR. KEONI KUOHA (testifyingon Resolution No. 22-144 CD1 FD1: and Committee Report
Nos. 22-61 & 22-62):
Aloha‘auinala. So close to the end. Aloha kakou. ‘O wau‘o Keoni Kuoha. I have the
privilegeof lobbyingthisCouncil as Directorof the House Maui Initiativeof the Hawaii
Community Foundation. Ialso come to you as a commissioner chosen by thisCouncil
to serve on the current Charter Commission. I'm here to affirmthat your Charter
Commission has fulfilleditskuleana. We spent over a year working to develop a final
set of proposals and we have put forward the best proposals possible.
Today you are consideringthree additions.Two ofthese proposals were never brought
before the Charter Commission so itmakes sense that thisCouncil consider adding
them to the ballot. However, one of these proposals was thoroughly vetted by the
Charter Commission and the majorityofthe commissioners did not believe thatputting
forward thisproposalwas inthe best interestof County residents.
I'm opposed to establishingplanning commissions for all community plan areas;
however, I didn'tstartoff with this view. When the proposal firstcame before the
Charter Commission, i supported itwholeheartedly. I thought itwould enable a
planning process that would represent the best interestsof our communities. Itwas
simple, more community voice in process creates outcomes that reflectthe needs of
the community. However, as Ilooked deeper intothe issue, Ichanged my mind.
Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 166
First,according to Harvard Law Review, localplanning boards are often subject to
capture by homeowners who oppose the constructionof affordablehousing out of
concern that a new development might change the character of theircommunity by
triggeringan influxof lower income and minority residents,creatingcongestion and
safety risksand depressing property prices. This observation is reinforcedacross the
affordable housing literature.
Second, according to the research literature,participantsinpublicmeetings are usually
not representative a cross-section of the general public. Instead they are
disproportionatelylikelyto be white, male, older,homeowners, frequent voters and
opposed to the projectat issue. This illustratesa deeply engrained equityissue inthe
land use planning process. The people withthe resources to participateinthe planning
process are not representativeof the broader community.
As a side note,today'stestimony against Hale Waipu'ilaniillustratesthispoint.These
two broadly established views in the research led me to my key concern that more
planning commissions willstifleaffordable housing development even more. This
Council has committed to housing as a human right.To fulfillthat commitment, the
only responsiblecourse of action isto oppose establishingplanning commissions for
allcommunity plan areas.
On two separate but ultimatelyrelatednotes,firstIsupport item 22-61, Hale Waipu'ilani
forthe reasons partlyIalready explained here. Please note that alltestifiersbrought
up the floodingissue, an issue that they got to choose to livewith. But most also
revealed that they simply prefer the neighborhood have less trafficand lower density
buildings. Itisunderstandable thatthey would have thispreference. The challenge
before thisCouncil isto put our community's need for affordable housing above the
preferences of the project'sneighbors. The Council should also address the flooding,
traffic,and safetyissues inthe area.
Second, I oppose item 22-62, Bill107 in itscurrent form. Jeff Gilbreath and I have
worked on some language thatwe believe accomplishes the goals of the current bill
while settingup the system for success. We would appreciate the opportunity to
discuss and furtherrefinethislanguage with the Council and other key stakeholders.
In closingjustwant to thank you for your patience and your fortitudein carryingout
your kuleana forthisCounty. Mahalo nui.

Sugimura.

incorporated intoAppendix A, Part II. These amendments do not affectthe total

holiday,thatthe deadline actuallymoves to the next workday. So, for a deadline that
was calculated for a Sunday, July 17, actual deadline in accordance with the Code,
would be Monday. July 18. Thank you.

Regular Meeting of the Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15. 2022
Page 203
in regular contact with the Office of Information Practices to try to understand the
nuances of this amended statute. The statute itselfjust says that Sunshine Law
boards, includingthe County Councils, and itsCommittees, can't require that all
testimony be atthe beginning ofthe,ofthe meeting, which the Council has done inthe
past. So, OIP has suggested thattestimony needs to be taken up eitherby item,or by
section,or both. Or you can exercise eitherofthose functionsduring a meeting before
you begin deliberationson a particularitem or sectionof items. There's no requirement
to allow testifiersto testifyat the beginning of the meeting. So, that'sa courtesy that
the Council allowed at the July 15 meeting. So, in essence, we, we agree with the
Deputy County Clerk's interpretation,and apologize for any confusion, again as we
continue to work out the nuances ofthisnew law. Thank you. Chair.

MS. SHAY CHAN HODGES (testifyingon County Communication 22-167):
Aloha. My name isShay Chan Hodges, and Iwould liketo address some of the issues
raisedby Water Deputy DirectorShayne Agawa. On Friday,he compared the goals
of a Community Water Authorityto the purchase of a car, raisingconcerns about its
monthly payments, maintenance, and operationalcosts. His analogy doesn’t quite fit
because the East Maui Water Authoritywould not be purchasing the aqueduct, only
requestingStatewater leases.And DLNR has clearlystatedthata County entitywould
not be charged forthose leases, so there isno cost foracquisition.
There isa partof Mr. Agawa's analogy that'sworth lookingat,however. As we consider
whether to take on the maintenance and gas forour free car from DLNR, we need to
remember thatwe are currentlypaying Mahi Pono and EMI to use thatsame car,which
EMI has been rentingfrom the State fordecades. And yes, the County has been
paying the same rentalrateforthe car since the 70s.
Infact,on July8, 1976, my husband's father,Tony Hodges, wrote to BLNR to object
to the differencebetween the pricepaid to the State by A&B forthe water leases and
the pricepaid to A&B by the County forthe same water. At the time,EMI was paying
the State .0018 cents per thousand gallonsand sellingitto the County for6 cents per
thousand gallons.The Statewas collectingabout $160,000 a year on the four permits.
IfEMI/Mahi Pono were paying the same amount forthe four leases intoday’sdollars,
thatwould be over $700,000. Thus, they are paying 70 percent less for the leases
today than what A&B paid 45 years ago.
As I'msure you allknow, DLNR ishiringconsultantscurrentlyto assess the value of
the water,which isn'teasy because it'sa publictrustand not a commodity. Since the
State hasn'tadjusted the rates in halfa century,itis very likelythat even before
attempting to appraise the water's value, DLNR willat least triplewhat they charge
EMI/Mahi Pono to bring itin linewith today'sdollars.
I hope the Department of Water Supply is preparing accurate projectionson the
County's futurecosts when Mahi Pono passes on those increases to the County, and
when the Water Department must pass on the higher wholesale cost to farmers,
Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 209
ranchers, and other users. Those projections should assume that Mahi Pono will
consider what the market willbear. The County pays 28 cents to Maui Land and Pine
and 50 cents to Wailuku Water Company, currently,forevery thousand gallons.
So, back to our car. We're currentlypaying EMt/Mahi Pono to use a car thatthey are
rentingfrom the State. And we can'tcontrolwhen or how we use It,or itscondition
now or inthe future.Alternatively,we can directlyrentthe car at no costfrom the State.
And since we only need it17 percent of the time, we can Uber or Turo itforthe restof
the time and use thatmoney to put ina new engine and improve the gas mileage, make
itsafer and sometimes even use itto drive our grandma to the doctor.
Iemailed you folksa Powerpoint. I'llfinishup. Iemailed you a Powerpoint thismorning
that shows thataftergross income and expenses thatensure good care of the system,
we can have between $1.3 and $8 million in net revenues before we even access
Federal grants, or financing,or other investment. This seems likea more fiscally
responsible plan than the current one of taking on unpredictableextracost under Mahi
Pono's control. Mahalo.

we're not going to be here. You know, that,that'sway too longforus towaittogetour^

And also,the Supreme Court inthe Waiahole Case saidthatthe publictrustpurposes
have priorityover privatecommercial uses, and they do notenjoy the same protection
as the publictrust. Off-stream diverterswho seek to use water for theirprivate
commercial gain have the burden ofjustifyingproposed uses inlightofthe protected
publicrightsinthe resource. So, so these guys, yes they did the FEIS, but,Imean
nothingwas proven inthere. They have more resources thatthey could use, which
they're not utilizingat this point. They've got the groundwater that they can use.
They've got lands withinthe,between the lease area of Huelo and, and Kamole Weir,
which istheirproperty.They have not exercised theirrightsto use theirlands. Allthey
want is the free water. And so, I,I support the Resolution 119, and the 167 is,is

MR. DAVE DELEON (testifyingon Resolution No. 22-119 CD1 FDD:
Aloha. Thank you for this opportunity to speak in opposition to the proposed
amendment. My name isDave Deleon. I'mtestifyingas a Haiku residentand property
owner. The proposal that'sbefore the Council appears to be rushed in an effortto
forestallthe State issuance of 30-year water lease to A&B/Mahi Pono partnership.
As Councilmember Sugimura recentlypointed out ina, ina statement, ifthisproposal
passes, the publicwillbe asked to endorse a proposal forwhich there has been no
due diligence. This proposal would resultin a massive addition to the County's
responsibilitieswithout any grounding in whether the County is capable of the
proposed undertaking,or without any sense of what itwould cost the taxpayers and
the water consumers. Without that information, how are the voters supposed to make
an informed decision?
My familydepends on water from the EMI system, and especiallyintimes of drought.
Over the lastthree decades I've lived in Haiku, I'vewatched the localclimate grow
drier.Lastyear's,lastwinter'stwo-months drought was reallybad. For about 60 days,
no, there was no rain whatsoever. Itwas so bad the County had to truck water into
Nahiku. When Nahiku goes dry,you know something's up. What that is,isa change
in the wind pattern,itproduces less rainfall.There's another apparent impact, this is
another apparent impact of climate change.
Changing, changing who's in charge of the watershed willnot change that fact,but
might make itmore difficultforthe community toadapt to the changing situation.Here's
some other thoughts on the subject. No knowledgeable person I know of has
confidence thatthe County willbe able to meet the responsibilitiesthat thisproposal
lays out. The EMI system isboth massive and irreplaceable.Willthe County reallybe
able to acquire and properlymaintain it?
The privately-ownedEMI system has successfullybeen providing Upcountry Maui with
itsmain water source at a nominal price for the last60 years. Our community has
been able to depend on it.Why isthatno longer a reallygood deal? Maui isalready
an incrediblyexpensive place to live.Actions thatincrease the cost of water willhave
a serious impact on those who can afforditthe least.My bottom lineisthisproposal
ispremature at best. Inthistime of climate uncertainty,the lastthing we should do is
rush to make radicaldecisions to our water system that could negatively affectour
abilityto meet future needs. EMI must be maintained forthe good of the Upcountry
community. As the saying goes, ifit'snot broken, don'tfixit.Mahalo.

CHAIR LEE: So, discussion, Mr. Sinenci.
COUNCILMEMBER SINENCI: Thank you, Chair. Members, we discussed several options
for what to callthis new collectiveentity,includingthe "Maui Nui Community Water
Systems". Ifthe voters approved thisCharter amendment, the water authoritieswould
be established as County of Maui agencies under the administrativebranch. To reflect
their officialadministrative nature, I wanted to retain the term "Maui County" when
referencingit.But as we learned during the APT Committee meetings, the County of
Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 224
Maui has priorityover other non-municipal interestswhen applying for State water
licenses. So, using the Maui County name in theiragency titlewillhelp to instantly
demonstrate theirofficialMaui County status.
Ialso proposed using the term "Water Authorities"...develop the proposed, the water
authorityorganizationalstructurearound researching differentmodels of water system
management in the U.S. and around the world. Water authorities are the most
common model of water system management in the world setup as self-supporting
government agencies with community boards. Water authoritiesaround the world and
across America collectand distributewater in a transparent,community-oriented and
fiscallyresponsiblemanner. Ithinkit'simportantto reflectthe structureofthe proposed
entityas a water authorityin the name of the organization. This willassist with
qualifyingforgrant funding opportunitiesthatrequirecommunity involvement.
And using the name "Water Authority"willhelp to promote public education efforts.
The term "Water Authority"isa generallyrecognized terminology for a known model
of operation that our community members are already familiarwith. And to prevent
confusion and duplication,the term "water systems" isused throughout the resolution
to referto the water resource deliverysystems that are to be managed. This would
result in a water systems that manage water systems. So, using the term "water
authority"avoids having the same term referenced,but separate withinthe inter,and
interdependent of each other.
As forthe proposal to add specificstaffinSection 8-19.1,thisstems from community
feedback, the Members input,and stakeholders interestsinsuring staffingto assist
communities countywide with the establishment of water authorities.And to provide
immediate staffingfor the newly-created East Maui Community Water Authority,
including grant writers to obtain Federal, State, and private funding for feasibility
studies, business modeling for long-term capitalimprovement projects;community
liaisons for outreach, public engagement, and receiving stakeholder input. And
technicalanalyst to investigate,evaluate and negotiate potentialadditionalsources
once acquired, collect data required to meet State regulations and provide the
community with informationabout the status of water resources.
Chair,1believethese amendments do not significantlychange the main proposal we've
been supporting, but itdoes provide clarificationthat would be helpfulto the County
and the community as we continue with this Charter amendment process. So, i
respectfullyrequest the Members support of my motion. Thank you, Chair.

Yes, Mr. Clerk?

MS. SHEKINAH CANTERE (testifyingon Committee Report No. 22-61):
Aloha again. My name is Shekinah Cantere. My next testimony is to oppose
CR 22-61, the Hale Waipu'ilaniProject.Although Isupport affordablehousing being
builtforlocalresidents,Ioppose thisprojectmainly because thispropertyislocatedin
a FEMA flood zone.
Igrew up on the makai side of where thishousing proposal is. Ican personally share
with you that growing up on Ewa Place, which isrightby Maui Sunset, only about a
ten-minute walk from the Waipu'ilaniProject,my three olderbrothersand Iwould have
to barricadeour yard so thatthe insideof our hale wouldn't get flooded. Almost every
heavy rainfall,my neighbors,the Moody's, who liverightacross from us,do get flooded
inside theirhouse. As children,my brothers and Iwould kayak in our flooded street.
That's how deep the water gets.
This proposed spot is located in a wetland and is also a safety hazard due to the
surrounding roads having no curbs, sidewalk or shoulders. This projectwillcause
more congestion in this area and affectthe neighborhood’s way of lifeby adding
unnecessary stressof being overpopulated. There are also safetyconcerns forwhen
emergencies and evacuations happen. I'veheard other people talkabout a land swap
and Iapprove ofthe idea so thatwe can conserve thissiteas is.
I'dliketo encourage allof you here and listeningtoday, to make every decisionnot
only based on what isneeded now, but also what's going to be needed forour future
generations. Ireallyappreciate ailof your time. Mahalo nui.

MR. RANDY YAMANUHA [testifyingon BillNo. 21 CD1 FD1 (2022)1:
Good morning. Honorable Chair and fellow Councilmembers. My name is Randy
Yamanuha, a retiredprincipalof Maui High School and former president of both the
Maui interscholasticLeague and the Hawaii High School AthleticAssociation. I am
here today to testifyon Bill21.
Although the billhas itsmerits. I'm asking you to reconsider in not passing the billat
thistime untilsome unintended consequences and communitywide ramificationscan
be discussed with allaffected parties.As previous testimony mentioned, the obvious
financialimpacts in the loss of revenues from the decreasing gate receipts and
investments innew equipment inorder to comply with the billrequirements.
Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 242
Other consequences thatImay pointout isthe greaterriskforthe healthand safetyof
our student athletes. Moving athleticcompetition during the heat of the day places
students inan increased riskforheat-relatedinjuries.Other unintended consequences
by moving events to the daytime willgreatlyimpact our community's sports leagues as
itwilllessen availableplaying fieldswhile high school teams and community teams
compete forthe same fieldsduring a smaller time window.
Another issue should be considered moving events to the daytime and itsimpact inthe
league's abilityto secure officials.For example, in football,most of the officialshave
a regularday jobs and moving events early inthe day willnegativelyimpact the number
of officialsavailableforsportingevents. On a side note,we've had judges, prosecuting
attorneys as footballofficials,which they wouldn't be able to officiatedaytime events
due to work schedules and they'llbe sorely missed by the league and fans as they
bring some sensibility,they bring some sense of credibilityto the game. With
increasing number of schools in the MIL, scheduling games during the week is a
necessitydue to facilitiesand officials'availability.
However, today, the most important unintended consequences Iwant to speak about
coming as an educator to you ismoving games to the daytime willimpact our student's
education. Athleticpractices and games were moved to the end of the school day to
lessen the amount of students missing instructionalclass time. Having day games,
especiallyinthe MIL, which isa tri-isleleague, travelistime, isa big component for
teams. Students will miss more instructional class time because they willhave to leave
school earlierin order to have proper warm-up time as injuryprevention priorto the
sporting event commences.
Lastly,our students and community willbe affectedsociallyand emotionally Ifthere
are no evening activitiesfor them as we found out during the pandemic lockdown.
Have we thoroughlydiscussed the impact of our students and the greatercommunity?
In closing,please consider your decision. I ask you to consider allthe unintended
consequences of Bill21. Thank you for this opportunity to speak on behalf of the

MR. THOMAS CROLY (testifying on Committee Report Nos. 22-63 & 22-67):
Aloha, Council,
understand the difficultiestoday. You know, one reason Ithinkthatthatwe would like
to testifyright before you make decision-making is so that we reach all the
Counciimembers. And of course, we're holdingoffdecision-making rightnow because
we’re missing a Councilmember. So, Ijustsay thatforthose thatjustoccurs to me.
A confusing set of circumstances for these testimonies, but I
So, letme testifyon 22-63 first.That's the one where we're increasing the home
exemption from 200,000 to 300,000 and from 300,000 to 400,000 for a homeowner
who also has a long-term rental. I think that thiswas a missed opportunity for the
Council to do what Ihad asked thatyou do, which isdiscuss propertytaxes outside of
the budget.
The idea of changing the exemption, the homeowner exemption, it'sa great idea. And
Ithinkthat.I'mglad thatyou're doing this,and I'm not testifyingagainst it.But why are
we going to 300,000? Why don'twe go to 400,000? Would 350 be a betternumber?
Would 500,000 be a betternumber? None of those thingsreallygot considered and I
do want you to understand that when you give an exemption, that means we're not
going to tax you on thisamount. And then, everyone gets that exemption who falls
intothat,thatcategory. And based on the rate,thatexemption iskind of the same for
everybody. But what you'redoing here isyou're actuallygivinga bigger tax break for
homeowners who own very expensive properties, and a lower tax break for
homeowners who own lesser valued properties.
So, Iwould have likedto have seen itget questions and answered as faras what ifwe
gave a $400,000 exemption and we raisedthe ratefor,perhaps, from $2 per thousand
where itis now, to $3.50 per thousand where itwas when we lasthad a $300,000
exemption? What effectwould that have on our taxes? Well, Ican tellyou itwould
help people at the lower end much, much more, and itwould tax people at the higher
end to a greater amount.

July 15, 2022
Page 259
The currentscenario thatyou have rightnow, thatitseems likelyyou'llcontinue next

COUNCILMEMBER PALTIN: Thank you. Chair. Your Budget. Finance, and Economic
Development Committee met on June 29, 2022, to discuss Bill110 (2022). This bill
would amend Chapter 3.48, Maui County Code, to allow a real property tax of
50 percent of the minimum tax per year forseverely disabled veterans who are defined
as individualswho have been given a 70 percent or higher disabilityrating by the
United States Department of Veteran Affairs.
This billdecreases the required disabilityratingfrom 100 percent to 70 percent,
allowing more Maui County veterans to qualifyforthisexemption. During the meeting,
the Department of Finance said applicantsmust certifythat they own and occupy the
home to qualifyforthe exemption.
Your Committee voted 9-0 to recommend passage on firstreading of Bill110 (2022).
Irespectfullyask forthe Councilmembers support of the motion. Thank you. Chair.

Discussion, Member Molina.
COUNCILMEMBER MOLINA: Thank you. Madam Chair. Your GREAT Committee met on
June 28, 2022, to discuss Resolution 22-158, which would authorize proceedings in
eminent domain to acquire the property located at 5385 Lower HonoapiilaniRoad in
Lahaina, Maui, Hawaii. And the proposed resolutionwould statethatthisstructurebe
used fora publicpurpose forHawaiian culturaleducation or any otherpublicpurposes
forconsiderationby present Administrationor futureAdministration.
Sections 46-1.5(6),and 46-61, and 46-62, and Chapter 101 of Hawaii Revised
Statutes,authorizes counties to exercise power of condemnation by eminent domain
when itisinthe public'sbest interestto do so.
And since 2021, thispropertyhas been the focus of community criticismand concern
due to the size and height of the structure and the permittingprocess thatallowed
construction to continue.
Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 268
And your Committee also notes the Council enacted Ordinance 5233 in2021, relating
to hoteluse and development standards inthe NapiliBay Civic Improvement District,
in response to the community's concern about this problem. I'dliketo thank my
colleague from West Maui, Member Paltin,forthat.
And Ordinance 5233, which took effectAugust 9, 2021, limitsstructuresto a maximum
oftwo storiesand 30 feetinheight,includingallappurtenances such as elevatorshafts,
stairwells,vent pipes, and antennas, and prohibitedany hotel or apartment-hotel use
unless established by August 6,2021.
And sorry to be such iongwinded on this.Madam Chair. But your Committee also
noted the Council's approval of 22-158 would approve the Administration with the
authorityto pursue eminent domain by filinga lawsuitin Second CircuitCourt on the
County's behalfto purchase the propertyinfee simple foritsfairmarket value.
So, with that said, Madam Chair, your Committee, to conclude, your Committee
supported the need to obtain community input on the futureof the property to use itin
a manner that is respectfuland reasonable to the neighboring community, while
ensuring communication and transparency, ifacquired by the County. And for
example, your Committee noted establishing a public building in West Maui for
Hawaiian culturaleducation, among other publicpurposes forthis.And also supports,
which also supports the Maui County General Plan, which directs officialsto
"perpetuate the Hawaiian culture as a vitalforce in the livesof residents"and to
"establishcenters and programs of excellence forthe perpetrationof Hawaiian arts
and culture."

And discussion, Mr. Johnson.
Regular Meeting ofthe Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 285
COUNCILMEMBER JOHNSON; Thank you, Chair. Members, your Affordable Housing
Committee met on June 6 and 13, 2022, to consider the proposed Hale Waipu'ilani
Workforce Housing Projectby developer Van Bruce Investments.
The projectwillconsistof eighttwo-storybuildingswith two one-bedroom units,eight
two-bedroom units, and 18 three-bedroom units for sale to households earning
between 18 [sic]and 140 AMI.
Under Chapter 2.97, the Council must approve, approve with modifications, or
disapprove with the proposed projectwithin 60 days afterthe project'ssubmittal.
Because the 60-day deadline fall,fallson a weekend, the legal deadline under
Section 1.04.020, Maui County Code, isJuly 18, 2022.
Your Committee received comments on the projectthrough publictestimony and from
various County agencies, including Department of Housing, Human Concerns,
Planning, PublicWorks, and Water Supply.
Your Committee discussed concerns about floodingand drainage, trafficcongestion,
projectdensity,cost of the units,and possibledisturbance to wetlands and or cultural
assets.
Although thisProjectwould provide 28 residentialworkforce housing units.I'mhearing
loud and clearfrom the testifiersabout the ongoing concerns, particularlythe frequent
floodingand drainage issues,and the relatedhealth-safetyand monetary implications.
Ibelievedevelopers also heard thisconcern from the beginning and decided to ignore
the voice of the community and carry on regardless.
As Chair of the Affordable Housing Committee, I wholeheartedly support more
affordablehousing . . .and Ican say thatthisCouncil also does thatas well. 1fight
hard and strong for it,but as they say in the real estate world, "location,location,
location". I'dalso add to that,"price,price,price",which are the highest ends of the
workforce AMI.
Plus, the riskmay be too great on our workforce familiesand on the environment.
When your hairison fire,you don'tput itout with the wetlands.
Members, thisishard, but Ibelieve that the rightthing to do isdisapprove the project
and filethe CR. Iask forthe Members fullsupport of my motion.
Regular Meeting ofthe Councilofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 286

Member King.

restorationhas been expanding. We have more experts on wetlands, which isone of
the reasons why we've got the wetland preservation billinfrontof us. And Idon'tthink
there'sany doubt inanybody's mind thatthisisa wetland. Ialso don'tthinkthere'sany
doubt in anybody's mind that we support affordable housing as Mr. Johnson said.
Sometimes there'sinappropriatelocationsforcertainprojects,and thisisone ofthem.
Nothing personal, and we've allsaid that against the developer. But itdoesn't,it
doesn't follow that every single projectthat comes before us is going to be totally
appropriate, and this is one that just doesn't fallinto that category of appropriate
affordablehousing location,or even the design of being thathigh up ina floodzone.
So, I'velooked at.I'velooked at other projectswhere Idid see some NIMBYism and
I'vesupported the project. This is not one of them. You know, justbecause people
don'twant itintheirneighborhood doesn'tmean it'sa good idea,and thateverything's
automaticallyNIMBYism.
So, we have to look at these projects,we're tryingto builda community, not justfillin
housing wherever there'sentitledland. And thisis reallynot appropriate. So, I'm

MR. DELEON: Thank you forthisopportunityto testifyinoppositiontothe Resolution22-144,
to dissolve the Maui Planning Commission and replacing with six new planning
commissions based on community plan districts.I'm Dave Deleon and I'veserved on
the recent Maui Charter Commission. Charter Commission seriously reviewed a
similarproposal,and ultimatelyrejectedit.My personal oppositiontothisconcept was
based on the numerous testimony we received pointingout the inefficiencyof replacing
our existingplanning commission with sixnew planning commissions.
The Planning Department's opposition to thisproposal was quiteinformative.The
change would be quite expensive for staff.Itwould be, therewould not be enough
business to justifythe existence of most of these bodies. Itwould be difficultforthe
Regular Meeting of the Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 293
staffto staffthem with adequately trainedcommissioners. And because of competing
rules and overlapping jurisdictions,there was a real opportunity for regulatory
confusion and jurisdictionalchaos.
The Administrationhas been pointingout a recentmajor reduction incitizenswillingto
serve on boards and commissions. This proposal would resultin six new 7-member
commissions, as I understand it. And it would be, therefore require another
33 commissioners over the nine we already have.
The Charter Commission ultimatelyadopted a proposal,which thisCouncil modified
and endorsed, that addressed the needs to, the need to assure that every Maui
community is represented on the existing planning commission. The Charter
Commission accepted the Council'smodificationsand isnow set to be on the ballotin
November.
So, given the Council'sacceptance of the,of the existingballotproposal, itisbaffling
to findthisnew proposal before the Council. This proposal isinabsolute conflictwith
the existingaccepted ballotproposal. Ifboth were to be accepted by the voters,as the
Corporation Counsel has pointed out, result would be a legal,would be legal
challenges that could conceivably shut down the County's permit approving process,
as well as planning reviews, untilthe courts decides who's incharge.
And then there'sthe problem with the ballotquestion itself,which failsto declare that
this proposal willgive the Council broad new powers to micromanage these new
commissions. Under our Charter,the planning,planning isan administrativefunction.
Why isthe Council attemptingto give itselfthiskind of a new authority?And why isn't
thatnew authoritymentioned inthe ballotproposal? The November ballotalready has,
we already have a lotof expansion on the November ballot.We don'treallyneed this
one as well. So, Idon't reallyhear a good reason to do so. Mahalo.

RESOLUTION
22-144, CPI, FD1NO.
PROPOSING AN AMENDMENT TO CHAPTER 8
OF ARTICLE 8 OF THE REVISED CHARTER OF
THE COUNTY OF MAUI (1983),AS AMENDED,
TO ESTABLISH PLANNING COMMISSIONS FOR
ALL COMMUNITY PU\N AREAS ON MAUI AND
DISSOLVE THE MAUI PLANNING COMMISSION
CHAIR LEE: Member Molina,thisyours?
COUNCILMEMBER MOLINA: Thank, thank you. Madam Chair.
MOVE TO PASS RESOLUTION 22-144, CD1, FD1, ON
SECOND AND FINAL READING.
Regular Meeting of the Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 301
COUNCILMEMBER KING:
SECOND.
CHAIR LEE: Moved by Member Molina,seconded by Member King, to pass the Resolution
22-144 on second and finalreading.
Member Molina.
COUNCILMEMBER MOLINA: Thank you, Madam Chair. This resolutionwould place on the
next General Electionballotthe question of whether the Charter should be further
amended, effectiveJuly 1,2023, to establishplanning commissions forallcommunity
plan areas on Maui and dissolvethe Maui Planning Commission.
Your proposed Charteramendment would promote home ruleinland use and planning
by givingeach of Maui's six community plan areas itsown planning commission.
As noted at firstreading,thisproposal would amend the same Charter section as the
Charter Commission proposal, but that itis not uncommon for multiple proposals
amending the same Charter section be on the same ballot.
AllsuccessfulCharter amendments must be read inharmony with each other,as has
happened throughoutthe County's history,except when the Charter Commission and
the Council have competing alternativesunder Chapter 50, Hawaii Revised Statutes,
inwhich case onlythe proposal withthe largestamount, or largestnumber of votes is
given effect.The County Clerk isotherwise empowered and obligatedto give effectto
each proposed Charter amendment thatreceivesa majorityvote,
Legal action has not been required in the past to determine how to incorporate
approved Charter amendments intoan updated Maui County Charter aftera General
Electionand it'snot requiredhere. Iask forthe Members support ofthismotion. Thank
you.
CHAIR LEE: Further,any discussion?
Yes, Mr. Sinenci. You're muted.
COUNCILMEMBER SINENCI: Chair, mahalo.
CHAIR LEE: Oh, okay.
Regular Meeting of the Council of the County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 302
COUNCILMEMBER SINENCI: Mahalo to Member Molina for that explanation. Iwas, I did
have a question for the Clerk, ifthisCharter amendment willbe an alternativeto the
Charter amendment number 4? But itsounds likeit'lljust be who gets the highest
votes, which one gets the highest votes?
CHAIR LEE; Mr. Clerk.
DEPUTY COUNTY CLERK: Yes, Chair. That's our current understanding at the moment.
We wouldn'tinterpretitas, like,an alternative,likethe other alternativesthatthe body
proposed when you were consideringthe Charter Commission's proposals. But yes,
ifthere are competing, competing proposals on the ballot,our currentunderstanding
isthatthe largestvote-receiverwould be the one that would be incorporated intothe
Charter. Thank you.
COUNCILMEMBER SINENCI; Thank you. Thank you, Chair.
CHAIR LEE; Any more questionsor comments?
Yes, Corp. Counsel.
FIRST DEPUTY CORPORATION COUNSEL; Thank you verymuch. Chair Lee. We are not
certainthatthatwould be the outcome. There's no provision in how to handle a, two
Charter amendments thatare opposed to one another. We do believe that we would
need to seek court interventionto help us interpretwhich one is adopted and which
one isnot. It's,we don'tthinkthatitwould be simply the one that got the most votes,
although thatmay be what a courtwould decide.
