amendment. It'snot actuallyan alternativeto any of the Charter Commission
Regular Meeting of the Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 303
proposals, that'sa separate process that'sset-up. And you willsee on the General
Election ballot based on the process that's already concluded with the Charter
Commission, therewillbe some alternativeson specificissues,where eithera Charter
Commission proposal, or a County Council proposal willget approved based on the
most number of votes on that particularsubjectwhere there isan alternative.This is
a separate process under the Charter that, and that'sthe case for allthree of the
Charter amendments that have been considered today. And ifany of the Charter
amendments that the Council initiatesget a majorityvote, then they'llbe deemed
approved.
And Office of Council Services is confident we can work with the Officeof the County
Clerk and the Department of the Corporation Counsel to produce an updated Charter
based on any Charter amendments approved by the electorateon November 8,
without requiringjudicialaction. Thank you. Chair.

Maui Community Plan.

Committee. And itgives me a lotof hope thatit'snot justour community, but also West
Maui, East Maui and Paia-Haiku willhave similar reaction to a planning commission in
theirareas. People want to be involved inthe futuredestiny and they have not been.
So, I,I urge the Members fullsupport of this,to put iton the ballotand letthe people
decide ifthey want representation intheircommunities. And let'sstop withholding that
representation. Thank you. Chair.

DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC WORKS JORDAN MOLINA [testifying on Bill No. 21 CD1 EDI
(2022)1:
Aloha Council. Ismy audio okay?
CHAIR LEE: Yes.
DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC WORKS: Alright,thank you. Aloha, Maui County Council. I am
Jordan Molina. I'mthe Directorof PublicWorks testifyingon behalf of the Department
regarding Bill21. Firstoff,Iwould liketo say thatthisbilldoes provide benefitsto Maui
County. The conversion to LEDs willhelp us reduce our energy consumption and
reduce lightpollutioninMaui County, furtheringour reduction,our demands on MECO,
reducing our fossilfuel consumption, and giving us energy savings that we can go
towards other environmental benefits.
However, there are some inappropriatemeasures that only serve to unnecessarily
harm Maui County. Regulating blue lightcontent requirestremendous cost but offers
no verifiablebenefitprotectingseabirds. The Department urges the Council to delete
thisrequirement and referthe matter back to Committee for furtherdiscussion.
There isno evidence thatsuggests seabirdswillbenefitfrom limitinga certaincolorof
blue light,of light. The Department thoroughly went through this,the matter of
reviewing allevidence and studieswhen we got put through the lawsuit,as well as in
preparing our environmental assessment forour streetlightconversion project. None
of the evidence can firmlystatethata certainpercentage of a certaincolor of lightwill
reduce seabird fallout.
Of major concern isthe impact to our streetlightconversion project.This willbe a direct
loss of millionsof dollarsin both having to purchase new fixturesto be compliant, as
well as delaying the projectand the realizationof the energy savings that come with
converting to LED. Additionally,thisbill'sattack on the work of the public and the
community to develop responsible streetlightingstandards for Maui County.
Regular Meeting of the Council ofthe County of Maui
July 15, 2022
Page 316
As partof the conversion project,a community stakeholder group was assembled to
evaluate various lightingprojectsand various lightingcharacteristicsof concern to
determine which isthe best lightforMaui County. This group included representatives
from Save our Seabirds and the U,S. InstituteforAstronomy. Itwas determined that
inadditionto reducing lightpollution,improvingvisibilitywas justas important.
The lastconcern I'dliketo express is the cost for the Public Works Department to
become blue lightpolice. This willrequirefunding and trainingto get a handle on all
the criteriathatcomes with measuring and enforcingblue lightcontent and developing
a listof approved products for people to become compliant.
In conclusion, this billdoes provide good benefits,but itdoesn't have to do so
recklessly.The blue lightcontent isa reckless move. The arbitraryblue lightregulation
only serves to delay our streetlightconversion projectand attacks the ... is not
consistentwith the goals of our islandand community plans.
On behalf of the Department, I urge again, the Council to consider deletingthe blue
lightcontent as the regulationonly serves to add cost to our operation and provide no
verifiablebenefitto reducing seabird.

CHIEF OF POLICE JOHN PELLETIER [testifying on BillNo. 21 CD1 FD1 (2022)1:
Aloha. Can you guys hear me?
CHAIR LEE: Yes. Chief. Hi.
CHIEF OF POLICE: Hi. Forgive me, Iwas justbouncing in between meetings tryingto be
as effectiveas possible. I know that I'm on a clock for three minutes and so Ijust
wanted to clarify,when I spoke about a week and a half ago, I had asked for an
exemption, not an extension. And the way that itwas written,and Iwas asking forall
of publicsafety,not justfor MPD. And so, there'sa three-year,basicallya 1,095-day
extension. And so, I just was going to ask that public safety be removed from it
completely and that there isa true exemption and that we don'thave to worry about
this moving forward. Because we're talkingabout lifebeing affectedon this,and all
the reasons thatImentioned in public.And justas Councilmember Kama mentioned,
Ihave to,Ihave to support,you know, lifesafetyfirstand foremost.
And so, there'sa DOJ study thatwas done quite some time out of the COPS Office.
And what itbasicallysays isthatthere was methodology used, and they had programs
in the United States and United Kingdom, and they concluded that improved street

MR. KENT: Hi. Aloha, Chair Lee and Councilmembers. My name isJoe Kent, and I'mthe
Executive Vice-President of the Grassroot Institute of Hawaii. We're an economic
research organization,and a non-profittaxpayer watchdog thatpromotes the values of
individualliberty,economic freedom, and accountable government. I'm also a paid
lobbyistforour institution.
Bill107 seems to be a well-meaning attempt to lower housing prices,but respectfully,
our analysis of the billisthat itwould hurt the people it'ssupposed to help. The bill
would affectthe pricecalculationsforallhomes thatqualifyas affordablehousing and
through a complicated means, itwould effectivelylower the pricethat an affordable
home can sellforby around 20 to 25 percent.
Now, this would lead to developers taking losses. The County could cover those
losses, but thatwould mean taxpayers would be paying to refund developers forthose
costs. Inother words, Maui residentswho are strugglingto pay theirmortgages would
also be paying for the mortgages of theirneighbors. And ifit'sdone through bonds,
then the taxpayers would have to repay the bond plus interest.And interestratesare
high rightnow, so thismeans thatmoney would be going towards debt serviceinstead
of infrastructurelikeroads, bridges,and etc.
Another pointisthatany money from the County or from foundations does not have to
be granted to developers to cover theirlosses. Developers are not guaranteed that
money from the County. So, they would have to spend theirmoney and take a huge
riskof a loss on the projectwith the hope thatthe Council would look favorablyupon
them. And as we know, the Council doesn't always look favorablyupon allaffordable
housing projects.Many, yes, but not all.
So. insummary, there aren't,there'sno such thing as a free lunch. There's no way to
hide the losses,except by pushing itonto eitherdevelopers or taxpayers. Eitherway,
this bill increases the risk for home builders who may be taking significantlosses,
especiallywhen itcomes to 100 percent affordable housing projects. Thanks for
allowing me to testify.

However, you heard testimony from some of the lenders who said no, thisstilldoesn't
work. So, Ido think thisneeds to go back to Committee and needs to be where the
affordable prices and the calculation that's being used is consistent with the
calculationsbeing used by the lenders that say, yes, ifyou make thismuch money,
we'lllend you thismuch money inorder to buy the house.
But there'sone thingthat Ithinkthe County can do that would be significantin here.
And that isthe old rules said they were only considering principaland interest.This
one says we're considering principal,interest,taxes, and insurance, and other fees.
You know what the County can do to help affordablehousing rightoffthe bat issay
we're going to give a five-yearexemption from real property taxes for any of these
affordable houses. Then, you don't have to calculatethat in.
So, ratherthan tryingto come up with money thatyou would be givingdirectlyto the,
the purchaser, or money that you would be givingto the developer, the County just
doesn't have that component in there. So, please give some thought to waiving
property taxes for a period of fiveyears for any affordable house that'sbeing sold.
That's basicallygivingthe money directlyto the person and helpingfacilitatetheir,their
purchase of the home.
But I do believe thatthisshould go back to Committee rightnow, and should come
back with the agreement, ifyou will,of the lenderswho say yep thisworks, we would
be able to lend money at,to people in these income classes in these amounts. So,
again, great idea. I'lltake creditforhaving itseveralyears ago and presenting itto the
Council,but nobody acted on itthen. But thank you, Mr. Johnson, foractingon itnow.
And let'sjustget itrightand move itforward. Thank you.

requirement for board, commission, and committee members to at a minimum, be a

ORDINANCE NO.
BILL N0.2I, CDl, FDI (2022)
A BILL FOR AN ORDINANCE AMENDING CHAPTER 20.35, MAUI COUNTY
CODE, RELATING TO PROTECTING SEABIRDS FROM OUTDOOR LIGHTING
BE IT ORDAINED BY THE PEOPLE OF THE COUNTY OF MAUI:
SECTION 1. The purpose of thisOrdinance isto protectnative Hawaiian
seabirdsfrom becoming disorientedby artificiallightsduring theirmaiden flights
from their burrows out to sea. This is often referred to as “fallout” and leaves
the birdsat irskofpredationby cats,mongoose, and other predators, as well as
vulnerable to vehicle collisions and starvation.
NativeHawaiian seabirdsare an important part of Hawaiian history and
were traditionallyused by navigators to find their way back home to their
islands. They also help our fishermen locateschools of ahi and are critical to
our watersheds by helping to fertilizethe soilsand nearshore coral reef habitats
with marine nutrientsfound in theirdroppings.
The Councils intent is to protect native Hawaiian seabirds as much as
possible from interference of onshore sources of artificiallight.
SECTION 2. Section 20.35.060, Maui County Code, isamended to read
as follows:
“20.35.060 General Requirements. A.

ORDINANCE NO.	
BILL NO. 21.CD1. FD1 (2022^
A BILL FOR AN ORDINANCE AMENDING CHAPTER 20.35. MAUI COUNTY
CODE. RELATING TO PROTECTING SEABIRDS FROM OUTDOOR LIGHTING
This billproposes to amend Chapter 20.35,Maui County Code, to regulatealloutdoor
lightingfixtures,includingswimming and decorativepoollighting,tolimitshortwavelength
contentthatare down directed,fullyshielded,and mounted as low as physicallypossible
to limitlighttrespass and reflectionoff ground surfaces to protect native Hawaiian
seabirdsfrom becoming disorientedby artificiallight.
I.KATHY L. KAOHU, County Clerkof the County of Maui, State of Hawaii, DO
HEREBY CERTIFY thatthe foregoingBILL NO. 21, CD1, FD1 (2022) was passed on
FirstReading by the Council of the County of Maui, State of Hawaii, on the 1st day of
July,2022, by the followingvote:
AYES: Councilmembers GabrielJohnson, KellyT. King,Michael J. Molina,
Tamara A.M. Paltin,Shane M. Sinenci, Vice-Chair Keani N.W.
Rawlins-Fernandez, and Chair Alice L. Lee.
NOES: Councilmember Yuki Lei K. Sugimura.
EXCUSED: Councilmember Natalie A. Kama.
DATED atWailuku,Maul, Hawaii,this8thofJuly,2022.
KATHY L. KAOHU. COUNTY CLERK

Dear Chair Lee and Members of the Council:
SUBJECT: COUNCIL RESOLUTION 22-119, CD1, FD1 “PROPOSING AN AMENDMENT
TO THE REVISED CHARTER OF THE COUNTY OF MAUI (1983),AS AMENDED, TO
ESTABLISH A MAUI NUI COMMUNITY WATER SYSTEMS AND AN EAST MAUI
REGIONAL COMMUNITY BOARD”
For over 30 years offightingallthe powers thatbe - the state,county,laborunions,big
corporations,theirvast network of supporters,here we are today. When you thinkabout the
historyofthe East Maui waters,thisfightdates back to the 1800’s.
The dewateringofthe streams deprivedallthe people of East Maui and greatlyimpacted all
of our communities. Itwas so heartbreakingformany not to see water in the rivers. The
entireEast Maui was diverted- the largestdiversionsever. These are crown lands that
belong tothe people. We are protectingour constitutionalrightswhich isnative rightsinthe
publictrust.
We need to gain controlover our own water rights.Hawaii’s publictrustresources should be
controlledby Hawai’i.A foreignfor-profitentityshould not be managing or controllingour
water in East Maui. They willmanage itintheirown best interestand not the interestof the
county, nor the publictrust.They are responsibleto theirshareholders to make a profit.
DHHL supports the County receivingthe leases because a privateforeign entityhas no
obligationto uphold the publictrustdoctrineor the Hawaiian Homes Commission Act which
guarantee water to Hawaiian Homes. They have had troublegettingwater under the current
lease agreement.
P.O. Box 81642, Ha’iku, HI 96708 Namokuaupuniokoolauhui@gmail.com (808) 248-4401
There isplentyofwaterforeveryone ifthesystem isfixedand thewatershed ismanaged
properly.We want to be surethatwe focuson growingour water supply forfuture
generations.
We have the expertiseand in-depthknowledge thatcan properly manage the watershed and
efficientlyrestorethe eco system. We have the most hands-on experience and generational
knowledge of anyone involvedand needed to properlymanage the watersheds.
What we want iscommunity partnership,management, and stewardship. It’sbeen four years
since streams were restored in our moku and we have done everything withinour power and
means to be good stewards. This isnot a business propositionfor us, but one of sacred
kuleana!
We have no quarrelwith company operativeson the ground and our community currentlyhas
a good relationshipwith representativesfrom the company. Like Maui County we support
agricultureand want to see everyone succeed. We have never denied any farmer or
business access to water. Upgrading the system on publicand privateland benefits
everyone.
The time isnow to take controlof our water! Our community’s mission isto be good
stewards of the land and its resources. We want a seat at the tablealways when East Maui
water isconcerned or any issue concerning East Maui. We remain open toworking withall
stakeholders to move forward in a pono way.
Thank you forthe opportunityto testify.
Aloha
Amanda M. Martin

Aloha my name isKalae Martin,
Iam a kanaka maoli born on the islandof Maui. My parents were one of the firstresidentson E.
Waipuilani Road and have livedthere since 1972. Iam deeply concerned with The Hale Waipuilani
Housing development CR22-61 and the negativeimpact itwillcontribute.Ioppose thisprojectforthe
followingreasons:
1) Flooding:Ihave personallyexperienced nature'sfuryover the years from tsunamis,hurricanes,
brush firessome of which resulted in evacuation to safershelterand higherground. The most common
threatwe endure isflooding.There’s been numerous times these roads are closed due to
inaccessibilityand floodingintoneighboringhomes resultinginconsiderable damage.
2) Road Safety:Igrew up on Waipuilaniwhen therewas no access to LiloaStreet,itwas safe to play
ball,rideyour bikefreelyon the road.As timepassed the road opened, paved withspeed bumps and
became very unsafe due tothe factthatpeople speed on the road with disregardto the posted 20mph
signs.MPD isnotoriousforflyingdown the road athigh speeds. We keep our kidsinthe house or play
inthedrivewayunder adultsupervision.There’smany pedestrians,bicyclistsand now electricbikes
thatspeed up the roads.There are no sidewalkstowalk on.
S. KiheiRd. Another busy and dangerous road.Even though there’scrosswalks it’shard to see
pedestriansbecause of lackof lighting.
SeveraloccasionsWaipuilaniroad becomes gridiockedwith trafficdue to detours foraccidents and
road work. The projectwould cause more traffic.
3)Work Force Housing:Itwas suggestedinpastzoom meetings these housing would be idealfor
policeofficers,firefighters,nurses and possiblya working young adultALU livingtogether.For me who
has an employed young adultson.Ican tellyou he would not be interestedinthe priceand size or
probablyqualifyforthistype of housing.So ifwe’re not making it“reasonably affordable”forlocal
residentsthen who are we attracting?How do we get localfamiliesintoactual homes?
4) Kuleana; Inthe lastzoom meeting on thisprojectIfeltmy na’au (gut)lead me ina differentdirection
to address our kuleana as kanaka. Therefore,Isuggested the developers ho’olohe (listen)to the
kupuna who have personallyexperiencedlivingthereformany years.We each have the kuleana to be
good stewards of what God has entrusted in our care which ishis‘ainaand one another.This type of
project is not idealforthisresidentialarea and an a’ole for me.
Finally,I’minsupportof bill22119 as a Kalo farmer,it'simportantthatour community and our county
use our water resources, not a corporation.We’ve seen inthe past what corporationshave done. This
billshould be put on the ballotto vote.
Mahalo foryour time

current sales priceguideline.

plus contracts.

1

rate,which serves to lower sales prices and increase their out of pocket costs.

Jim Hayes testimony
From:

You don'toftenget emailfromjim@psi-hi.com.Learnwhv thisisimportant
Dear Clerk,
Iprovidedoraltestimonytoday justbeforethe lunch break. Councilmembers asked thatIprovidemy testimonyin ■
writing.They also asked thatIshare the DraftEnvironmentalAssessment (DEA) with them. Iunderstand that
DirectorMolina issending the DEA to the Councilmembers via email.
My testimony follows:
My name isJim Hayes. Iam theprincipalplanneratPlanningSolutions,Inc.inHonolulu.
I am testifyingon BillNo. 21,CDl, FDI (2022).Iam not a paid lobbyist.
I haveyearsofexperienceassessingimpactstothreatenedand endangered seabirdsin Hawai‘i. I led a team
thatrecentlycompleted a Draft EnvironmentalAssessment of the proposed Maui County Streetlight
Conversion Project.The Draft Environmental Assessment will appear in the July 23 editionof The
Environmental Notice.
The Maui CountyStreetlightConversionProjectinvolvesthereplacementoftheapproximately4,900County-
owned streetlightfixtures.Itwould involvethe removal of theexistingHigh PressureSodium (HPS) light
fixturesand the installationof fully-shielded,energy-savingLight Emitting Diode (LED) streetlight
fixtures.The LEDs would havea eorrelatedcolortemperature(CCT) of 2700K, they could be wirelessly
controlled,and theycomply withexistingadministrativerules.The projectincludesthe implementationof
adaptivelightingtoavoidand minimizeimpactstowildlife;thosemeasureswould includingdimming,when
and where appropriate.
In the Draft Environmental Assessment we consider an alternativethatwould utilizea lowerbluelightcontent
streetlight,suchastheone thatwould be requiredunder Bill21. That alternativewas eliminated,and Bill21
reeonsidered,forthefollowingreasons:
● A lowerbluelightcontentalternativewould notprovidebettershielding,which isthemost critical
elementof lightingtoavoidand minimize impactstoseabirds.
● The potentialwildlifeimpactsassociatedwith an alternativethatused a low blue lightcontent
fixturecountywide would likelybe similartotheimpactsassociatedwiththeproposedconversion
project.There aretwo main reasons forthis:
- First,thereisno evidencethatseabirdlightattractionwould be meaningfullyreducedusing
a low blue lightcontentfixture.Itisimportantto note thatthe U.S. Fish and Wildlife
Serviceand FlawaiT’sDepartment of Land and NaturalResource do not mention using a
low bluelightproducttoavoidand minimizeimpactsto Hawaiian seabirds.
Second, theproposed conversionproject’sadaptivelightingmeasures, includethe use of
low blue lightfixturesin certainshorelinesituations.This is important because itissea
turtles,not seabirds,thatare adverselyaffectedby blue light.
● That duringthe2016 demonstrationprojecton Maui Lani Parkway we learnedthatresidentsand
agency representativespreferstreetlightsthathave more than 15 percentblue light.The public
and safetyagencieswould finda low bluelightalternativeunappealing and potentiallydetrimental
totheirsafety,as testifierEricMiyasato demonstrated.
1
● Furtherconsideringsafety,theCounty of Maui PoliceDepartmenttoldusthatLED lightingis
recommended ina policytitled“Crime PreventionThrough EnvironmentalDesign.”LEDs are
recommended becausetheyallowcolorsand objectstobemorevisibleandappearsharper.These
benefitsareonlyrealizedwhen theLED producea broadspectrumoflight.A low bluelightLED
would notproducea broadspectrumoflightand,therefore,be inconsistentwiththedesiresofthe
Maui PoliceDepartmentand theCrime PreventionTliroughEnvironmentalDesignpolicies.
Have a good day,
Jim Hayes
PrincipalEnvironmental Planner
Planning Solutions, Inc.
711 KapiolaniBoulevard, Suite 950
Honolulu, Hawai'i 96813-5213

disciplines.
Our concerns includethe following:
● Funding - Willthere be enough funding to make the necessary changes? With thisbill
being all-encompassing,the concern iswhere the lightsassociated with high school youth
athleticevents fallregardingpriorityinthe budget. The risingcost of equipment and
shippingexpenses are being feltat alllevels.We are concerned that now thateverything is
more expensive, itwilltake longer to secure the funds withinthe exemption period and the
nightgame opportunitiesthatwe have been afforded by State and County venues forour
student-athleteswillbe taken away.
● Timelinessofchanging ofequipment - County and the DOE have differentprocedures and
protocols. Because of the constant use of the facilitiesby both the schools and the
community, the concern isthe length of time needed to make the changes. Shutting down
fieldsforextended periods of time to make these changes willimpact practicetimes and
availabilityto hold MIL and stateevents. Iam unclearhow itworks with the County
regardinghow they schedule major projects.With the DOE, projectshave already been
linedup, some formany years,which may put thisprojectoutside the exemption window.
● Maintenance, upkeep and updating equipment - We are concerned that there willbe
enough funding availableto do allofthese things.We are concerned that there are a very
limitednumber of companies thatare trainedto fixissues regarding these types of light
fixturesand there isproper equipment on the islandto do so. The Department of Education
has theirown procurement system and itdoes take time to secure funding and get
approved vendors.
● Projects already being planned and funded - The concern isthat other projectswillbe put
on the side inorder to satisfythisrequirement. We are inthe design phase to get new
locker rooms-dance-weight room facilities.Baldwin High School also justrecentlygot
funding forthe design phase of a girlslockerroom. 1 am personally concerned that a
projectto create lightingcompliance willnow put those criticalprojectswe have waited
years foron hold and, again, our student-athletesand allthose students that are required to
take PE in the school will continue to lose out.
As the MIL baseball coordinator,many schools relyon County facilitiesto support theirprograms.
Maehara stadium isconsidered one of the flagshipgrass stadiums in the state and iswhere 2 MIL teams
hold practicesand iswhere our MIL baseball pre-season, regular season and postseason games are
played. In addition,the HHSAA state tournament isheld here every 3 years which brings inteams from
Oahu and the Big Islandand istelevisedon OC16. The statetournament being on Maui allowsour Maui
teams to save money on travelas wellas allowsfamily,friendsand the community an opportunitytowatch
our student-athletesin-personcompete againstthe best in the state.
2P -e ^ I Jui-
Our baseballspecificconcerns are the following:
H r ● officialshortageandhavebeenforsometime.Mostofthese
3 officialshave primaryjobsand umpiringistheirsecond job. Having games atnightallows
these officialsto finish work and then make itto the fieldintime to umpire.
● Safety - We are concerned that changes made to the lightswillaffectthe abilityforplayers
to track down the baseball (flyballs,foulballs,infieldhits,etc.).Heat Illnessisalso a
concern ifday games are the only option.
● Access to the community - Most parents work during the day and most teams need to travel
to get to the stadium. Night games allow parents and supporters to attend afterwork and
allow players to play afterschool isdone. Not being able to have nightgames because
lightsare not availablewillput strainon the familieswanting to see theirkids play and a
strainon the players who want theirfriendsand familythere to support them.
● Scheduling - Division1 plays a 12 game schedule plus an MIL tournament which is
supported by the factthatwe can have nightgames so multipleteams can play at one
central venue at Maehara stadium. Ifwe were unable to have nightgames, we would need
to play at multiplefieldsand would be limitedby daylightwhich puts a strainon the officials
being limitedto only officiating1 game due to locationand we would need to startearlier.
To get our game season inand to ensure games could be played completely (beforewe run
out of daylight)would mean pullingkids out of school and we are concerned thatwould put
a strain on their academic success.
● Opportunities-With practicesand games confinedto daylighthours,thereisan inequity
created with opportunitiesforour student-athletesin“prime”event hours. Student-athletes
on Maui willlose opportunitiesthatotherstudent-athletesinthe statehave and thatwould
put them at a disadvantage forsuch things as scholarshipsand recruitment. Most state
tournaments have the higher ranked games at night(higherranked opening match-ups,
quarterfinals,semifinalsand championship games) and ifour kidsare not used to playing
under the lightsitisagain a disadvantage.
We are asking thatthe Billbe sent back to committee so thatthese concerns can be addressed
and thatthere isa solidplan that has been developed and in place to help ensure that our
student-athletes,student body, familiesand community supporters inthe MIL do not lose out again. We
are tryingto speak and representthe many student-athletesand stakeholderswho have gone through so
much with the shutdown of seasons and the restrictions that continue to be in place with high school
sports. We are concerned thatunintended issues created by thisbillmay/willbecome an additional
burden and affectour children and our community's social-emotionalrecovery invery negative ways at a
criticaltime where they are juststartingto see thingsget better.
Thank you foryour time and consideration.
Aloha:
Wade Hondo

system. (Duringthe day Waipuilanird has alreadyway to much trafficand walking /biking
pedestrianshave nowhere to go except the middle ofthe road.)(pets/childrenare high riskfor
accidents)

intersectionsare already maxed out and added 28 unitsand the people associated to that isway
too much....more people more cars more accidents)

are now. Kihei isalready a disasterwhen itcomes to excess rain as we found out on December
12th 2021...mud slides)

RE: Opposed with Recommendations Bill107, CDl m<3P
ao
x:
Aloha e Councilmembers,
Iam writingon behalf of localnonprofits,Hawaiian Community Assets and Hawaii Community Lending,
to oppose Bill107, CDl in itscurrent form and recommend amended language.
The updated sales price guidelines proposed in the billMUST be contingent upon the Council
committing the subsidy required for our local, low-income residents to become homebuvers and
affordable builders to recoup their construction costs.
Our organizationshave provided guidance to the County Councilon three separate occasions,explaining
this:first,inthe Maui County Comprehensive AffordableHousing Plan that you contracted us to
complete and which we submitted on June 30, 2021, second, during our presentation to the Affordable
Housing Committee on November 29, 2021, and third,in our writtentestimony for the June 30, 2022
AffordableHousing Committee hearinginwhich we asked the Council to amend Bill107.
Despite our efforts,councilmembers informed us that the County has the abilityto commit subsidiesto
our local,low-income residentsto meet the updated salespriceguidelinesproposed in Bill107. In our
team's review of the currentCounty code,we have concluded the County infactdoes NOT currently
have the abilityto provide the subsidiesto our localpeople to meet the update salespriceguidelines
as they are proposed.
Passage of Bill107 in itscurrent form could slow - or at worse, halt altogether - the development of
homes our low-income, localpeople desperately need and at the same time, reduce the few 100%
affordablebuilderswe are heavilyreliantupon.
The Councilcan stilltake actionto remedy the situationby amending the billas follows:
C. Salesprice- dwellingunits.The standard salespriceof a new dwelling unit [shall]must be
establishedby the department based on currentHUD [price]income guidelinesf.l.and must
include principal and interest, capped at 31 percent of the homeowner's gross monthly income.
The subsidized sale price of a new dwelling unit must be established by the department based
on current HUD income guidelines, and must include principal, interest, taxes, homeowner's
insurance, mortgage insurance (ifapplicable), and homeowner's association dues capped at 31
percent of the homeowner's gross monthly income. Subsidized sale price guidelines, or price
guidelines between the standard and subsidized sale price guidelines, proportionate to countv
subsidies, will be effective upon commitment by the council of subsidies for home purchase,
made available to local residents on the county's affordable housing listupon project approval.
Ifsubsidies are not committed bv the council, the new dwelling unit will be held to the standard
sale price guidelines.
/N^HawaiianCommunity
?;^iASSETS
HAWAII
COMMUNITY
" LENDING
o:
Th^roposedlanguageisconsistentwiththerecommendationsinthe Maui CountyComprehensive
Affordable Housing Plan.Itisbased offmath inour homeownership proforma,developed by first
collectingthe greatestamount of data possiblefrom localbuilderson theircoststo constructand then
ass^sedbysubjectmatterexpertsonourteampriortomakingtherecommendationintheplan.
Amending Bill107, CDl with the proposed language isa commonsense approach to affordable
housing development. Itwould ensure that both our local,low-income familiesare able to purchase
homes at pricesthey can afford and our affordable housing builderscan buildand recoup construction
costs.
1hope thiscouncilwillconsider thisamended language as a way to move forward on affordable housing
for our localpeople, builtby our localbuilders.
Feel free to contact me directlyat 808.587.7653 or ieff@hawaiiancommunitv.net should you have any
questions or need additionalinformation.

& 12The “affordable” condominium project proposed on Waipuilani Rd in Kihei
of Haste Makes Waste.
OFFICE OF THEYes, we need affordable homes to be available for purchase by our localr^Sgilgr^^y
Itisdebatable as to how affordablethisprojectreallyis.Most people seeking affordable
housing fallinto income levelsbelow what these condos willbe offeredat.Interestratesare
rising,making loans even harder to get. These unitscould easilyfallintomarket ratesales.
Affordable homes need to be good quality and must provide a healthy and sustainable
environment that supports good community development. There must be a long view in
the planning of affordable homes.
This projectisvery short sighted.
1. Itis located in an area that is known to flood regularly,sometimes catastrophically,
causing ruinto our near shore waters and damage to existing homes. These problems
willbe exacerbated by thisproject.
2. Itisinan area that has no sidewalks, curbs, guttersor drainage infrastructure.The
developer istryingto exempt themselves from supplying these basic needs.
3. The density of housing and parking on thissmall sitewillbe very uncomfortable for
unsuspecting new as well as existingresidents.Itwillbe a heat island.Good
community lifewillnot be promoted.
4. The sitedrainage proposals willnot work, and they would be very “unaffordable” to
try to maintain.
- Pervious concrete willnot remain pervious due to dust and mud infiltration.
- Grass pavers cannot be watered when cars are parked on them.
- Water storage tanks beneath the buildingsare not effectivein floods
Itwould be far less damaging to allow the existingR-3 zoning to prevail in this case. The
impact on flooding and congestion would likelybe much less. There would be fewer than
halfthe number of parking spaces required even ifthe sitewere able to be developed to
maximum capacity which itprobably would not be. Itis a misunderstanding that thisisa
neighborhood of offisland owners. The offisland owners are concentrated inthe Maui Sunset
Condominium complex nearby.Residentialhomes builton thissitewould probably be sold to
local families,not off islanders who tend to livein condominiums or resort communities.
Ideallythis site should be an example of wetland restoration where the most good can be
done to recreate balance in the flooding issues that plague this part of Kihei. The County
would be wise to consider purchasing this lotor trading the owners for a more
appropriate site where they can develop truly affordable housing that would not be
saddled with these issues.
Currentlywe have genuinelyaffordablerentalprojectscoming that can help our affordable
housing needs as we work towards high qualityenvironmentallyand sociallysustainable
affordable homes. Kal’ulu0 Halele’a,a 63 - 2 to 4 bedroom unit rentalproject,isunder
construction now for the 30%-60% income level. Ikenakea - 3 Leaf isin the process of
designing and permittinga 90 unit100% affordablerentalproject in “Downtown Kihei”.Itis
centrallylocated by shops, schools and willhave sidewalks and bike paths.These are 2
examples of good “affordablehousing".
Let’s not be hasty, thereby creating more problems for the future.We can think about the
true impact of our decisions and make the ones that we willappreciate in the long run.
Please say NO to this project in this location.
Please say YES to well sited,good quality affordable homes that offerwell being and
communities that can thrive.
Thank you very much for your consideration of this important matter.
SincerelySubmitted, Randy Wagner, AIA, LEED AP

TESTIMONY IN SUPPORT OF BILL 21, CD 1, FD1
A BILL FOR AN ORDINANCE AMENDING CHAPTER 20.35, MAUI COU;
RELATING TO PROTECTING SEABIRDS FROM OUTDOOR LIGHTING
(Second and FinalReading))
OFF! ,OF THE
''CLERK
Maui County Council
July 15,2022
9:00 a.m.
Good morning. Chair Lee, Vice-ChairRawlins-Fernandez, and members of the Council:
My name isDavid Lane Henkin, and Iam an attorneywith Earthjustice.’Earthjusticesubmits
thistestimonyon behalfoftheCenterforBiologicalDiversityand ConservationCouncil for
HawaiT instrongsupportofBill21,CD 1,FD 1,which would amend Maui County's lighting
ordinancetoincreaseprotectionsforHawaii's threatenedand endangered seabirds.Bill21 is
consistent with the best available science on how to minimize harm toimperiledseabirdsfrom
artificiallights.Bill21 alsoprovidesdear guidanceon how to implement thesenew lighting
requirementswith minimal burden on the community. Finally,the currentversionofBill21
addressesallreasonableconcernsraisedby members of thepublicamd the administration,
allowingample time forcomplianceand ensuringthatemergency serviceswillnot be
interrupted.For thesereasons,we urge theCouncilto adopt thismeasure as a win-win forthe
community and Hawaii's nativeseabirds.
ArtificiallightsinMaui County regularlyattractand disorientHawaiian petrels,band-rumped
storm petrels,and Newell'sshearwatersastheynavigatebetween the ocean and nesting
colonies,resultinginfallout,injury,and death.Stateand federalwildlifeagenciescharged with
helpingtopreventthesespeciesfrom goingextinctrecognizethatartificiallightattractionposes
an ongoing threattothespecies'survivaland recovery.Best practicestominimize light
attraction,and help preventextinction,includereducing shortwavelength "bluelight"in
outdoor lightingfixtures,preventingoutdoor lightingfrom shiningupward or over theocean,
and eliminatingreflectivesurfacesthatbounce lightintothesky.Bill21 would require
compliancewiththesebestpracticestogiveour nativeseabirdsthebestchance of survival.
To addressconcernsabout increasedregulatoryburdens forbusinessesand residents.Bill21,
CD 1,FD 1 providesa reasonablephase-inperiod—nearly threeand a halfyears—for the
replacementofexistingoutdoorlighting.SeeSection6 ofBill21 (ordinancedoes not takeeffect
untilJanuary1,2023);Proposed Section20.35.070.B(exempting existinglightingforthreeyears
aftereffectivedate).The billfurtherfacilitatescompliance with thenew lightingrequirements

percentageofshortwavelength light—failstoincorporatethelatestscienceon how bestto
minimize harm toimperiledseabirdsfrom artificiallighting.The CCT tellsyou onlyhow
yellow or blue the overallcoloroflightemittedfrom a lightbulb appears;ittellsyou absolutely
nothingaboutwhat percentageofthatlightismade up oftheshortwavelength lightthatis
harmful toimperiledseabirds.Lightingmanufacturerscan reducetheCCT oftheirlighting
simply by adding more long wavelength light,without reducing any of theshortwavelength
lightthatattractsnativeseabirds,leadingtofalloutand associatedinjuryand death.Limitations
on harmful lightingneed tobe definedinterms of the percentageofshortwavelength light(as
the Hawai'i County lightingordinance currentlydoes and asBill21 would), not interms of
degrees Kelvin.

Good morning. Chair Lee,Vice-ChairRawlins-Fernandez,and members of the Council:
My name isDavid Lane Henkin, and Iam an attorneywith Earthjustice.’Earthjustice
previouslysubmitted testimony on behalfof theCenter forBiologicalDiversityand
ConservationCouncilforHawai‘iinstrongsupportof Bill21,CD 1,FD 1,which would amend
Maui County'slightingordinancetoincreaseprotectionsfor Hawaii's threatened and
endangered seabirds.We providethissupplementaltestimonyto respond toclaimsby thebill's
opponents thatexpertwildlifeagenciesdo not supportreducing theblue-lightcontentof
outdoorlightingtoprotectimperiledseabirdsinMaui County. That issimply not the case.
Earlierthisyear,theDepartment ofLand and NaturalResources,DivisionofForestryand
Wildlife(DOFAW) providedcomments on theCounty ofMaui Department of PublicWorks'
proposaltoreplacetheCounty'sexistinghigh-pressuresodium streetlights(which are amber in
colorand have low blue-lightcontent)with brightwhite LED fixtures.DOF AW raisedconcerns
abouttheharm thatsuch lightinginflictson protectedseabirds,as well as on threatened and
endangered sea turtles.
To protectimperiledwildlife,DOF AW urged theDepartment of PublicWorks touse only LED
streetlights"designedor filteredso as tohave a correlatedcolortemperature of 2200 Kelvin or
less,or a bhielightcontentoflessthan2%." (Emphasisadded). DOF AW used the same
definitionof "bluelightcontent"as found inBill21 ("theratiooftheamount ofenergy emitted
by theoutdoor lightfixturebetween 400 and 500 nm, divided by the amount ofenergy between
400 and 700 nm") and noted that"LED lightswith thesespecificationshave been found tohave
fewer impactson protectedwildlife,includingseabirdsand turtles,and are consistent,for
example,with lightingordinanceson theislandofHawai'i." (Emphasis added). A copy of
DOFAW's letter is attached.
Thank you fortheopportunitytoprovidethissupplementaltestimony instrongsupport ofBill
21, CDl, FDl.
I am registeredas a lobbyist.

Dear Ms. Dagdag-Andaya,
Subject:Requestforconsultationforproposedstreetlightreplacementatvarious
locations;DPW ProjectNo. 22-44
The Departmentof Land and NaturalResources,Divisionof Forestryand Wildlife
(DOFA^ hasreceivedthesubjectrequestforconsultation.As GeneralElectrichasceased
theproductionof high-pressuresodium(HPS)lightfixtures,theDepartmentof Public
Works (DPW) proposestoreplacethoselightswhen theyarebrokenorburnt-outwithnew
light-emittingdiode(LED) fixtures.DPW seeksconcurrencethatthereplacementofthe
HPS lightswithLED lightsisexemptfromthepreparationofanenvironmentalassessment
(EA),pursuanttoChapter343,HawaiMRevisedStatutes(HRS).Pursuantto§11-200.1-15,
Hawai‘iAdministrativeRules,exemptionsfrom thepreparationof an EA areprovidedfor
certaincategoriesofprojectsthatwillindividuallyandcumulativelyprobablyhaveminimal
orno significanteffects.
DOFAW appreciatestheintenttoreplacebrokenstreetlightsincompliancewithlighting
standardsidentifiedin §201-8.5,HRS. However, we have documentedincidencesof
protectedseabirdsbeingdownedatLED streetlightsreportedtohavebeenratedat4,000
Kelvinorless.The DivisionofAquaticResourcesisadditionallyconcernedwiththeimpacts
oflightingon thenestingsuccessofbothESA endangeredhawksbillandthreatenedgreen
sea turtleson Maui. We thereforerecommend thatthose replacementLED lightsbeing
installedunder an exemption from preparationof EA be designedorfilteredsoastohavea
correlatedcolortemperatureof 2200 Kelvin or less,ora bluelightcontentoflessthan2%.
Blue lightcontentreferstheratiooftlieamount ofenergyemittedbytheoutdoorlightfixture
between 400 and 500 nm, dividedby theamount ofenergybetween400 and 700 nm. LED
lightswiththesespecificationshavebeenfoundtohavefewerimpactsonprotectedwildlife,
includingseabirdsand turtles,and areconsistent,forexample,withlightingordinanceson
theislandof Hawai‘i.
If you have any questions,pleasecontactPaul Radley,ProtectedSpeciesHabitat
Consen^ationPlanningCoordinatorat(808)295-1123orpaul.m.radley@hawaii.gov.

Aloha Council Members, OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERK
Iam writinginregardsto the proposed Hale WaipuilaniWorkforce Affordable
housing proposed for 16 East Waipuilani Rd. in Kihei.
I have lived at 1087 S Kihei Rd. since 1995. Our home isone of five,(approximate)
quarter acre lotsmaking up the Sunset EstatesSubdivisionwhich runs along
South KiheiRd. bordered on the South by Kauha'a St.and on the North by East
Waipuilani Rd. Three of these fivelotshave a main house and one cottage and the
remaining two have only one dwelling.The proposed development isslottedfor
the one-and-a-half-acrepropertylocatedat 16 E.WaipuilaniRd.,which isdirectly
behind thesefivelots,one ofwhich isoursand isproposed to add 28 unitson the
same amount of land as ourfive homes!!!
There isa plethoraof reasonswhy thisproposed development isa horrendous
plan for thislocation.A few of the reasons include that the development is
locatedina wetland area,a Tsunami evacuation zone, a flood zone, iszoned R-3,
which means thisarea islimitedto dwellingsnormallyassociated with residential
neighborhoods and would add to the alreadycongested automobile and
pedestrian trafficin thisarea. We have knocked on doors, collectingmore than
200 signaturesof our neighbors inthisarea and NO ONE we have spoken with
wants or can justifythisdevelopment proposed for our neighborhood.
Over the years we have endured flooding,due to lackof drainage and poor
development and car accidentsdue to hightrafficand no trafficlights.There have
even been pedestrian injuriesand fatalitiesdue to the fact that there are no
sidewalks,no bike paths or lightingon WaipuilaniRd. The intersectionof
Waipuilani Rd. and S KiheiRd isa major cross road which leads to a high traffic
neighborhood and publicpark.
This area forthe proposed 28 unitsisinfacta wet land area. When itrainsthis
area soaks up most of the water however during heavy rainswhat cannot be
absorbed intothiswet land spillsover and out intoour yards and streets.Ail four
of our properties,adjacentto thisproposed location,experience flooding during
1
Eheavy rainswhich has not been solvedto thisday. Itiscompletely irresponsibleto
develop thisparcelwithout solvingthe current issuesand infrastructure.
Pertainingto safety,there are NO sidewalks along S. KiheiRd.,E.Waipuilani or on
the North side of Kauha'a St.Along E.Waipuilani Rd. cars are parked on both
sides of the streetleavingroom for only a singlecar to drive down the street.
Driverand pedestrianvisibilityare extremely poor and to add 28 more families
and theirvehiclesto thisneighborhood on only an acre and a halfof land willonly
exasperate and endanger residents.During normal day to day livingthiswillbe
disastrous.Imagine what itwillbe likeinan emergency! (i.e.,fire.Tsunami
evacuation,major hurricaneetc.)
Jw'
We are requestingthisdevelopment gets a thumbs down and no exemptions be
allowed forthisproject.Ideallythisarea should be kept open space.
With proper planningthiscould be a win-win situationforthe developer and
established residents of Maui county.
Mahalo,
Mary Jo Padilla

FROM: Dept, of Water Supply,Deputy DirectorShayne R. Agawa
SUBJECT: Dept,of Water Supply WrittenTestimony on Reso 22-171,Reso 22-119, and County
Communication 22-167. Testimony givenverballyby DWS Deputy Director,Shayne
R. Agawa, attheJuly15,2022 CouncilMeeting.
DWS Testimony on Reso 22-171. Reso 22-119. & County Communication 22-167
RESO. 22-171
DWS stronglysupportsthisresolution,which urgesthemayor toauthorizea comprehensivefeasibility
studyfortheEMI watersystem inEastMaui. A feasibilitystudy willaddressmany of the concerns
thatDWS has,and inmy opinion,concernswhich allof you (councilmembers) and thegeneralpublic
shouldhave beforemaking a huge decisionintheformationof regionalcommunity boards and the
Maui Nui Community Water Systems. How can a decisionof thismagnitude be made withouthaving
accuratecostsof acquiring,operatingand maintainingtheseregionalwater systems and without
knowing an accuratenumber of theftiturerevenuegeneratedby theseregionalwater systems?
Example: When you purchasea new car,do you justsay‘T willbuy thatcartherebecause I love it.”
Or do most ofus ask what thepurchasepricewillbe,what thecostof maintenance willbe, does that
carhave everythingyou need as is,orwillyou need to purchaseaccessoriesor upgrades inthefuture
tomeet your needs? Another thingmost of us thinkabout once we know thetotalcostof purchasing
thisnew carisam Iabletomake themonthlypaymentswith the amount 1 make atmy job (your
revenue).Ibelieveallof us would not signon thedottedlinetopurchasethatcaruntilwe know the
answers tothosequestions.
DWS feelsthatthisresolutionisan importantfirststepinconsideringthe County’s bestinteraction
withtheEastMaui watersystem,whichever agency takesthe lead in the statewater leaseprocess. We
stillneed importantquestionsanswered beforewe make huge decisionson water management issue
and we believethisresolutionwillgetus thoseanswers.

RESO. 22-119 &CC 22-167
Overall DWS Comments:
DWS requeststhatmore claritybe givenwhen theterms""watercollectionand deliveiy
systems""arc used, thatthose terms arc not to be confusedwithwatercollectionand delivery
systemsunderthejurisdictionof DWS. We requestthatthe section8-11.2 be clarifiedto
acknowledgeourdepartment’songoingabilitytoacquireand manage water sources.
- DWS regularlydevelopsnew sourcesand alsoacceptssources such as privatewells or other
infrastructure,ifitisin the best interestof the departmentand our customers,and we want to
be suretheDWS continuestohave theabilitytodo so.
2"*^Whereas.ResoPg.1.
Any increasetothecostof water forlocalfarmersand ranchersjeopardizestheeffortsto
promote localfood securityforMaui
4'^Whereas.Reso Pg.1.
The creationof an additionallayerof bureaucracy reducesefficiencyand increasescosts.
5“^Whereas.Reso Pg.1
Wholesale regionalwater authoritysystems generallyown or operateallmajor watersupply
faeiliticsincludingreservoirs,treatmentplants,and transmissionlines.Relatedcostsof
servicesarebilledtomunicipalitiesthatown and operatetransmissionfacilitiesand linesin
serviceareas,issueend user billings,and provide serviceconnectionsto end usercustomers.
8“^Whereas.Reso Pg.2
The DWS isan existinggovernment agency with theabilitytoprovidecleanand safedrinking
water ataffordablerateswhile managing revenue,operatingexpenses,capitalimprovements,
and debt service.Maui Nui Community Water Systems would relyupon ratescharged to
DWS. The administrativeoverhead and operationalcostsof rumiingtheregionalwater
systems would be passed on toDWS inthe fonn of high wholesalewater rates;DWS
customerswould ultimatelyabsorb theserateincreases.
2 Whereas. Reso Pg. 3
DWS has a long-standingpracticeof supportingagriculture.Water costsforlocalfarmersand
rancherscontinueto be subsidizedby generaluse customers. The FY22 Water Rate Study,
preparedby Harris& Associates,indicatesthatagriculturalcustomerspay about 30% of the
totalcostsrequiredtoprovidethewater they use.
Maui Nui Water Systems’ ""additionalrevenue'"cannottranslatetoreduced agriculturalwater
ratesas system costswould be funded by increasedwholesaleratestoDWS. DWS would then
be requiredtopass on thisadditionalcoststoallcustomers.

OFFICE OF THE

Dear Councilmembers,
My name isSterlingHiga, and 1livein Paia with my wife and three
children.
Iserve as executive director of Housing Hawaii’s Future, a movement
led by young localswho willcreate opportunitiesfor Hawaii's Gen Z and
Millennialsby ending the workforce housing shortage. We focus on the
housing needs of the “gap” group that earn 80%-140% AMI, the localswho are
affected by thismeasure.
Iwrite with a simple request: PLEASE. SLOW. DOWN.
Please defer thisbill,and allow for a more robust debate that involves
allstakeholders,especiallythe students and young locals who willinheritthe
decision thatyou’re making.
The next generation of Maui - the people most impacted by this
measure - are busy working, startingtheircareers and providing fortheir
families.They don’t have time to track measures likethis or to show up for
hearings scheduled for9am on a Friday morning.
Low sales prices would be a great boon to young localswho are
starting their careers and families,but there has been no explanation of how
thismeasure willlead to lower prices.
In the absence of a robust subsidy program, Bill107 willlead to a
decrease in supply.This would have a catastrophicimpact on the young
people of Maui who are already strugglingto achieve home ownership.
For sale housing at 80% AMI isoften the firstrung on the ladder of
home ownership. Ifyou reduce the supply of housing at that level,you confine
people to be perpetual renters,and you increase the likelihoodthat localswill
leave for California,Texas, and Nevada, where housing is more affordable.
Please defer thisbilland reconsider itafteryou’ve made a plan to
engage the community. Iwould be happy to assistin planning effortsto
engage students and young locals,and you can reach me at
sterlinQ'fgjhawaiisfuture.orgifyou have any questions.
Thanks,
July 15,2022

Dear Council Chair Lee and Members:
SUBJECT: BILL 21, GDI (2022)RELATING TO OUTDOOR LIGHTING COMMITTEE
REPORT 22-47
The Department of PublicWorks opposes the proposed billinitscurrentform because ofthe
directand immediate impacts to the publicthat willresult.The Department requeststhe billbe
recommitted for further discussion.
In additionto the concerns raised by the Department of the CorporationCounsel in their
correspondencetotheMaui County CouncildatedJune 17,2022,theDepartmentisconcernedabout
thefollowingunintendedconsequences thatwillresultfromthepassage oftheproposedbill.
Housing projectsmust be stopped immediatelyupon enactment ofthisordinance
to verifycompliance oftheirlightingfixtures.
The bill’sthree-yearexemptiononlyappliesto“fixturesinstalledpriortotheenactmentof
the ordinance”.Any new housing projectthathas not yetinstalledtheiroutdoorlights
must be stoppedupon enactmentofthisordinanceuntilcompliancecanbe verified.New
permitsforhousingprojectscannotbe issueduntilcompliancecan be verified.
1.
Honorable Michael P. Victorino
For Transmittal to:
Honorable AliceL. Lee. Council Chair and
Members ofthe Maui County Council
June 29.2022
Page 2
Itwilltake time forthe Department to establishthe trainingand expertisenecessary to
determine whether a lightingfixtureiscompliant,particularlyas itrelatesto measuring
bluelightcontentaccordingto thebill'sdefinition.Unlessprojectsoptfortheremovalof
alloutdoorlightsassociatedwiththeirprojects,theprojectsmust be stoppedorbe subject
to enforcement action for non-compliance untilsuch time when the Department is
preparedto make compliance determinations.
The Department recommends the proposed billbe amended to exempt residential
propertiesfrom complying with the new lightingstandards,which Isconsistentwiththe
Hawaii County ordinance.
Current energy efficiencyprojects involving lightingmust be abandoned and the
associated material costs and energy savings will be immediately lost.
The County spent $3.8 millionto purchase 4,800 brand-new streetlightfixturesas partof
itsStreetlightConversion Project(the“Project”).None ofthese fixturescomply withthe
filteredor blue lightcontent requirements of the proposed bill.For the Department to
continue the project,another 4,800 streetlightfixturesmust be purchased at a cost that
willbe significantlyhigherthan the initial$3.8 millionalreadyspent.
2.
Additionally,the conversionfrom high-pressuresodium to lightemittingdiode (LED) light
fixtureisestimated to save the County $660,000 per year inelectricitycosts. Completion
ofthe Projecthas alreadybeen delayed by threeyears resultinginlostenergysavingsof
approximately$2 million.The Projectwillneed to be entirelyrestartedto ensure
compliancewiththe proposed bill.Thiswillcause an additionalthreeyears ofdelaysand
resultinthe loss of an additional$2 millioninelectricitysavings.
The Department recommends the proposed billbe amended to allowfora limited
exemption forstreetlightsto allowthe County’scurrentstreetlightconversionprojectto
proceed and requirethatsubsequent streetlightreplacementsto comply withthe new
lightingstandards.
Emergency operationsand CIP projectswillbe hindered.
Events and activitiesconducted at nightrequirethe use oftemporary lighting.Unlikethe
Hawaii County ordinance,the proposed billdoes not providean exemptionforsuch
temporarylightingand thereare no readilyavailablealternativesthatwould complywith
the proposedbill’srequirements.Any currentor proposednightactivitieswouldneed to
be suspended untilcompliancecan be determined.
Impacted activitiesincludeemergency operationsand capitalimprovementprojects.
Without temporary lighting,nightwork is no ionger a viabletoolto managingtraffic
impactsinsituationswhere concurrentroadwayprojectsand post-disastercleanupsare
occurringinthe same vicinity.
The Departmentrecommends the proposedbillbe amended toallowforan exemption
fortemporary lighting.
3.
Honorable Michael P. Victorino
For Transmittal to;
Honorable AliceL. Lee, Council Chair and
Members ofthe Maui County Council
June 29, 2022
Page 3
The requirement for wall surfaces to be “non-reflective”is unenforceableand
contraryto the purpose and intentof the County’s Energy Code.
The proposed billrequiresany wallsurfacehitby lighttobe “non-reflective”,butdoes not
definewhat thismeans. The billattempts to provideexamples of non-reflectivesurfaces,
such as the use ofmatte paint.However, by definition,matte paintdoes notcomply with
the requirementof being non-reflective.The Merriam-Webster dictionarydefines“non-
reflective”to mean not capable of reflectinglight.The leastreflectivepaintdeveloped to
date,named SingularityBlack,stillreflects1.5% of lightthathitsthe surface. Typical
matte blackpaintreflectsabout 20% oflightthathitsthe surface.No surfacecan be non-
reflectiveexcept inthe absence of light.
4.
Additionally,requiringbuildingsurfacestobe blackordarkcolorsunderminesthebenefits
that the County’s Energy Code seeks to achieve. A consequence of the reduced
reflectivityprovided by dark colorsisan increase in heat absorption. Buildingcooling
systems have to work harder and expend more energy to counteractthe additionalheat
loads absorbed by the dark-coloredsurfaces. The added heat thatdark surfaceswill
absorb reduces the energy efficiencyofthe building.
The Department recommends the proposed billbe amended to deletethe requirement
for non-reflective surfaces.
The requirement for “filtered**LEDs isunenforceable.
The proposed billrequiresoutdoor lightfixturesto be a “filteredLED". A lightfilterisan
accessorydeviceand isnot an integralpartofa lightfixture.The proposed billwould
requirelightfixturestobe filteredeven ifthe lightfixturealonecomplieswiththebluelight
contentrequirement.Residentsand businesswould notbe abletofindcompliantlighting
because filtersare not a commonly availableaccessory foroutdoor lighting.
The Department recommends the proposed billbe amended to deletethe requirement
for“filtered”lightfixtures.
No evidence supports less blue lightwillresultinlessseabirdfallout.
The Departmentrecognizesthatartificialhuman activitieshave the potentialto impact
naturalprocesses inthe environment. However, the Departmentisnot aware of any
evidence showing thatreducingthe blue lightcontentofoutdoorlightingresultsinany
measurable reduction to seabird fallout.Therefore, itisunclear whether the purported
benefitsoftheproposed billwilloutweighitsunintendedconsequences.
The StateDepartment of Land and NaturalResources,who istheState'sauthorityon
wildlifemanagement and seabirdfallout,maintainsa SeabirdFalloutSeason webpage
fhttps://dlnr.hawaii.qov/wi!diife/seabird-fallout-season/)that providesinformationabout
seabird-friendlylighting.The strategiespresentedon thewebpage forreducinglight
5.
6.
Honorable Michael P. Victorino
For Transmittal to:
Honorable AliceL. Lee. Council Chair and
Members ofthe Maui County Council
June 29. 2022
Page 4
attractionfocuseson controllingthe directionand intensityoflight.Nowhere on thepage
does itrecommend prohibitingblue light.
In May 2022, Hawaii PacificUniversityand the U.S. Fishand WildlifeServicepublished
a studyconductedon Oahu thatevaluatedtheimpactson wedge-tailedshearwaterfallout
caused by convertingstreetlightsfrom high-pressuresodium toLED fixtures,itwas found
that the conversion to LED fixtures had no observable impact on fledglingfallout.
The Department recommends the proposed billbe amended to deletethe requirement
relatingto blue lightcontent so thatthe County’s effortscan be betterdirectedat
implementingthe bill'smore meaningful mitigationsrelatingto controllingthe direction
and intensityofoutdoor lightinstallations.
Thank you foryour considerationof thismatter. You may contactme at 270-7845 withany
questions.
Sincerely,
JORDAN MOLINA
Director of Public Works

Good morning Honorable Chair Lee and Fellow Council Members:
My name isRandy Yamanuha, retiredprincipalof Maui High School and
former presidentof both the Maui InterscholasticLeague (MIL) and
Hawaii High School AthleticAssociation(HHSAA). 1am here today
testifyingon BillNo. 21. Although the billhas itsmerits,Iam asking you
to reconsiderin not passingthe billat thistime untilsome of the
unintended consequences and community wide ramificationscan be
discussedwith allaffectedparties.As previoustestimoniesmentioned
the obviousfinancialimpactsinlossof revenue from decreasing gate
receiptsand investmentinnew equipment inorderto comply with the
billrequirements.OtherconsequencesthatIwant topoint out isthe
greaterriskforthe healthand safetyof our student athletes. Moving
athleticcompetitionduringtheheatoftheday placesstudents at an
increasedriskforheat relatedinjuries.Other unintended
consequencesby moving eventstothe daytimewillgreatlyimpact our
community sportsleaguesas itwilllessenavailableplayingfieldsdue to
highschoolteams and community teams competingforthe same fields
during a smaller time window. Another issueshould be considered
moving eventsto the daytime and itsimpact on the league'sabilityto
secure officials.For example, infootballmost officialshave regularday
jobsand movingtheeventstoearlierintheday willnegativelyimpact
the number of officialavailablefor sporting events. On a side note,
we've had judges and prosecutingattorneysas footballofficials,which
they wouldn't be able to officiatedaytime events due to work
schedules and they would be sorely missed by the league and fans as
they bring a sense of creditabilityto the game. With the increasing
number of schools inthe MIL, scheduling games during the week isa
necessitydue to facilityand officials'availability.
The most important unintended consequence that Iwant to speak
about coming as an educator ismoving games to daytime willimpact
our student'seducation.Athleticpracticesand games were moved to
the end ofthe schoolday to lessenthe amount studentsmiss
instructionalclasstime. Having day games and especiallyfor the MIL
which isa tri-isleleague,traveltime isa bigcomponent for teams.
Students willmiss more instructionalclass time because they willhave
to leaveschoolearlierinorderto have properwarm up time as injury
prevention priorto the sporting event commences.
Lastly,forour studentsand community theywillbe affectedsocially
and emotionallyifthere are no evening activitiesfor them as we found
out duringthe pandemic lockdown. Have we thoroughlydiscussed the
impact on our students and greater community? There are probably
many more impacts for our students that haven't been mentioned.
Inclosing,please reconsideryour decisionsand Iask you to consider all
the unintended consequences of Bill21. Thank you forthe opportunity
to speak on behalf of our students.

Updated testimony:Hale Waipuilanisiteshould remainas,«,raSubject:
conservation".
Update 7/14/2022:
The Hale Waipuilaniprojectshould not be approved; We should not allow new housing to be builtinthe flood
zone. This area has multipleriskfactorsand there are many betterlocationsto buildaffordablehomes.
There are 2500 Affordablehomes inthe pipeline;Maui currentlyhas 36 Affordable Housing projectsin the
works and according to Mayor Victorino(editorialreAffordable Housing Maui News 4/30/22),there are 2,500
affordablehomes inthe pipeline.
Disapprovingwillhave a negligibleimpact on AffordableHousing; Disapproving this28-unitprojectwillhave a
negligibleimpact on Maui'saffordablehousing and represents only about 1 percent of affordablehousing in
the pipeline.
A landswap dealisthe bestoption;Maui County currentlydoesn'thave the 133 milliondollarbudget to fix
drainageInfrastructuretomake Kiheisafefrom flooding.But the County does have land.The County has land
availableatthe KiheiPoliceStation,atMaui Lani,and inthe centralvalleynear Pu’unene. Any ofthese areas
are farbettersuitedforhousing,and arefreefrom the flooding,and coastal hazards of tsunami, sea levelrise,
and climatechange.
The Developer has been offered a Land Swap; The Maui County has offeredthe developer the opportunityto
discussa landswap forcounty-owned land,ina more suitablelocation.Although the developer initiallyagreed
theyare now sayingitwould taketoo longforthatto happen.
The County needs to protectKihei;Kiheicannot accommodate more traffic,or more housing infloodzones,
so we need to reduce development on the floodplain,and encourage a retreatfrom the shoreline.Urban
growth areas and new housingshouldbe locatedon higherground, and farfrom known floodzones. Only the
County has the power tomake the decisionsnecessaryto make thishappen.
The best use of 16 E WaipuilaniRoad isConservation;
This siteshould be placed intoconservation.This isone ofthe area’slastremaining open spaces. And
itis part of the localwatershed and wetland nexus.
Keeping thislotas conservationhelps the County to meet itsgoals of Open Space preservation,from
the MIP, and KMCP Community Plan,as wellas achieve the goals of shoreline retreatas
recommended inthe CARE Committee and Multiplestudies.
Also, thisactionhelps us to depopulate and relocatethe Kiheifloodzone, as was recommended by
former planning directorDavid Goode.
Conserving thislotat 16 E WaipuilaniRoad willhelpto stabilizethe degraded watershed, and willhelp
preserve the site’sbeneficialfunction as the area’s de facto drainage basin.
This alsohelpsthe County towards itsgoal ofdesignatingand acquiring land for "green drainage
infrastructure”,as recommended inthe SMWP, and the KDMP.
Keep the propertyas is;Preservingthislotas is,has many benefitsand willnot overburden the local
community or itsaging infrastructure.The site’soriginalhistorichouse can remain, and continue to provide
1
housing.The open space and undeveloped land,can continueto provide beneficialservices.The site's
uncompacted Jaucus and Loamy sand soilscan continue to functionas a settlingarea forneighborhood
floodwaters,and the site'snativevegetationcan be encouraged to provideground cover and habitatfornative
species.Other possibleuses could includebecoming an urban naturepark or a community garden.
