ZKZ JUL 15 PH 12:31Testimony CR 22-61. July 15-2022:
Hi my name isDayid Dorn, Ihaye liyednext to thispropertyforCfclv^rjgJ^^fS.jifipgpose
theHaleWaipuilaniProject. COUNTY CLERK
We shouldn’t be building more houses in Kihei’s worst flood zone:
There are betterplaces to buildhousing away from flood plainsand coastal hazards.
Kihei has an aging infrastructure,bad traffic,and a lack of area drainage. The localarea
surrounding thisspecificprojecthas no drainage and isknown to flood.
The Kihei Drainage Master Plan states that:
“The intersectionof East Waipuilani Road and South Kihei Road is the low spot of
South Kihei Road. Water isunable to drainefficientlyduring heayy rainfallbecause the
localdrainage system isnot designed to cope withlarge runoff.Pumping is required
to take out the water. The segment of South Kihei Road from West Waipuilani Road to
West Lipoa Streethas almost a leyelslope and isflooded during heavy rainfall”.
(KDMP 66/388)
Decades of neglect and bad planning decisions has leftthis community highly
exposed to flooding with inadequate stormwater drainage, roads, and infrastructure.
Naturalwaterways haye been compromised, because buildinghas been allowed in
wetlands, streambeds, gulches, and floodzones. The mistakes of the past haye created
a legacyofpoorlyplanned neighborhoods,and dysfunctionalstormwater systems.
Residents of my neighborhood livewith aging infrastructure,and lack of amenities
such as sidewalks,and no stormwater drainage system. The authoritiessay that
drainagefixescould be 20 years away. We livewiththe constant threatof flooding,and
there isno reliefinsight.
Most of you counselors do not live in our neighborhood, so you may find it
difficultto relateto this,except KellyKing who livesinour Moku. When itfloods
here no one comes to help you. When the water comes in,sometimes in the middle
of the night,ittakes over. Itcan be swiftand silentand take you by surprise,and
sometimes people do not wake up untiltheirbeds are floatingon floodwater. You cannot
stop the floodwater because itfindsa way in,even coming up through the ground, or
coming up drain pipes and sewers.
You cannot call 911 because they are too busy to help,you cannot flagdown a
passing firetruck,because they willnot stop,and you are on your own. Nobody is
1
.:?1r\
comingefhelpSinlessyouhavefamilyorfriendsthatyou can call,and they willhave to
make itto you through the floods.
:-0t
People stranded intheircars wait hours to be rescued and other vehicles drive around
them not stopping to help.
Parents have no choice but to risk the floodwater to get back to their children at
home, or to tryto evacuate. IfYou are old or disabledyou can only wait and pray that
you willbe okay. Sometimes we also lose power fordays, and we have been stuck
insideour home unable to leave forgroceriesor supplies. Neighbors cannot help you
because they too are inthe same situation.So you fightany way you can to tryto save
what isimportant,the people, and perhaps the photos and memories.
Living atWaipuManI Ihave been a victim of flooding several times over, but we
have been lucky to have flood insurance that helped us, but we almost didn'thave even
that.Afterfloodingwe often livewith the floodingeffectsfordays and weeks afterward,
waiting for pump trucks,then the contaminated mud to dry enough foritto be scraped
away, 50 truckloadsat a time.Months afterwarddust,silt,mold, continue,and ittakes a
long time to rebound from the impacts.The ocean can stay brown formonths, and
some people are unable to move back intotheirhomes untilthey have had an
expensive restoration.
Flooding in this exact location is already bad, but itcould get much worse. My
neighbors and Iare extremely concerned thathis projectwillmake floodingworse. And
we have no confidence in the promises of the Developer. They have told us thatthey
are not responsible for regional flooding, and they are not able to reduce alltheir
stormwater production, and they toldus thatthey willnot be responsible for
flooding harm to neighbors afterthe units are sold. They toldus thatthe residents
and HOA are responsible for their own flood insurance, and the homeowners will
be responsible for maintaining any stormwater system they install.The developer
has toldyou thatthey intendto add almost one Acre of new roof and impervious
services, and thatthey willproduce an additional6000 gallons per hour of
stormwater. That also said they could not stop the stormwater coming offtheirproperty,
but they could make itcleaner.Betsilladmitted that “there is (ground)water allover
this site”,and Carnicelliacknowledged that the area has a flooding problem.
The Planned Drainage System willnot work.
Several people includinglocaldrainage experts,builders,watershed scientists,and
architectshave testifiedthatthe planned under-ground/under-buildingdrainage
retentionsystem willnot work. The proposed method of stormwater disposalrelieson
2
percolationof stormwater intogroundwater using narrow perforatedpipesin
underground storage ponds. This type of system isknown to be high maintenance and
quicklylosesitseffectivenessifnot properlymaintained.
Underground Storage System; This area site is within the FEMA AH flood zone with
anticipated1-3 feet surface ponding. So any stormwater detention“holdingtanks" would
need to be placed above the 3-footflood height.Any underground storage system
would not work during anticipatedstorms with 3-footdeep ponding and surface flooding.
Underground percolation will not work with rising water tables:
The proposed underground “perforated-piperetentionponds" would not work inthe
event of risinggroundwater. Prolonged rainevents such as Kona Storms can last
severaldays, and typicallysaturatethe ground and bring water tables to the surface.
So the proposed underground drainage solutionswould likelyfailat the worst possible
times during a Kona Storm floodingevent.
The worst storms are vet to come:
We have notseen a 100-year storm inour lifetimes.Allof Kihei'sworst storms in the
lastthreedecades or more have not reached the anticipated100-year storm threshold.
Even though many storms are extremelysevere and propertyisdamaged, the biggest
storms are yet to come.
The Developer has been offered a Land Swap: The Maui County has offeredthe
developer the opportunityto discuss a land swap forcounty-owned land, in a more
suitablelocation.Although the developer initiallyagreed, they are now saying itwould
take too long forthatto happen.
Carnicelli said the 16E was not a wetland according to ACE (Army Corps of
EngineersT
FYI, The ACE designation by itselfisnot enough to protect a wetland. Even ifthis
Sitewas designated as an ACE “JurisdictionalWetland", Betsillwould stilltryto develop
it.Just as they already did to several wetland lotson Halama Street.
ills up to the County to decide what itwants to protect.Sites such as 16 E
Waipuilnido not have to meet the specificACE wetland criteriato be important to the
watershed. The County needs to value the beneficialfunctionaland services that these
types of sitesprovide to the area’s naturaldrainage.
3
Local Flooding Issues:
● Mr. Carnicellisaid that“most testimonywas forregionalflooding,and not local
flooding”.This is not the case. There has been a lotof specifictestimony given
by the immediate neighborsatallofthe meetings,regarding the specificsof
floodingat thisexact site.
● You have heard testimonyfrom the adjoiningneighbors livingon at leastthree
sides ofthisproperty.You have heard first-handtestimonyof flooding,diagrams,
and photos.
● The County has also heard verbal testimony and received writtentestimony from
the immediate neighbors regarding floodingwitnessed on thissiteitself,
describingthe water flowingacross thissiteand onto South Kihei Road, affecting
downslope properties.And you have heard specificdetailsof stormwater water
coming under the fencing and rock wallson the makai side,and floodingat this
siteaffectinggroundwater.
● And you have heard testimony from people affectedimmediately downslope on
West Waipuilani Road, Ewa Place, and South Kihei Road.
● You have received a petition,regarding the specificissues at thissitefrom the
200 immediate neighbors livinginapprox 100 affectedhouses neighboring this
property.
Site-specificdrainage testimony:There has been a lotof testimony given about the
immediate drainage problems, lack of curbs and stormwater drains.There has been
testimony about drainage through soilson thissite,as wellas the conditionofthe
groundwater and high water tableatthisspecificsite.
There is no doubt thatthisDevelopment willworsen the floodingconditions:
The Hale Waipuilanidevelopment would add approximately One Acre more of
stormwater producing surfaces.The combined area of Roof, Driveways, and other
impervious surfaces thatwillproduce stormwater isabout an acre. This represents a
significantchange to existingdrainage and floodingconditions.The proposed drainage
calculationsestimate approximately 6,000 gallons/hrwillbe added to the local
stormwater loads.
There isa high probabilitythatthe downslope neighbors and propertieswillbe impacted
by drainage from thisproject.Drainage from thissitealready affectsthe downslope
neighbors, so any increase instormwater runofffrom present conditions could have a
devastating impact on neighboring properties.Even adding a few inches to the flood
height or adding those thousands gallonsof expected stormwater runoff,can have
harsh consequences forthe neighbors. These harmful effectsare farreaching as
4
hundreds of homes share the same low elevation and are situated in a natural
depression thatfillswith stormwater.
Managing Surface Flow: There has been a lack of information by the developer about
surfaceflowconditions.And they have no clearplan about how they would manage
surface flows going onto thissite,especiallyduring Kona Storms and 3-footdeep
ponding.
Existing Surface-Flow Conditions: Currently,storm water surface-flows intothisproperty
from WaipuilaniRoad as wellas from (and to)South Kihei Road. Surface stormwater
flows from the NE corner to the SW corner of the site. Water flows downslope diagonally
across thissite.Flowing downslope, the surface water passes under the chainlinkfence
or unfenced portion(inthe SW corner)and across the 2 neighboring properties.The
corner properties are 1103 S Kihei Road, and 1093 S Kihei Road. Aftercrossing these
properties,stormwater then fillsthe South Kihei Road intersection at Kauhaa Street.
Floodwaters accumulate in this area and backfill into neighboring propertiesin both
directionsincludingthe 5-lotsimmediately makai of the property.
The 5 adjoining lots immediately makai of 16 E Waipuilani Road are:
1103 S Kihei Road. 1093 S Kihei Road, 1087 S Kihei Road. 1077 S Kihei Road, 6 E
Waipuilani Road. Propertiesacross SKR are also affected.And along W. Waipuilani
Road and Ewa Place and UluniuRoad. And severalsmaller,private roads makai of the
property are also affected.
Neighboring propertiesfacing the north side of 16 E Waipuilani Road are.
1051 S KiheiRoad, 15 E WaipuilaniRoad, 9 Eleu Place, on the NW catty-corneris37
WaipuilaniRoad. Elevationsvary littlebetween the 16E Waipuilani Road property and
the roadway. Other neighboringpropertiesare on the same levelwhere they are
vulnerableto flooding.
On-Slab Houses: Houses inthisarea are generallybuilton-slab because of the high
water table,and basements and below ground constructionisnot possible.Most of the
neighboring houses are builton-slab,and have littleresistance forflooding.
Eleu PlaceAA/aipuilani Road Intersection:
The entireintersection(Eleu Place and E. WaipuilaniRd.) also floods,and water ponds
here to a depth of severalfeet.Most houses along Eleu also flood at the same time.
5
Drainage patterns and Surface flows.
● East Waipuilani Road slopes down from the eastern side at Liloa Street end and
isthe lowest atthe corner of WaipuilaniRoad and South Kihei Road.
● WaipuilaniRoad varieslittleinelevationnear thisend and ismostly flatbetween
Eleu and South Kihei Road.
● The entireportionof the road along the northernside of 16 E Waipuilaniisflat,
and iswhere water pools and accumulates. This northern side of the propertyis
levelwith or slightlylower than the roadway.
● When itrains,water flows down Waipuilani Road, or onto thisproperty,between
Eleu and SKR, where itfloodsthe roadway and flows onto the property and
adjoininghouses.
● Water flowing onto the northern side of the 16 E Waipuilani property surface
flows across from the Northeast corner towards the South West corner. Shallow
parts of thislotwillaccumulate water, where there are generallyponds to a depth
of a foot or so. And then excess stormwater will drain towards the SE corner
across the southernmost propertieson the makai side.
● On the Makai side of the property,only 3 out of 5 houses have rock or concrete
walls. And the last 2 houses have either a chain link fence or no fence. Actually
the 4th house has a chain linkfence,and the last(5th)house doesn'thave any
fence at all.
● The majorityofthe outgoing surfacewater flowsover the cornerproperty(1103
South Kihei Road) and flows onto South KiheiRoad.
● Stormwater coming onto South KiheiRoad from thispropertywillfloodthe
Kauhaa/SKR intersection.Stormwater ponds here and backfills,where itwill
migrate north and south along SKR and enter the frontside ofthe property’s
makai neighbors.
Neighbors adjoining the Eastern side of the property include:
38 E Waipuilani,36 E Waipuilani,30 E Waipuilani,26 E WaipuilaniRoad. These
propertiesare on the same elevationas 16 E Waipuilani,and are separated by a low
row of cinder blocks thatvaries from 6-20 inches high.This wall isinconsistentand
broken inmany places.Several propertieson the eastern side have stormwater drains
or french drains that either soak excess rain water intothe groundwater or directsurface
flow toward the subjectproperty.The driveway on the eastern sidefeaturesseveral
largegrated stormwater drainsthatreach about 5 feetindepth and are permanently
filledwith a footor two of water. Presumably thisdrain water comes from the water table
which is known to be close to the surface in this entire area.
6
The southern side of the property:
The Southern side isa 5-6 foot high retainingwall from the meadowlands projectand
Kauhaa Road. N.B. That southern retainingwall exhibitssigns of permanent water
seepage into16 E Waipuilani property side.
The Urbanized Watershed: In developed areas, the watershed becomes urbanized.
Water that flowed in streams flows into stormwater drains, and streets become
tributaries.Housing developments and parking areas become the catchment areas, and
low-lyingareas become drainage basins. This isdescribed inthe South Maui
Watershed Plan. Unfortunatelyinthe absence of proper urban drainage planning,
nature has adapted to the environment. But unfortunatelyforresidentsthispartof Kihei
islocated ina drainage basin with no ocean outlets.
Sources of Flooding: Floodingonto and around thissitecomes from a number of
differentsources.And willeventuallyend up inthisneighborhood. Localized flooding
from heavy Kona storms produces vast amounts of stormwater, hardened surface roads
and rooftopsincrease localstormwater productionof surface runofffar exceeds the
absorption.Storms at higherelevationscreateflashflooding,and swell stormwater
channels,gulliesand gulches.Stormwater and heavy rains can also increase
groundwater, and cause water tables to rise,This creates areas of ground floodingand
increased exfiltrationofwater coming out ofthe ground. Surface water and groundwater
flowsdownslope. Rain fallinginthe lower partofthe watershed, below PiilaniHighway,
willoftentravelalong streetsand through propertiesuntilithitsthe lowest portions of
the floodplain.This particularpartof Kiheihas no outletsto the ocean. So stormwater
buildsup, ponds, and backfillsintohouses and other areas.
Flooding from localstreams and gulches:
Local streams and gulches willalso flood,break theirbanks and flow laterallyaffectthis
area. Floodwaters from Kulanihakoi,WaipuilaniGulch, PiilaniHighway, and Keokea
flow downhilland joinatthe floodplain,inparticularat South Kihei Road. Any one or
severalsources can combine and travellaterallyalong South Kihei road. South Kihei
Road acts as a transverseauwai (waterchannel).
Shared Water Table: Allthe neighboringpropertiesshare a common water table,and
what affectsone impacts them all.Adding or disposingof thisdevelopment's stormwater
intothe groundwater could raisethe water table foralladjoining properties.This would
have the greatestimpact duringthe heaviestrainsand storms.
7
There are 2500 Affordable homes in the pipeline:
Maui currentlyhas 36 AffordableHousing projectsinthe works and accordingto Mayor
Victorino(editorialre AffordableHousing Maui News 4/30/22),there are 2,500
affordablehomes inthe pipeline.
Disapproving willhave a negligibleimpact on Affordable Housing:
Disapproving this28-unitprojectwillhave a negligibleimpact on Maui’s affordable
housing and represents only about 1 percent of affordablehousing inthe pipeline.
Betsill already has multiple projects in the works:
Betsillalready has multipleprojectsinthe works, includingthe 204 unitKuikahi project
in Wailuku. And Betsdilhas been offered a land swap from the County to relocate Hale
Waipuilaniprojectto higher ground. What they ultimatelydecide to do isup to them.
They say they are “entitled”to buildmarket-ratehomes under the currentzoning, but
they willalso have to prove proper drainage foran SMA also.
Betsill and Alaula Builders can be good neighbors:
HopefullyBetsillistrulya “good neighbor” as they claim,so surelythey should want the
safest housing forour futureworkforce.To thisend they should be amenable to
relocatingthe Hale Waipuilaniprojectto a bettersuitedarea,where thereare adequate
amenities, and no flood zones, or coastal hazards.
Land swap deal is the best option:
Maui County currentlydoesn'thave the 133 milliondollarbudget tofixdrainage
Infrastructureto make Kihei safe from flooding.But the County does have land.The
County has land availableatthe KiheiPoliceStation,at Maui Lani,and inthe central
valleynear Pu’unene. Any of these areas are farbettersuitedforhousing,and are free
from the flooding,and coastalhazards oftsunami,sea levelrise,and climatechange.
County needs to protect Kihei:
Kihei cannot accommodate more traffic,or more housing infloodzones, so we need to
reduce development on the floodplain,and encourage a retreatfrom the shoreline.
New urban areas and housing should be on higher ground, and farfrom known flood
zones. Only the County has the power to make the choices necessary to make this
happen.
8
Best use of 16 E Waipuilani is Conservation:
● This siteshould be placed intoconservation.This isone of the area’slast
remaining open spaces. And itispartofthe localwatershed and wetland nexus.
● Keeping thislotas conservationhelps the County to meet itsgoals ofOpen
Space preservation,from the MIP, and KMCP Community Plan,as wellas
achieve the goals of shorelineretreatas recommended inCARE Committee and
Multiplestudies.
● Also thisaction helps us to depopulate and relocatethe Kiheifloodzone, and
was recommended by David Goode.
● Conserving thislotat 16 E WaipuilaniRoad willhelp to stabilizethe degraded
watershed, and willhelp preserve the site’sbeneficialfunctionas the area's de
facto drainage basin.
● This also helps the County towards itsgoal of designatingand acquiring land for
“green drainage infrastructure”,as recommended inthe SMWP, and the KDMP.
Keep the property as is:
Preserving thislotas is,has many benefitsand willnot overburden the localcommunity
or itsaging infrastructure.The site’soriginalhistorichouse can remain, and continue to
provide housing. The open space and undeveloped land,can continue to provide its
beneficialservices.The uncompacted Jaucus sand soilscan continue to function as a
settlingarea forneighborhood floodwaters,and the site'snative vegetation can be
encouraged to provide ground cover and habitatfornative species. Other possible uses
could includebecoming an urban nature park or a community garden.
Regards,
David Dorn

Testimony cc 22-61:1oppose the Hale Waipuilaniproject,
To:
Cc:
Subject:
Dear Council Persons,
1 oppose the Hale Waipuilani project.
Various people have created a falsenarrativethatthe people livinginthe floodzone chose to livehere.
This narrativeispushed by buildersand lobbyists.This isjusta form ofvictimblaming thatsuitstheiragenda.
Do not listento lobbyistsand constructionindustryshillsthatare victim-blamingpeople livinginfloodzones.
They have totallyignored the vast amount of testimony regardingthe area problems,inparticularthe
dangerous lack of basic infrastructure.And the known hazards.
Kiheiisunderrepresented on the Planning Commission
Outsiders also do not know the problems of dealing withthe Maui County PlanningCommission, which rubber
stamps allconstructionprojects,regardlessofthe environmentalimpactsoffloodingconsiderations.We have
been to many Planning Commission meetings and had our testimonycompletelyignoredorwe have been told
that itisjustnot true.Outsiders also do not know thatour planningcommission does nothave a singlemember
that livesin Kihei.Outsiders are always tryingto tellKiheiresidentswhat isgood forthem, and they
consistentlyignorethe testimony of localresidents,and theirlivedexperiences,orthe localconditionsthatwe
experience here.
My neighbors and Idid not choose to livein a floodzone.
But we now realizethatwe do, and we also are trapped livinghere,because ofthe housingmarket.We didnot
move here because itwas a flood zone, we came here because itwas what we could afford.
Mortgage QualificationIssues; many people livinginthisareawould notqualifyfora mortgagetoday,some
are retiredor do not make enough fortoday'sprices.Many cannoteven qualifytorefinancetheirown homes.
When Ibought my home Iwas self-employedand was abletomake a “StatedIncome”loan,(akaa “No Docs
Loan") at 7.5% interest.Iwould never have got a mortgage otherwise.Nowadays thesetypesofloansare not
available,also Iwouldn't qualifyforanother mortgage today.So Icannotmove toa new home unlessIcan pay
cash for it.In short,1am stuck here, unless 1cash-out and move to a smaller,cheaper apartment.
When Ibought my home over 20 years ago Iwas never tolditwas a floodzone,
● 1have never lived in the area before,
● and as a first-timehomeowner Ididn'tknow what questions to ask.
● Ididn'tknow what a titlecompany was, or mortgages, or anythingrelatedtohome ownership.
● Ichose my home because itwas the cheapest one inKihei,and theonlyone thatIcouldafford.
Because itwas required by the mortgage company;
● 1 had a home inspection/valuation
● Ihad a termiteinspection
● Iwas requiredby the mortgage company to have floodinsurance
● I also needed home insurance,
● and Ihad to have titleprotectioninsurance.
1
What Was not declared or disclosed to me:
● Itwas never declared to me that this area had a bad floodingproblem,
● i was never declared that this location had no stormwater drainage,
● or that my home’s floorlevelwas below the area’s Base Flood elevation,
● Ithinkthatmaybe the FEMA Firm designation came later.
● 1 was never informed that I would not be able to buildohanas withoutraisingthe floorlevelofthe entire
house.
● Iwas never toldthat my house was inthe SMA, or subject to CZM.
● 1was never informed that my home was in the Tsunami Zone.
● Iwas never informed thatmy home was subjectto Sea Level Rise.
● Itisa new rulethat REA (Real Estate Agents) have to declare to homebuyers thathouses are inthe
SLRXA.
NB, Many of these items are stillnot requiredto be disclosedto homebuyers today.
The Flood Zone has been created around us.
According to long-timeresidentJimmy Gomes (73 years livinghere on WaipuilaniRoad),
This area always flooded,but itwas never thisbad. The water came down the mountain but was filteredby the
vegetation and would run clean to the ocean. But over time development has worsened the flooding.More
concrete and lessvegetationhave made the floodingworse year by year.
Inthe last20 years since Iowned my home, 1have experiencedworsening floodingand more frequent
flooding.
The floodinggot worse afterBetsillbuiltthe Meadowlands projectnextdoor in2017. There was a major flood
in Kiheiduring the constructionof Betsill’sprojectson WaipuilaniGulch Some say because duringconstruction
they blocked the stream bed there.And floodinggot worse afterBetsillbuiltintothe gulches at Kulanihakoi
gulch,Keokea Gulch, and otherwetlands.And floodinggets worse every time someone buildshousing
projectsuphill.
Because there isno proper County stormwater system, most projectshave been allowed to dump their
stormwater onto neighboringpropertiesina daisy-chaineffect.Subsequently, allstormwater accumulates in
the lowestelevationson the floodplain.This iswhat the Planning Commission has allowed and we are not
experiencingthe cumulativeeffectsofstormwater dumping.
The County and Planning Commission have allowed the floodingto get worse:
The County and Planning Commission have allowed the floodingtogetworse inmy area because theynever
requiredany ofthe developersto createa pepper drainagesystem, and many times deferred theirrequired
SMA infrastructure contributions.
The Maui County helped to createthe floodingproblems thatwe are experiencingtoday.By negligenceand
omission,the county has allowed a dangerous conditionto develop because they never added the appropriate
infrastructure,orfollowedthroughon theirdrainageplans orwatershed management plans.
Please listento the local residents who are affected by thefloodingproblems.And who standsto be impacted
by the increasingfloodingproblemsthatoverdevelopmentat thissitecould cause.
Regards,
David Dorn

GRASSROOT RECEIVED
INSTITUTE OF HAWAII im JUL 15 AH & 02
OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERK
July 15, 2022
9 a.m.
Council Chamber, Kalana O Maui Building
8th Floor,200 South High St.
Wailuku, Hawaii 96793
To: Council of the County of Maui
Alice Lee, Chair
Keani Rawlins-Fernandez, Vice-Chair
From: Grassroot Institute of Hawaii
Joe Kent, Executive Vice President
RE: Bill107 (’CPU to establisha new method of determining the sales priceof an affordable
dwelling unitto include the totalhousing costs associated with home ownership such as
principal,interest,taxes, homeowner's insurance, privatemortgage insurance, and
homeowner's association dues.
Comments Only
Aloha, Chair and Council members.
My name isJoe Kent and Iam the executive vice president of the Grassroot Instituteof Hawaii
a 501 (c)3 nonprofitpolicyresearch organization.
The instituteisa taxpayer watchdog group that promotes the values of individualliberty,
economic freedom and accountable government.
We have concerns that Bill107 (CD1), though well-intentioned,could cause homebuilders to
stop buildingaffordablehomes. This isbecause the billwould reduce the sale priceof a home
by approximately 20% to 22%, which would virtuallywipe out any profithomebuilders currently
enjoy and significantlyreduce the financialincentivefor homebuilders to investin projects.
Affordable housing projectsalready sellat below-market pricesand barelypencilout as itis.As
a result,homebuilders recoup by adding to the pricesof theirhigher-end homes.
Bill107 makes the below-market pricesforworkforce homes even lower by requiringtotal
housing costs to fitwithin31% of gross household income. “Totalhousing costs”under thisbill
includenot onlythe mortgage and interest,but also homeowner associationfees,propertytax,
privatemortgage insurance and home insurance. Those additionalcosts can vary,but on
,average, adding them to the totalpricethatcan be charged forworkforce housing would lower
the actualsales cost of the homes by 20 to 22%.
Reducing priceseven furtherby simple decree would erase what littleincentive homebuilders
have to buildaffordablehousing. The resultwillbe no new affordablehousing projectsat all.
which would hurt the very people thisproposal isintended to help.
Instead of tryingto lower pricesby mandate, Maui County Council members should consider
reformingthe county’sregulationsto allow more housing to be built,as outlinedinthe report
“How to buildaffordable,thrivingneighborhoods,” produced by the nationalState Policy
Network.''
Here isa summary of proposals from that reportmost applicable to Maui:
» Allow smaller housing. Let housing be as small as builders,buyers and renterswant.
» Allow smaller lots. Minimum lot sizes have no clear health or safetyjustification.
» Allow residences in commercial districts.Allow officeparks and malls to buildhousing.
Allow vacant downtown storefronts to convert to residential use.
» Allow tallerbuildings. Remove limitson height.Where firedepartment capabilitiesmight be
affected,ensure thatnew buildingscan providefortheirown fireresponse.
» Allow subdivision of existing structures. Historically,one of the most important sources of
low-cost housing has been single-familyhomes thatwere turned intoboarding homes or
subdivided intoapartments.
Allow single-room occupancy buildings. Housing with shared bathroom and/or kitchen
facilitiesbenefitslow-income residents,especiallynear dense employment districts.
»
Legalize the “Golden Girls.” Remove or loosen restrictionson the number of occupants or
nonrelated persons allowed ina housing unit,except for firecode restrictions.
»
» Waive some building code requirements for temporary homeless shelters. Imperfect
housing forunsheltered homeless populations isbetterthan the status quo.
» Allow extra kitchens. Permit homeowners to add additional kitchens to adapt to their
evolving needs.
» Allow accessory apartments. Accessory dwellingunitsadd more housing and often can
make itpossibleforthe homeowners to betteraffordtheirown housing costs.
' “How to build affordable, thrivingneighborhoods.” State PolicyNetwork, Local Government Working
Group, 2019.
» Allow larger “house” on the same land. Reduce setback, floorarea ratioand lot-coverage
rules.
» End single-family-only zoning. Many people cannot afforda large,detached house. Allow
duplexes, triplexesorfourplexes inplaces zoned foronly single-familyhouses.
» Enact a “Permit Freedom Act” for building permits. Require clearpermittingstandards
defined response time and the optionforindependent judicialreview.
» Publish pre-approved plans. Pre-approve plans forcommon projects likeADUs or
single-familyhomes, then permitexpedited review forprojectsusing these plans.
» Reduce politicalapprovals. Ifa projectcomplies with existingzoning, itshouldn’trequire
more than a planning department approval and buildingpermits to proceed.
» Set up one-stop, parallel-process permitting. Let applicantssubmit allpermitting
documents inone place.Allow departmental reviews to happen at the same time.
Outsource building permit application review. Outsource plumbing, electrical,mechanical
and structuralcode review when demand peaks.
»
We urge the Maui County Council to shelve any proposal thatwould stifleaffordablehousing in
the islands,such as thiscurrentprice-fixingproposal.
Instead,please consider the reform optionslistedabove, which would be more likelyto succeed
inincreasinghousing supply and easing housing pricesforMaui residentsseeking a home.
Sincerely,
Joe Kent
Executive vice president
Grassroot Institute of Hawaii

RECEIVEDTo: Maui County Council Affordable Housing Committee
Re: RESO 22-130, Testimony In Opposition Based on Personal Experience JUL I4 PH I? 50
From:HelenDaleAllen-Alioto ppFormer residentof the Waipuilani Beach Park area near the proposed
Honorable Maui Council Representatives:
PLEASE DO NOT PASS THE PROPOSED WAIPUILANI HALE DEVELOPMENT. IF BUILT.
THE DEVELOPMENT WILL CAUSE MORE HARM THAN GOOD. THE DEVELOPERS WILL
BENEFIT TO THE DETRIMENT OF THE ADJOINING NEIGHBORHOOD AND THE WHOLE
KIHEI COASTLINE AND OCEAN,AND, IS LIKELY TO NOT BENEFIT THOSE NEEDING
AFFORDABLE HOUSING.
Ioppose RESO 22-130 forthe followingreasons:
A.My experiences with floodinginthe inthe Waipuilanineighborhood
I.My property
From July 2002 through October 20 2017 Ilivedinon the end of Eva Place which isadjacentto
the property in question (Waipuilani Road ending at Waipuilani Beach Park which continues on
to Uluniu Road past the UFW land and then inback of MacDonalds near KeiheiRoad (‘“the
Property”).
Ihad an elevationstudy done when we bought the property.Our house was measured at about
7 feetabove sea leveland the lowest partofthe yard was at2 1/2feetabove sea level,The
water table in most of the yard was from 5 feetto maybe 11 or 2 inches above the water table.
Judging from the floods thatwe experienced, the Waipulani neighborhood stands inmost ofthe
area around 2 1/2 feet or so below sea level. With most floods includingthe most catastrophic
floods,most ofthe neighborhood became a lake.
2.Floods
During the time that Ilivedon Ewa Place, most every time thatthere was measurable rainfall,
the roads particularlyWaipuilani Road and Eva Place
became impassable except forfour wheel drivevehicles.There were severalcatastrophicfloods
inwhich not only the roads were impassable but many or most of the houses inthe
neighborhood were inundated with mud and the water pooled likea swimming pool across the
neighborhood especiallythe Property inquestion.
Two times Iwatched as water rushed in from the top of my property as ittraveledfrom uphill.
The water rushed in and receded slowly,especiallyaround the Property.
Major Flood 1
\stood on dry land at the highest pointof my property as the water rushed in. Within a few
minutes Iwas standing in at least2 feet of water as the water rushed in.
The water water rushed invery rapidlyand then the neighborhood became was one big lake.
The water stood forseveral days. The Property was a lake along with the adjacent propertynext
to mine. - W 1
Major Flood 2
There was a major storm that dropped several inches of rainover several hours. The roads
quicklybecame impassable. Rocks and large pieces of debris rushed around the yard. The
next day Ipaddled a surf ski from my house 100 feet to the street,Eva Place. The water was at
least3 feetinplaces on my driveway.The whole neighborhood again was a lake and the water
stood and slowly receded over a period of about 2 weeks. The Property was inthe worse area
forthe standing water and lasted over a long period of time.The receding water smelled of paint
or solvent as well as a sewer type smell.
Flood water
As can be imagined, the water from the flooding was extremely dirtyand unhealthy.Mosquitoes
became intolerable(and while cleaning up my yard Ideveloped a Staphylococcus infection
from scratching a bite).It’shard to believe that the Property could be developed with the
potentialforthese types of conditions.
Other floods
One nightIwoke up to see thatthe whole neighborhood was flooded and most neighbors had
mud in theirhouses and, no rain had fallen.Obviously the water had traveled down hilland
quicklyfilledup the neighborhood.
During and afterthe Japanese Tsunami the floodcame infrom the ocean side.My propertywas
likean islandbut uphillsuch as on Kehei Road infrom of Longs Drug Store,there were fishleft
on the road as the tides went in and out from the Waiahule and the Waipuilani creeks on both
sides.Again, the Property flooded.The strange currentsin the ocean continued for at leasttwo
or three months
Ocean Impact
Ispent hundreds of hours inthe ocean water offthe WaipuilaniBeach Park (and continueto do
so).Most days Iwalked along the shore inthe area.Aftereven minor flooding(measurable
rainfall),the ocean would become brown and fullof debris. Iwitnessed the ocean water
becoming more cloudy and, the degradation of the coral,not justoffof Waipuilani but also along
the whole Kihei coastal waters.
B.South Maui Flood
In 2017 we sold the property on Eva Place and moved to South Maui as the flood insurance
was going as high as $6,000 by the next year so. We liveon a gullywhich historicallystayed dry
except foronce every several years. On December 4, 2021, we experienced a flashflood. We
are stillcleaning up and repairingthe property.The flood water rushed likea riverfrom Kula,
through our neighborhood with an approximately 8 foot high wall of water, as witnessed by
neighbors, tunneled through our garage and out the back. Contributing to the flashflood was the
deep gullythatoverflowed and the streetwater ran as a river.Eventuallythe water ended up in
the Ocean. We, as many of our neighbors up and downhillfrom us are stillcleaning and
repairingfrom the flashflood sixmonths laterand most of us did not have flood insurance.
OPPOSITION TO MORE DEVELOPMENT ALONG AND NEAR THE KIHEI COSTAL AREAS
Ifthe developers are allowed to go through with plans to buildaffordablehousing on the
WaipuilaniProperty,not only willthe propertycause furtherfloodingto the Waipuilani
neighborhood but the tenants willhave to contend with the same type of floodingthathas been
experienced inthe neighborhood through the years. Like the neighboring resortssuch as Maui
Sunset, the sump pumps and other means drain the floodingintothe neighboring areas, and the
proposed development would have to includethistype of floodalleviation.Historically,according
to my neighbors on Eva Place, whose familieshave livedin the area for generations, the area
changed from an estuary type naturalcoastlineto what the Kihei coastlineistoday.
We must stop any more development along the Kiheicoastlineand uphil.We must findways for
the waters to spread out instead of making narrow rushing dangerous riversthattraveldown to
the ocean.
Affordablehousing inallof Hawaii isone ofthe top major issues to be dealtwith but developing
an acre and a halfof one of the one of the only remaining estuary area in Kiheiwillcause more
problems than itwillsolve.This development willnot solvethe affordablehousing issue.The
proposed development willonly make existingissues worse and the property isnot a practical
area in which humans can live.
Thank you foryour consideration
H. Dale Allen-Alioto,retiredHI DOE teacher and Sierra Club member or 35 years, and property
owner
P. S. (Also,isthe development under the guise of “affordablehousing” willbe profitableto the
developers without trulyhelping those who need affordablehousing and what isgoing to stop
the development in question from raisingthe rentfrom whatever isconsidered affordablehigher
and priceout the people who need affordablehousing? Who isreallygoing to profitifthis
development goes through?)
Count^^lerk

ChairLee and FellowMaui County CouncilMembers:
My name isKeithAmemiya, former Hawaii High School AthleticAssociationExecutiveDirector,and I'mtestifyingin
opposition to BillNo. 21.
While protectingNative Hawaiian seabirdsiscertainlyimportant, there'ssimply not enough information and basisto
substantiatethe bill'spassage atthistime.
Indeed, I'veattended many Maui InterscholasticLeague outdoor nighttime athleticevents and haven't been made
aware of any danger to seabirdscaused by the lighting.
Additionally,longtime MIL administrators,coaches,former players,parents,and fans are similarlyunaware of such
dangers.
The downside of banning nighttimegames with outdoor lightingare simply too great to allow thisbillto pass.
Further,based upon past historyand the many competing State and County needs, even ifthe MIL isprovided time
to change the lightingat the numerous athleticfacilitiesin question, the likelihoodof the necessary funding being
provided and installationbeing made ina timelymanner isuncertainat best.
In sum, BillNo. 21 may have the best of intentionsforseabirds,but our youth willsuffergreatlyIfit'spassed.
Therefore,Irespectfullyrequestthatthe Councilvote againstBillNo. 21's passage.
Mahalo foryour time and consideration.

Aloha Chair Lee and Members,
Iam a licensedrelatorand do not supportBill107,CDl (2022),initscurrentform. In tryingtobuild
homes on Maui forprimary or rentals,itisso difficultto getanythingdone. From permittingdelays,to
increasesinmaterialand laborcostand regulatoryred-tape,ibsnot easy tobuildhomes on Maui, much less
workforcehomes. Beinginrealestate,Iknow thattherearenot a lotofhomes availablebelow $1M, and by
loweringtheaffordablehousingsalespricesasproposedwillfurtlierreduce thenumber ofhomes and widen
the gap betH^eenaffordableand market homes. Pleaseleavethesalespricesin theircurrentform. Insteadof
loweringtheaffordablehousingsalespricespleaseconsidersubsidizingtheindividualbuyers who need the
help or have theCounty pay forinfrastructureand initiatezoning changes.Provide incentives,not
disincentives.
From firsthand experience,Iam tryingtodo therightthingby buildingmore ADU and rentalhomes on our
properties,and yetIfinditso difficulttonavigatethevariousrulesthatarecounterproductivetothe
process.
The gap fortheabilityofour childrentobe independantand own theirown homes grows wider and wider

U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service Recommended Avoidance and Minimization
Measures forLightingEffectstoFederallyListedSpecies
Subject:
Dear Keola Whittaker:
Thank you forreachingouttous and requestinginformationon how Maui County can avoidand
minimize adverseeffectsto federallylistedHawaiian seabirdsand seaturtlesfrom artificial
lighting.We appreciateyour coordinationwith our officeand acknowledge thework you have
done tomodify County lightingthrougha recentlyupdatedMaui County Ordinancetoavoid
impacts to federallylistedHawaiian seabirdsand seaturtlesfrom lighting.
We offerthefollowingrecommended measures to avoidand minimize takeof Hawaiian seabirds
and sea turtles.Our comments and thisletterare prepared under theauthorityof,and in
accordance with,provisionsof the Endangered SpeciesAct of 1973 (16 U.S.C. 1531 etseq.)as
amended (ESA).
Avoidance and Minimization Measures for the Endangered Hawaiian petrel{Pterodroma
sandwichemis)^ Threatened Newell’s shearwater {Piiffiniisaiiricularisnewelli)^and
Endangered Hawai‘i DistinctPopulation Segment of the band-rumped storm-petrel
{Oceanodroma castro)(Hawaiian seabirds):
Hawaiian seabirdsmay traversethe projectarea atnightduringthebreeding,nestingand
fledgingseasons(March 1 to December 15).Outdoor lightingcould resultinseabird
disorientation,fallout,and injmy or mortality.Seabirdsareattractedtolightsand aftercircling
the lightstheymay become exhaustedand collidewithnearbywires,buildings,or other
structuresor they may land on the ground.Downed seabirdsaresubjecttoincreasedmortality
PACIFIC REGION 1
Idaho, Oregon*, Washington,
American Samoa. Guam. Hawapi. northern Mariana islands
●PARTIAL
fCeola Whittaker 2r 1
dueJocollisionvvit^hautomobiles,stai-vation,andpredationby dogs,cats,andotherpredators.
Pledging seabirdstraversingtheprojectareabetween September 15 and December 15,in their
firstflightsfrom theirmountain neststo the sea,are particularlyvulnerableto lightattraction.
To avoid,and minimize adverse effectsand take of Hawaiian seabirdswe recommend
implementing the followinggeneralmeasures:
● Fullyshieldalloutdoor lightsso the bulb can only be seen from below.
● Install automatic motion sensor switches and controls on alloutdoor lightsor turnoff
lightswhen human activityisnot occurringinthe lightedarea.
● Avoid nighttimelighting,includingnighttimeconstniction,duringtheseabirdfledging
season,September 15 tlu'oughDecember 15.
it
There isresearchcurrentlybeing conducted thatisevaluatingthephysiologicalresponseof
Hawaiian seabirdstodifferentlightingcharacteristics.We expect thattliisresearchwilloffer
new insightsintohow Hawaiian seabirdsdiscernlightand could be used inlighting
modificationsto avoid causing seabirdfalloutin the future.Untilthe resultsof thisresearchare
available,our recommendations focus on effortsto reduce overalllightsand shieldalloutdoor
lights
Threatened (Central North PacificDPS) Green sea turtles{Chelonia mydas) and
Endangered Hawksbill sea turtle{Eretmochelys imbricata)(collectivelyreferred to as sea
turtles):Green sea turtlesmay neston any sandy beach area in the PacificIslands.Hawksbill
sea turtlesexhibita wide tolerancefornestingsubstrate(rangingfrom sandy beach to crushed
coral)with neststypicallyplacedunder vegetation.Both speciesexhibitstrongnestingsite
fidelity.Nestingoccurson beachesfrom May throughSeptember,peaking inJune and July,with
hatchlingsemerging throughNovember and December. We expectthatallsandy beaches may
potentiallyprovidenestingand/orbaskinghabitatforgreenseaturtlesand thatunlessspecific
modificationshave occurredtoalterlighting,we assume thatalllightingthatisvisiblefrom a
beach environmenthas thepotentialtoaffectseaturtlenestingand hatchlingemergence.
Optimal sea tuitlcnestinghabitatisa dark beach freeof barriersthatrestrictsea turtle
movement. Nesting turtlesmay be deterredfrom approachingor layingsuccessfulnestson
lightedor disturbedbeaches.They may become disorientedby artificiallighting,leadingto
exhaustionand placement of a nestinan inappropriatelocation(suchasator below thehightide
line).Hatchlingsthatemerge from nestsmay alsobe disorientedby artificiallighting.Inland
areasvisiblefrom thebeach shouldbe sufficientlydark to allow forsuccessfulnavigationby
hatchlingstothe ocean.
To avoid and minimize projectimpacts to sea turtlesfrom lightingwe recommend incorporating
the followingapplicablemeasures intoyour projectdescription:
● Avoid nighttimelighting,includingnighttimeconstructionwork, during the nestingand
hatchingseason(May to December).
● Minimize theuse of lightingon or nearbeaches and shieldallproject-relatedlightsso the
lightisnot visiblefrom any beach,
o Iflightscan’tbe fullyshieldedor ifheadlightsmust be used,fullyenclosethe
lightsourcewith lightfilteringtapeor filters.
Keola Whittaker 3
● Incorporatedesignmeasures intotheconstructionor operationof buildingsadjacentto
the beach to reduce ambient outdoor lightingsuch as;
o tintingor usingautomaticwindow shades forexteriorwindows thatfacethe
beach;
o reducingtheheightof exteriorlightingtobelow 3 feetand pointeddownward or
away from the beach; and
o minimize lightintensityto thelowestlevelfeasibleand,when possible,include
timers and motion sensors.
Conclusion
We look forward to continued coordinationwith you in advance of and throughout theupcoming
seabirdfledgingand sea turtlenestingseasons.Our hope isthatimplementationof these
measures willeffectivelyavoid take of listedHawaiian seabirdsand seaturtles.We requestthat
you continueto keep us apprisedas avoidanceand minimizationmeasuresareimplemented.
While we anticipatethe identifiedavoidanceand minimizationmeasures willreducethe
likelihoodof lightingimpactsto listedseabirdsand seaturtles,whethertakecan be fullyavoided
can only be determinedthrough robustmonitoring,reporting,and adaptivemanagement. Iftake
cannot be fullyavoided,Maui County should develop a HabitatConservationPlanand applyfor
an IncidentalTake Permit pursuantto Section10 of theESA. Additionally,we recommend
working with theHawaiT Divisionof Forestryand WildliferegardingcompliancewiththeState
of Hawai‘i Endangered SpeciesLaw 195-D. IfMaui County pursuesdevelopmentofa Habitat
ConservationPlan,we encourage working closelywith the Serviceand the Statetoensureour
requiredissuancecriteriaaremet. The HabitatConservationPlanninghandbook providesmore
detail on the content of a Habitat Conservation Plan and can be found onlineatthefollowing
website: https://www.fws.gov/sites/default/Tiles/docuiTients/hab itai-conservation-planning-
handbook-entirc.pdf
Ifyou have any questionsregardingthisresponse,pleasecontactLindsyAsman, IslandTeam
Manager forHawaii Islandand Maui Nui (808-348-3394 or lindsy_asman@fws.gov).

here both now and forfuturegenerationsMy Testimony:
Aloha,thisisJames Bickfordfrom Hoolawa valleyon the north shore of Maui. Since 1984 we have had a fruitand
flower farm along the Hana sideof Hoolawa stream. For years EMI controlledthe water in Hoolawa.
They took allthe water inthe stream at the lower ditchat the bridge above our property.When Itrained hard the
riveroverflowed the ditch.This changes the qualityand alllifealong the stream.Thisabsolutelydoes not work for
farmers who relyon the water during dry weather and don't need water when itrainshard and the ditchoverflows.
Iam testifyingto support the charteramendment to createa Water Authorityforthe county of Maui.
Please no more researchisnecessaryto recognizethe need to have a say inour water supply.The alternativeisto
give the controlto a foreigncorporation where the bottom lineisprofitand not the citizensof Maui.

Aloha Chair Lee,
I am writingon behalfof West Maui Land Company, and we do not supportBill107,
CDl (2022) initscurrentform. Reducing the salespriceswithouta back-stopwilldiscourage
new projectsand potentiallykillentitledaffordableprojects,includingan 80 unit100%
affordableprojectIam working on.With existingconditionsand salesprices,thisproject(and
possiblyothers),arealreadyteeteringon the edge of infeasibility.Cuttingsalespriceswould be a
nailin the coffin.Without a reasonable rateof return,builderswillbe unable to obtainfinancing,
or not even tryifthe projectisshowing a loss.
As a projectmanager forWest Maui Land Company, Ihave completedtwo 100% workforce
projectsand have a 100% affordable80 unitprojectinthe permittingprocess.I am familiarwith
thechallengesworkforcehousingprojectsfacefrom theentitlementprocesstopermitting,
infrastructure,constructionof the homes to salesthattakeyearsto gettothe finishline.
Conditionscan change dramaticallyas we have allseen inthepastfew yearswith supplychain
issues,materialcostsskyrocketingand interestratesrising,and adoptionof new standardsfor
construction.The 20IH and 2.97processesmay be consideredfasttrack,but afterthatthereis
no ‘fasttrack’processforthesubdivisionand permittingof affordableprojects,even withthe
‘pinkslip’.For example,on my 80-unitproject:
● 7 Vi MONTHS forre-approvalofplansforan off-sitemain waterlineextensioninan
existingroadway. Re-approvalprocessincludedagenciesrequestsforadditionalwork
that added thousands to the construction costs and consultantfees.This $600,000 main
county waterlineextensionisbeing funded on the backs of theworkforcehomes inthis
project.
● 7 MONTHS for comments from wastewater on the on-site. Comments includeda request
fortheplanstobe redrawn ata differentscalewhich requiredtwo weeks of engineering
July 14,2022
Chair Alice Lee & Members
Committee Report No. 22-62;FirstReading of Bill107,CDl (2022)
Page 2 of 2
time,and includeda requestto concreteencase lateralsthatdo not need to be encased
under thecode,addinga costof more than$50,000to theproject.
0 8 MONTHS and stillno comments from PublicWorks.
> ’ Thisisjustthetipoftheicebergofthechallengesand unknowns a projectfaces.Ittakestenacity
and capitalto buildaffordablehomes on Maui, and it’sa choicenot an obligation.Under the
currentpriceguidelinesand conditions,buildersarenot knocking down the door tobuild
affordablehomes.We havea supplyissue.Loweringthe salespriceswillmake projects
a infeasibletobuild,resultinginfewtono homes beingbuiltintheaffordablerangesand causing
existinghome pricesto continuerise.
In 2007, County Council had similarconcernsand doubled the affordabilityrequirementfrom
25% to 50%. This,coupled with poor market conditions,effectivelydestroyedtheworkforce
housing market until2014, when a new councilrevertedthe requirementback to 25%. Many of
you may not recallthis,but we can stilllearnfrom pastactions:By legislatinga higher
percentage,or as proposed today,loweringprices,does not encourage new inventory,and may
have theexactoppositeeffect.
Studiesafterstudieson Maui’s housing challengeshave been paid forby thecounty over the
years.Pleasefollowtherecommendations Policy8,Priority3 of theMaui County
Comprehensive Affordablehousing Plan and considersubsidizingthe individualbuyersand
leavingtheaffordablehousingsalespricesintheircurrentform. The County could encourage
housingby providingthenecessarypublicinfrastructureimprovements and initiatingzoning
changes in areasthatmake sense forhomes. We need alltypesof housing fora broad range of
income groups,professionsand lifestages.Let’swork togetherto findsolutionsforMaui’s
families.
Mahalo forconsideringour testimony.I can be reachedat 808-877-4202.

Heidi Bigelow

Mayor DateFor Transmittal to:
Honorable Kelly Takaya King, Chair
Climate Action, Resilience, and Environment Committee
200 South High Street
Wailuku, Hawaii 96793
Dear Chair King,
SUBJECT: BILL 21 CDl, FDl (2022), SEABIRD AND BIODIVERSITY
PROTECTION (PAF 22-164)
At the Council meeting of July 1, 2022, the Energy Commissioner served as
a resource on behalf of the Mayor’s administration and the Officeof Climate Change,
Resiliency, and Sustainability (CCRS) on the firstreading of Bill 21, CDl, FDl
(2022), entitled “A BILL FOR AN ORDINANCE AMENDING CHAPTER 20.35, MAUI
COUNTY CODE, RELATING TO OUTDOOR LIGHTING FOR PROTECTION OF
SEABIRDS.”
As stated in testimony provided at the Council meeting, we support protecting
wildlife and minimizing light pollution in Maui County, however, our
recommendation is to send Bill 21 back to Committee. This would allow for
discussion to address the narrow specificationof maximum 2% blue lightcontent,
the commercial availabilityand associated warranties of lightingfixturesthat meet
such a specification,and the realisticapplication and enforcement of Bill 21 for
commercial and residential use. We have conferred with Hawaii County and they
confirmed that their outdoor lighting specificationonly applies to commercial street
lighting and parking lot lighting.They have also confirmed that they have faced
issues with difficultyin sourcing commercially available lighting that meets the 2%
blue lightspecificationand offersa reasonable warranty that willcover replacement
of lightingifitfailsas a result of electricityfluctuations from the electricalgrid.We
recommend that Bill 21 include a hardship exemption that would allow for all
G'r
JuXy,lA,-2Q22 m
●Page 2 "
^ -iO
Counter Departments to be exempt from the maximum 2% blue light content
specificationif a commercially available lighting option is not available or if a
commercially available option does not offerindustry standard warranty terms.
Should you have any questions or requirefurtherclarification,please contact
me at ext. 8250 or the Energy Commissioner, Alex deRoodc at ext. 7203.
Sincerely,
Allison Cleghom
Environmental Coordinator
Office of Climate Change, Resiliency,and
Sustainability

From: Jonathan Conrad <10015690@kl2.hi.us> On Behalf Of Jonathan Conrad
Sent: Friday,July15, 2022 8:56 AM
To: ChristianK.Balagso<Christian.Balagso(S)mauicounty.us>
Subject: Re: Bill21
Mahalo forhearing my testimony against bill21.
The MIL isconcerned with the followingissues thatwillaffectthe 4-5000
student athletesas well as the many students, parents and supporters
from the 13 differentschools that make up the Maul Interscholastic
League. Sports highlyimpacted willbe football,cheer, girlsand boys
soccer, swimming and diving,baseball, softball,track and field,tennis
and water polo but the effectswillbe feltby alldisciplines.
As a former CertifiedAthleticTrainer of 23 yeas at Lahainaluna the health
and safetyof our student athletesIsand always be my primary focus. If
thisbillpasses itwillput Lahainaluna High School’s,as well as many MIL
athletes in an unsafe environment for practices and games. The State of
Hawaii High School AthleticAssociation has a Heat Acclimatizationand
Practice Policythatprotectsour student athlete.Itisthe duty of the
school athleticadministrators, athletictrainers,and athleticstaffto follow
these guidelines.Exertionalheat stroke isthe second leading cause of
death insports aftercardiac conditions,according to the National Center
forCatastrophic Sorts InjuryResearch, 2009. Inthe past 11 years, from
the time thisstudy was done, we have allwitnessed the increase of
temperature here on Maui.
We use wet bulb globalthermometer which isa device that monitors
temperature, wet bulb global temperature, relativehumidity,and wind
speed. The reading that device gives us isimputed intoa chartthattells
us how long practicecan be as well as how many water breaks are
mandated and what equipment needs to come off.Abiding to thispolicy
to keep our athletessafe, we have been inthe situationhere at
Lahainaluna to startour practices latein the afternoon and intothe
evening when itiscooler.
Ifwe were put intothe positionto play day games in Lahaina we would be
not only be puttingour student athletesat risk,we would also be puttinga
large portionof our student body and community supporters injeopardy
of contractingheat relatedinjuriesby justbeing present at the games.
We are asking thatthe Billbe sent back to committee so that these
concerns can be addressed and thatthere isa solidplan that has been
developed and in place to help ensure that our student-athletes,student
body, familiesand community supporters inthe MIL do not lose out
again. We are tryingto speak and represent the many student-athletes
and stakeholders who have gone through so much with the shutdown of
seasons and the restrictionsthat continue to be in place with high school
sports. We are concerned thatthisbillmay/will be an additionalburden
and affectour childrenand our community’s social-emotionalrecovery in
very negative ways at a criticaltime where they are juststartingto see
things get better.
Jonathan Conrad
Athletic Director
Lahainaluna High School
ionathan.conrad@kl2.hi.us

Aloha County Counci
Iam writingto you today to oppose the;t;
housing project.There are several reasons why Ido
not agree with thisprojectbeing built.
I liveone streetover from thisprojecton
Namauu place. Ihave experienced the major trafficand
floodingdamages that have occurred injustthe last
few years inthisarea.
First of a
We have an infrastructureproblem on South Kihei
Road and allowing any new constructioninthe area will
cause allof us on Namauu place, Hoonani and
Waipuilanimuch more problems. We only have an
entrance and exitthrough South KiheiRoad and our
wait times to get out of our streethas increased 3xs the
amount during thisyear.
We can not handle any more cars on our roads without
adding sidewalks,culvertrepairs,stop lightsand
possiblymore lanes of trafficto our already congested
South Kihei Road.
Theseidevelopers are not going to make repairsto the
Waipuilani road or South Kihei road that willbe caused
by the heavy'equipment and additionalcars that willbe
travelingevery day inthe area. They do not care about
our old infrastructureor anything that happens to our
way of lifeafterthese houses are built.They only care
about sellingthese homes.
Please remember thatWaipuilani,Namauu and
Hoonani are 3 of the oldest neighborhoods in Kiheiand
allof our way of lifewillbe greatlyaffectedby this
added constructionand congestion to the area. Also
the factthatthey are condo stylehomes does not fit
intoour neighborhoods. These condos are going to be
sold to outsiders who can afford them and will
eventually become rentalincome for most.
Ihave been working with my community foryears on
protectingour Waipuilanigulch and our Waipuilani
Beach Reserve. Icallthisprojectand the Hoonani
projectan attack on our naturalresources. These two
neighborhoods are builtaround our wetlands and this
projectshould not be allowed to happen forthat reason
alone.
We are currentlytryingto get a wetlands protection
resolutionpassed thatwould protectthese wetlands as
you allknow. Ifyou allow thisprojectto move forward,
you are going against allof our community members,
our south Maui vision plans and wetlands experts who
know and have toldthe council what willhappen ifwe
destroy our remaining wetlands.
We need our wetlands that are stillleft in the area to
remain intheirnaturalstate so that they can do their
job.They help our land heal when floodingoccurs and
help to stop runoffthatwillgo straightintoour ocean.
Affordable housing should be builtforMaui residence
and not forjustanyone who works here. Please say no
to thisprojectto protectour naturalresources and
focus your housing support towards trulyaffordable
housing forour people.
Locals don'twant to livein condos and they can'tafford
these proposed priceseven ifthey are coined as
“affordable”.Once our wetlands are destroyed they can
not be brought back. The floodingthat occurs
everywhere around Waipuilaniwillstillhappen ifthese
condos are built.It'sjusta matter ofwhen and where
the floodingwilloccur once developers are allowed to
buildon top of our lastremaining wetlands.
Please say no to destroying our wetlands and our
communities' qualityof life.Say no to Hale Waipuilani.
Sincerely,Brandi Corpuz
Rooted Kekahi Me Ka Aina
808-357-9170

Dear Council Chair Lee, Vice Chair Rawiins-Fernandez and allmembers of the Maui County
Council,
Iwrite to you to today to express my support forCouncil Resolution 22-119, CDl. FDl for the
establishment of a Maui Nul Community Water Systems and an East Maui Regional Community
Board.
As the StateSenator representingallof East Maui, UpCounty Maui along with Moloka'i,Lanai,
Kaho'olawe and Molokini Ifeel the establishment of the proposed system and board willgive a
much-needed voice and a sense of oversightto my constituents.
Respectfully,
Lynn DeCoite

Response to Bill107: CC22-62 Oppositon
RECEIVED
To:
mi JUL 15 PM 7- 26Subject:
OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERKYou don'toftenget emailfrom mike@scshawaii.com.Learnwhv thisisimportant
Aloha County Council
Iam opposed to Bill107 (CC22-62) which seeks to decrease the salespriceforaffordablehousing on Maui. We all
know affordablehousingisneeded but ifthisbillgoes intoeffect,no one willactuallybe buildingthese affordable
homes. Contractors/construction crews would face a losingmoney battlehere and thus, have no incentiveto build
them beyond a kindgestureto the community. Given the amount of time italready takes to buildanything on Maui,
given the variousregulationsand permits needed, the billfailsto considerthese lengthydelays,which also cost
money. Ioppose thisbill.
Mahalo,
Michael Dega, Ph.D.

Istronglyoppose the Waipuilani Hale Affordable Housing proposalfor16E.WaipuilaniRoacT'

My name isSuzanne Dorn and Iliveat 6 E.Waipuilani Road and IOPPOSE the proposed Waipuilanidevelopment at 16 E.
Waipuilani Road.
PLEASE DO NOT ALLOW ANY PROJECT IN THIS AREA -1 suggest making thisa conservationarea,and swap thislandfor
one of the County parcelsthat are availablefor affordablehousing.
Ihave reviewed allthe meetings/videos regardingthisdevelpement and Iam certainany projectbuilton thissitewill
cause an increaseinfloodingto our neighborhood.
Allowing 28 residences/townhomes {withone, two, and 3 bedrooms totaling72 bedrooms) and 59 parkingspaces to be
builtinthislocationisunsafe and totallyirresponsibleof any builder/developerto even considerthislocationforsuch a
dense project.This property islocatedina FEMA floodzone, tsunami zone, tsunami evacuation area and isina SMA
area. WaipuilaniRoad isthe tsunami evacuation streetforthe area. Ihave witnessed major floodinginthisarea over
the years. Waipuilani to Kauha'a Street isone of (oris)the area with the most floodinginallof South Maui.
Betsill/AlaulaBuildersare callingthisan "in-fill"project.Thisdevelopment isfarfrom an in-fillproject.Betsillhas asked
foran exemption inzoning to allow multi-family-duplex units.Please DO NOT allow thiszoning exemption. Allthe
homes in thisarea are zoned R-3, which isa lotarea of 10,000sq foot with an accessory dwelling of up to 720 square
feet. Allowinga zoning exemption to 28 duplex unitsand 59 parkingspaces on a 1.53 acre lotwilldrasticallychange and
have a negative impact on our neighborhood.
WaipuilaniRoad actsat the secondary (main) road when there are accidentsnear Lipoaor on the Highway, the
traffic/gridlockdown WaipuilaniRoad can be backed up at times allthe way to LiloaStreet.When we get our heavy
rainsfrom Kona storms or the runoff floodingfrom Upcounty Kauha'a and WaipuilaniRoad intersectionsbecome
undrivable.You can not even pass a car when drivingup Kauha'a to Liloa.Kauha'astreetisso narrow and filledwith
street parking Idoubt a firetruck could even drive down thisstreet.This small lotat 16 E Waipuilani Road can not
handle thisproject.
Ihave seen 16 E.Waipuilani Road and the surrounding area completely flooded and access cut offto the property allthe
way down to South KiheiRoad, thisproperty actsas de factodetentionbasinforstormwater. Allowingthisdense
projectto be builtWILL create additionalfloodingto the neighbors propertiesand thisalreadyfloodsensitivearea.
Please read thisarticlefrom Hawaii News Now 05/23/2011 -The articletalksabout thisexact area with statements from
former Planning DirectorDavid Goode. InthisarticleGoode talksabout "moving people out" of thisarea. David Goode
1
isnow a PlanningConsultantforAlaua/BetsillBrothersand was at our firstZoom meeting March 01, 2021. David Goode
suggested in 2011 that some people inthisexact area be moved out. How can thisWaipuilani Hale projectbe supported
by anyone knowing how bad the floodingisinthisexactarea and suggestthatthe residents/homesbe located?
https://www.hawaiinewsnow.com/storv/14702361/special-report-kihe i-underwater/
Quote from David Goode; There isan Option B that'smuch easierand much cheaper,but perhaps not os
realistic.
"Theleastcostlythingmight be tomove peopleout Has thateverbeen seriouslyconsidered?Well,thereore
instancesofcompletetowns alongtheMissouriRiverthatwere removed,"saidGoode. "That'snotgonna happen
inKihei,but theremight be a few where itdoesn'tmake sense tohove a structurerightinthemiddle."
T
Attached isa petitionstartedby localWaipuilaniresidentsand signed by over 200 residentslivingIn100
homes in the immediate area. I am aware of an "on-line counter petition"startedby Betsilito support their
Waipuilaniproject.We chose to not startan on-linepetitionand went door to door to speak to allthe
neighborsand;everyonethatwillbe affectedby thisdevelopment.We couldhave easilygatheredmany more
signaturesifwe chose to do an onlinesponsored petitionlikeBetsili.Our petitionissigned by local
residents/homeowners who are veryconcerned about thisdevastatingprojectinour Waipuilanicommunity.
The lastmeeting iswas mentioned that64% of homeowners inthisareaarefrom out of state.Ilooked up all
the home owners on WaipuilaniRoad, and 100% of these homes areowned by Maui residents,thereareonly4
homes on thisstreetthat are second homes (but the owners stillliveon Island)with 2 of the owners owning 2
homes on WaipuilaniRoad, These are alllocalresidentsinthisarea.
Pleaselistento the localresidents/neighbors,some who have livedinthisareafor60 plusyears.We allknow this
area,the land,the neighborhood, the roads,the flood issues,the safetyconcerns, etc and WE ALL suggest thisproject
be relocated to a more suitable area. Please consider our South Maui representativeKellyKingsviews on opposing this
projectas welllas the KiheiCommunity Association,Maui Tomorrrow, SierraClub,South Maui Save the Wetlands Hui,
and allthe area neighbors who are asking thisprojectto not be approved inthisarea.
1am respectfullyaskingthe Maui County Councilto not approve the 16 E.WaipuilaniRoad housingprojectfor
all the above reasons.
Mahalo,
Suzanne Dorn
808-283-7913

I OPPOSE HALE WAIPUILANI PROJECT CR 22-61
From:
Sent:
im JUL 15 AH » 15To:
Subject:
OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERK
I You don'toftenget email from suziedorn(S)gmail.com.Learn why thisisimportant
I OPPOSE HALE WAIPUILANI PROJECT
Mv name is Suzanne Dorn and Ioppose the Hale Waipuilani project.This projectneeds to be disapproved. My last
testimony Isuggested keeping the zoning to R3 singlefamily homes only,but afterreading allthe documents for this
development, listeningto the presentationsand comments from engineeringand other departments -1 am now
convinced that any development builton thislotwillhave a directnegative impact and create more floodingfor this
area. Iam askingforthispropertyto become a conservationzone; There iscounty land foraffordablehousing set aside
near the R/T park forabout 1200 homes, land inMaui Lani,near the Kihei policestationand in Puunene. Please DO NOT
approve thisproject-there should be no new houses in flood zones.
Maui has many affordablehousing projectsbeing built,there isa lotteryina few weeks inKiheiforover 100 affordable
homes, 40 homes innorth Kihei,Bestilisworking on 204 affordablehomes inwailuku,there isanother 700 affordable
home projectinthe plans,plusmany other projects.
At the lastmeeting Carnecelli said they were open to a land swap, theironly concern was itwould take time but why
should we rush this?They should put thisprojectina safe locationwhere itisa win-win for everyone. The county has
offereda landswap or the County can purchasethisland back; the developer only paid 1 milliondollarsfor it.Turn this
area intoa conservation area,thisisthe best possibleuse for 16 E Waipuilani Road
At the lastmeeting Carnecellisaidtheirflood water runoff willbe cleaner.
Ifyou do not livein this floodplain you do not know how dangerous and scary itis.These flood events are not justa few
hours,these floodevents can be days,months and sometimes years to recover. Ihave experienced many floodswhile
livinghere.We have 100 sand bags stockpiledinthe corner of our yard at alltimes. During storms and flood advisories
my husband and Ihave to sleep inshiftsso we can be alertto flooding.When floods damage our home we have claimed
on our flood insurance.
We believe this project will make flooding worse.
When the floodsstartthere isno help,every man ison theirown. When we call911 they can not help us,they say they
have too many other thingsto deal with. The streetsusuallyflood firstwhich makes evacuation difficultor impossible.
When the water startscoming intoyour house itisalready too lateto do anything about it.During floodswe have
begged passingfiretrucksforhelp,we have calledthe county and the authoritiesand no one can do anythingto help us.
The only people who have ever helped duringthe storms are our immediate neighbors.
There are no police,no emergency services,no road works blinkingsigns,there isabsolutelyno help. When the streets
flood,passingtruckscreate largewaves that createmore problems and the entiresituationiscomplete chaos.
The aftereffectscan be long lived,weVe been stuckin our home for a week untilthe mud driesenough to be shoveled
away to move our carsthere isno help for this.Aftermajor floodingthe Red Cross comes to check on us and our
homes, they giveus bleach and cleaningsuppliesand talkabout the riskof mold inthe house, body infectionsand staph
from the floodingwaters.
At the lastmeeting a Council member saidthey were thinkingof the 28 people who need homes, well Iam asking to
1
please consider the 28 plus people who can losetheirhomes due to the directimpacts thisprojectwould have on our
neighborhood.
an -
Mahalo foryour consideration.
-I « M ei jui swSuzanne Dorn
808-283-7913

[You don'toftenget emailfrom mjduberstein@gmail.com.Learn why thisisimportant at

Aloha
I have not testified on this item before.
That,unfortunately,was an oversight.
Buildinganything ina floodzone isnot smart development.
Attempting to put workforce or so-calledaffordable housing in a flood zone isdoubly ridiculous.
Iappreciate that a solutionisable to happen on behalf both of the developer—who should have known better—and the
County to make a land swap thatcan,with your approval,resultinthe flood zone land be turned intoa permanent
conservation site.
Do it,please.
You willbe remembered as officialswho willlong be remembered as making a criticaland positivestep inprotectingthe
aina.
And, the developer willbe able to buildtrue workforce and affordablehousing on a bettersite.
As we move intocreationof the South Maui Community Plan,thisstep willa model forthe future of our community.
Malama aina.
Ua Mau ke Ea o Ka Aina I Ka Pono

RE: Hawaii HotelAllianceOppositionto Bill21,CDl ,FD1 (2022)
Aloha Chair Lee, Vice-Chair Rawlins-Fernandez and Members of the County Council,
Mahalo forthisopportunityto providetestimonyinadvance of theCounty Council’sJune 21,2022
meeting.Maui’s lodgingindustryisdeeplycommitted to itsstewardshiproleforthe localenvironment—
includingtheprotectionof Hawaii’snativeseabirdspecies—but ifenactedBill21,CDl, FDl’s outdoor
lightingfixturerequirementswould impose impractical,ambiguous and unsaferequirementson
businesses(and residents)throughouttheCounty which run countertotheguidanceof stateand federal
agency expertsinthisspace.
For the reasonsour testimony willexpand upon below, the Hawaii Hotel Alliancemust regretfully
oppose Bill 21. CDl. FDL
Bill21.CDl, FDl Conflictswith PrevailingExpertOpinion:
Stateand federalagencieswith expertiseon what constitutesappropriatemeasurestoprotectendangered
seabirdshave developedcorrespondinglightminimizationguidelines.Importantly,theseguidelineswere
includedinAppendix E to theKauai SeabirdHabitatConservationPlan(KSHCP, orPlan)’—aPlan
preparedin2020 by the U.S. Fishand WildlifeService(USFWS) and theHawaii Departmentof Land
and NaturalResources(DLNR). This Appendix includesrequirementsrelatingtotheplacement,angling
and shieldingof lights,as well as otherkey lightingmitigationmeasures.At thetime the KSHCP was
prepared,severalgroups asked theagenciesto alsoimpose lightspectrumrequirementssimilartothose
found in Bill21,CD 1,FD1. Itisimportantto underscoretheexpertsattheseagenciesdeclinedtodo so.
Bill21.CDL FDl IgnoresEstablishedSafeW Parameters:
The wellbeingof our lodgingguestsand employees alikeisoftheutmostimportance,and somethingthat
should not be unduly compromised inanythingadoptedby theCounty viaBill21,CDl, FDl. There are
^See Attachment,"KSHCP Appendix E.
9359103.1
The Honorable AliceL. Lee,Chair ^
The Honorable Keani Rawlins-Fernandez,’Vicfe-Chair
Maui County Council
Page 2 - ■ ■■
\' ■
human safetybenefitsof lightswith shorterwavelengths,includingthattheycreategreatercontrastand
enhance
peripheralvision—yet Bill21, CD 1,FD1 does not properlyaccount forthiscriticaldynamic. Conversely,
recognitionof human safetyneeds ispresentwithinstateand federalagency lightminimizationguidelines
forprotectingendangered seabirds,and the KSHCP itselfrequiresappropriatelightinglevelstipulations
forparticularlightfunctions—recognizingthe standardspreparedby theIlluminatingEngineering
SocietyofNorth America (lESNA). More specifically,the lESNA standardsprovidelightlevel
recommendations forseveralapplications,includingparkinglots,walkways and roads—again, something
forwhich Bill21, CDl, FDl does not properlyaccount.The County should not be requiringlightlevels
be lessthan theserecognizedand patternedilluminationstandardsforhuman safety,which Bill21, CDl,
FDl would concerninglydo.
Exacerbatingtheseconcerns isthe factthatBill21,CDl, FDl would newly apply itsprovisionsto
"[tjemporaryhoteland condominium beach securitylighting”and ”[s]afetyand securitylightingfor
waterfeatures,” which were previouslyexempt inbroadscope.This could increasesafetyhazards for
hotelguestsand make itimpossibleforsecuritystaffto implement safetyor securitylightingattheir
hotelsand resorts,even temporarilytoresolvepressingsafetymatters.
Bill 21. CDl. FDl Contains Unworkable. Unsubstantiated Provisions:
A criticalrequirementwithinBill21, CDl, FDl would be thatalloutdoorlightingfixturesmust "limit
shortwavelengthcontenttono more thantwopercentof bluelightcontent” (with "bluelightcontent”
definedas "theratioof theamount ofenergy emittedby the outdoor lightifxturebetween 400 and 500 nm
dividedby theamount ofenergy between 400 and 700 nm ”).Such a standardwould be impossiblefor
most—if not all—residents and businesses to adhere to.
The shortwavelength contentmeasurement specifiedinBill21,CDl, FDl isnotsomething thatis
typicallyprovidedby lightbulb and fixturemanufacturers,nor isitincluded on the labelsfortheir
products,so one would be unable to detemiine ifnew lightstheypurchaseeffectivelycomply withthe
law.Most businessesand residentslacktheequipment and expertisethatwould be necessaryto perfonn
thecalculationsnecessarytodeterminecompliance,allwhile stateand federalagencieshave yetto
determineifthereisscientificsupportto suggestthisrequirementisbeneficialforseabirds.Stateand
federalagenciesknowledgeableaboutendangeredseabirdprotectionhave notrecommended adopting
thisforalloutdoorlighting,which means Bill21,CDl, FDl isagaina troublingoutlier.
Moreover, requiringall(exceptneon) outdoorlightsto be filteredlightemittingdiode bulbs(LEDs) is
concerningon added grounds,as LED filtersarenotcommonly used and arenotwidely available.Per the
County’sDeputyCorporationCounsel,^thereisonlyone vendor who arguablysellssuch filters,yet
everyoutdoorlightintheCountywould requireone—presentingcosttrepidations,supplyanxieties,and
even worriesover such a productvoidinglightmanufacturerwarrantiesgiven thisalterationof intended
use.All fora practiceoutsideof what expertswould constituteas necessary.
^See Memorandum, "Ordinanceon ProtectingSeabirdsfrom Outdoor LightingBiilNo. 21 CDl (2022)" (May 24,
2022}.
Page 2 of 3
The Honorable Alice L. Lee, Chair
The Honorable Keani Rawlins-Femandez, Vice-Chair
Maui County Council
Page 3
Further,Bill21, CDl, FDl would take effectimmediately forallnew or newly replacedlightingfixtures,
and provideonly a three-yeargraceperiodforexistingfixtures.Given thenumerous compliance
challengespresent,thiswould createimmediate difficultiesforbusinessessuch as hotelsand resortswith
large
numbers of outdoorlightingfixtures.And even a three-yearperiodmay not be enough time to comply,
particularlywith stipulationslikethoseforbluelightcontentand filteringthatarestymiedby present
manufacturerspecificationsand availabilities.
Concluding Remarks:
In sum, the lodgingindustryon Maui issteadfastlycommitted to protectingHawaii’snativeseabird
species,includingby minimizing lighting.When one minimizes lightingthough,itisalsoimportantthat
human safety—namely thesafetyof our guestsand employees—is not compromised, especiallywith
impracticaland burdensome requirementsthatdo nothold demonstrated,expert-validatedbenefits.
Bill21, CDl, FDl issimply not a sensibleapproach to the important protectionof Hawaii’s native
seabirds,especiallygiven that alternativeoptions are available.One logicaloptionwould be forMaui
to adopt requirementssimilarto those includedinthe KSHCP’s Appendix E, which reflectsmindful
policiesestablishedby subject-matterexpertsfrom USFWS and DLNR. Another optionwould be to
impose a correlatedcolortemperaturerequirement,astheStateimposeson itsown agencies(this
measurement istypicallyincludedon LED lightbulb labels).^Any new requirementsshouldalso
preserveexistingexemptions and allow enough leadtime forcompliance.
For the many reasons articulatedin our testimony, the Hawaii Hotel Alliance urges the County
Council to rejectBill21, CDl, FDl and instead pursue an alternativeconservation pathway.
Without such action,therisksand harms broughtaboutby Bill21,CDl, FDl’s requirements—^to
residents,businesses,guestsand more—^will greatlyexceedany benefitthatmay possiblybe attained.
Mahalo Nui Loa,
3
Gerard C. Gibson
President
Hawai'i Hotel Alliance
^ See Hawaii Revised Statutes Section 201-8.5.
