APPENDIX E: Guidelines for Adjusting Lighting at Facilities
1. Guidelines for Adjusting Lightingat Facilities
This appendix provides detailedguidelinesto inform minimization measures that can be
customized to address an array of possiblelightingissuesat Participantfacilities.A lighting
minimization plan to achieve the maximum extent practicablewillbe included ineach
ParticipantPIP.
These guidelinesrepresent best availablescience at the time of KSHCP permit issuance.Over
the lifethe plan,likelynew information and new technologieswillbe available,and this
appendix may be updated accordingly.
Not alllightingguidelinesare appropriateforalltypes of facilities.Some represent long term,
infrastructuresolutions,and others may be implemented on a seasonal basis.
1.1.Deactivate Non-Essential Lights
Prioritizationof seabird and honu lightattractionminimization measures involvesevaluating
lightneeds to determine ifnon-essentiallightscan be deactivatedduring the seabirdfallout
season (September 15 to December 15) and turtlenestingseason (May 15-December 15).
Deactivatingthe lightsavoids the potentialfor lightattractionthat those lightscould otherwise
cause. Turning offa subset of lights,both unshielded and shielded,during the falloutseason
(September 15 to December 15) can assistwith minimizingthe riskof seabirdlightattraction,if
those lightsare not necessary.IntheirPIPs,Applicantsmust provide rationaleforany facility
lightsthat cannot be deactivatedduring seabirdfalloutseason, and detailwhat other
minimization practiceswillbe implemented on lightsthat willremain illuminated.The
regulatoryagencies willreview the evaluation and justificationas provided in applicant PIPs.
Similarly,turningout lightsthat shine directlyon beaches during the turtlenestingseason (May
15-December 15) can prevent hatchling disorientation.Avoid use of the following lamp styles
on beachside or shore perpendicularto sidesof a structure:privatebalcony lights,up lights;
decorativelighting,not necessary for human safetyor security;pond lights;and beach lighting.
Timers or other similar devices should be used to ensure the selectedlightsremain offduring
the turtlenesting season. This measure may require the installationof independent light
switches. Conversely,to prevent accidentalactivation,lightfixturescan be removed forlights
that willno longer be needed at a facility.
1.2.InstallFullCut-off LightFixtures
A fullcut-offfixturerefersto a lightfixturewhich that does not shine lightabove a 90 degree
horizontalplane. For lightsnecessary to be activated,fullcut-offfixturesprovide an effective
measure to achieve lightminimization because they prevent lightfrom shiningdirectlyupward.
These types of lightshouse the lightbulb up within the fixtureso that no bulb protrudes below
(Figure1). Such fixturesmust be mounted at appropriate angle so they point directlydown to
the ground. Many lightmanufacturers provide lightfixtureinformation along with the light
specificationsto indicateifa fixtureisa fullcut-offdesign. The internationalDark Sky
Association(www.darksky.org) isa good source forinformationon fullcut-offlightsand
provides additionalreferences to lightengineering resources and lightmanufactures.
Figure 1: Examples of fullcut-offlightfixtures.Source: www.darkskv.org.
Along shorelines,exteriorfixtureson the seaward (makai)and the shore perpendicularsidesof
the building(and on the landward sideof the buildingifthey are visiblefrom the beach) should
be down-litfixtures,fullyshielded and fullcut-off,louvered, or recessed fixturesthat do not
have reflective inner surfaces. These fixtures should use low wattage bulbs (e.g.,<50w). All
exteriorfixtureson the landward (mauka) side of the buildingshould be directed downward
only (Witherington & Martin 2003).
1.3.Shielding LightFixtures
This minimization measure aims to achieve the functional equivalent of a fullcut-offlight
fixtureby installinga shield,visor,hood or similaron an existinglightfixtureto prevent light
from shiningupward and reducing trespass.Inadditionto the shielding,to achieve the
functional equivalent of a fullcut-offfixture,a lightfixtureshould be adjusted so that itpoints
directlydown perpendicularto the ground to createa level,horizontalplane between the
fixtureand the ground, and have the bulb housed withinthe lightfixture(Figures2 & 3). Reed
et al.(1985) suggest that in areas where other lightsources are rare,the shieldingof principal
lightswould likelyhave a largereffectindecreasingseabird lightattraction.
Figure 2: Installationof an appropriately sized floodlightshield.Source: www.darkskv.org.
Shielded Floodlight

1o>
Figure 3: Before and aftereffectsof shieldingand lightmanagement designed to minimize light
attractionriskto seabirds and to decrease lightpollution.Source: www.darkskv.org.
1.4. Angle Lights Downward
Angling and repositioninglightspresents a potentialalternativeto shieldingor replacinglight
fixturesand may be sufficientto make lightsfullycut-offand eliminatelightshininghorizontally
and vertically(Figure4). To achieve the functionalequivalentof a fullcut-offfixture,a light
fixtureshould be adjusted so that itpoints directlydown perpendicular to the ground to create
a level;horizontalplane between the fixtureand the ground, and have the bulb housed within
the lightfixture.Tree strapdownlights may be used to minimize seabirdlightattractionunless
turtlesmay be present on the adjacent beach.
4
Figure1-: (a)Wall mount cylinderdown-light,(b)bollardswith downward-directed louvers,and (c)
sign lightsangled downward. From
http://mvfwc.com/media/418417/SeaTurtle LiEhtineGuidelines.pdf(FWC 2011).
1.5. Place Lights Under Eaves
Lightfixturesplaced under buildingeaves can achieve the functionalequivalent of a fullcut-off
fixture.The architecturaleave acts as shieldto prevent lightfrom shiningdirectlyupward.
1.6.ShiftLightingAccording to the Moon Phase
This minimization measure addresses lightingfor which the need, or purpose, for the lighting
can be shiftedintiming each yearto coincide with the moon phase. Because a reduction in
lightattractionhas been correlatedwith the fullphase of the moon (Reed et al.1985; Telferet
al.1987) lightsfor essentialfunctions,and for which that function can be shiftedintiming,
should coincide with the fullphase of the moon and avoid the dark phase of the moon. Itis
important to note that a fullmoon that isobscured by heavy cloud cover could simulate the
dark phase of the moon. By not activatingthose lightsduring the dark phase of the moon the
effectof those lightsisreduced. Examples of activitiesthat could be minimized with this
measure include scheduling of nighttime events,such as festivalsor sporting events.
1.7. InstallMotion Sensors for Motion-activated Lighting
Motion sensors switch lightson only when triggered,thereby limitingthe time that the light
stays on and reducing itspotentialfor seabird lightattraction.Ifa sensor lightisrequired for
securitypurposes, the lightequipped with the sensor should be at low lightlevels.For
example. LightEmitting Diode (LED) streetlightsand parking lotlightscan be activatedwhen
needed and dim when no activityisdetected nearby. However for those fixtures,fullcut-off
designs or the functionalequivalents are recommended because of the possibilityof light
attractionoccurring when the motion-sensor lightisactivated or in the event that the motion
sensor equipment malfunctions and the lightremains on.
Where motion sensors are impractical(eg at sportingevents),stadium lightsshould be turned
offas soon as the publicleaves the stadium.
1.8. Decrease Lighting Levels
This measure addresses lowering lightintensitylevels(e.g.,measured inlumens) while still
meeting the need to safelycomplete tasks and serve the purpose of the light. Guidance on
standards forthe appropriate lightinglevelfor a particularlightfunctionshould be followed as
provided by the appropriateagency or professionaland technicalorganization. For example
the IlluminatingEngineering Society of North America (lESNA) provides recommendations for
lightlevelsfor severalapplicationsincludingparking lots,walkways, and roads. In addition
individualentitiesmay have standards and best practicesfor lightingneeds.
For many applicationswhere lightingisneeded, brighterlightingmay not always provide the
best lightingfor the needed function. Itisoften the case where reduced lightinglevelscan
provide for the needed function of the lighting.For example, forsecuritypurposes overly
brightlightstend to create blindspots,or very dark shadows, outsidethe litarea that preclude
effectivevisibility.Well placed, but reduced lightingcan provide for more effectivesecurity.
Therefore, when Participantsseek to enhance onsitevisibilityfor security,while reducing riskto
seabirds,the appropriate reductionof lightlevels(alongwith shieldingand re-anglinglights)
forms a startingpoint to accomplish both purposes.
1.9.Decrease Visibilityof InteriorLights
Facilitieswith largeand/or numerous windows, tallbuildingprofiles,or largeglassfacades may
alsopose a riskof lightattractionto Covered Seabirdson Kaua'i.The following measures are
based, in part,on effortsin citiesinCanada and the mainland USA to decrease harmful effects
of buildingson birdsand apply to seabirds in that they can decrease the amount of light
escaping from within buildings(Cityof Toronto 2007; Evans Ogden 2002):
● Installscreens or shades over largewindows that are lowered nightlyduring the fallout
season;
● Modify buildingsand decrease or eliminatelightglow from withina facility;
● Create glassopacity to prevent the escape of internallight.Tinted glassor filmwith a
visiblelighttransmittance value of 45 percent or lessshould be appliedto allwindows
and doors within lineof sightof the beach;
● Installphysicalscreens outside a building;
● Installlandscapinginfrontof largewindows;
● Close allwindow blindsafterdaylighthours untilsunrise;
● Stagger the operation of lightsin the evening or morning hours so that not alllightsare
turned on at once; and
● Maximize the number of offices or indoor rooms that turn offalllightsaftersunset;
● Place reminder noticeson switches to turn out lightsor draw curtains/blindsin
oceanfront rooms. This should include coastal areas that are on the perpendicular sides
of the structure;
Turn offroom and lanailightingthat are not needed;a.
b. Relocate moveable lamps away from windows that are visiblefrom the beach;
and
Close opaque curtainsor blindsafterdark to block insidelightfrom shining
outside.
c.
1.10.Use Light-lessTechnologies
Where conditionsand facilityneeds permit, technologies that do not use light,such as closed-
circuittelevision(CCT) with infraredilluminators,may be effectivelyemployed to "see" at night
thus enabling some of the lightsto be turned off.For example, any fenced areas or the dark
sides of facilitiescan be monitored with CCT so that lightsdo not need to be used or installed.
1.11.Plant Vegetation Around Lightsto Reduce LightVisibility
Trees and shrubs can be planted so that they over-arch lightsor shieldside visibilityof lights
along the coast or along a ridge,for example. Whether the lightsare mounted on 20-foot poles,
walkways, or within landscaped areas,having adjacent or overarching vegetation would further
reduce the riskof seabird lightattractionthat any residuallightscattermay pose. Long-term
planning and maintenance of screening vegetation isencouraged, where appropriate to the
uses and needs of the affected lights.
1.12.Lower Height of Lights
Lightthat islow in height has potentialto reduce the effectof lightattractionbecause lower
lightsmay be lessvisibleto passingseabirds.Installingground-level lighting,such as along
walkways, and reducing pole heightcan decrease lightwaste and trespass.
1.13. Use Longer LightWavelengths
In coastalareas,use of acceptable lightssuch as:LPS 18w, 35w, red, orange or amber LEDs
(truered,orange or amber diodes,but not filters),true red neon, and other lightingsources
that produce lightwavelengths of 560 nm or longer (Witherington et al.2014). Long
wavelength lights,e.g.,those that produce lightthat measures greater than 560 nanometers on
a spectroscope, are required for allconstruction visiblefrom and adjacent to sea turtlenesting
beaches. Turtlesare most sensitiveto shortwavelengths of light,probably because they livein
a marine environment that filtersout long wavelengths. Green turtlesare leastattractedto
longer wavelength lightinthe yellow-orange to red end of the spectrum (630 to 700 nm)
(Witheringtonand Martin 2000). Inthe absence of other lightsources, however, turtlesmay
stillbe attractedto long wavelength light.
Filtersdesigned to exclude transmission of short wavelengths (<570nm) can be fittedto high
pressure sodium (HPS) vapor lights.Such filtershave been found to be effectiveat avoiding
disruptionof nestingfemales (Salmon, 2006) but even filteredHPS lighthas been found to
attracthatchlings,although not as stronglyas unfilteredHPS lights(Sellaet al,2006). Filtering
alone is thus not sufficient to avoid attraction and disruptionof hatchlingorientation.Bright
white lightfixtures,such as metal halide,halogen, fluorescent,mercury vapor and incandescent
lamps, are not approved for beachside or shore perpendicular sidesof a structure.Limiteduse
of shorterwavelength lightsmay be approved inareas where directand indirectlightor glow
could not possiblybe visiblefrom the beach due to installationof opaque "lightfencing"(see
below).

Subject: Bill107,CDl (2022).A Billforan Ordinance Amending Chapter 2.96 Maui County
Code, RelatingtoAffordableHousing SalesPriceGuidelines
Dear Chair Lee:
The Hawaii Housing Financeand Development Corporation(HHFDC) would liketo offerthe followins
comments on Bill107,CDl (2022). One of the many factorsaffectingthe shortsupplyand high home
pricesin Hawaii isgovernment regulationthatlimitstheabilityofthehousingmarket to createtheunits
necessaiyto meet demand. According to a recentstudyby UHERO, theaverageregulatoiyburdensin
Hawaii aresignificantlyhigherthan thosefound inany otherstate.Maui countyranked number 6 outof
30 countieswiththehighestmedianhome prices.*
While we supportpursuinghousingpoliciesthataredesignedto promotethedevelopmentof affordable
housing,thisproposalto cap salespricesmay have theoppositeeffect.Itmay have theunintended
consequence of constrainingdevelopment of new for-saleaffordablehousingprojectsby making them
financiallyinfeasibleto build.
For example, under Bill 107 we estimatethatfor 100% AMI households,themaximum pricefora 1-
bedroom unitwould be about $360,000 and the maximum pricefora 2-bedroom unitwould be about
$390,000. Since the per-unitcostof new homes iswellover $400,000,developersmay choosenotto
develop thistype of housingand Maui County’s insufficientsupplyof affordablefor-salehousing may
only worsen over time.
Developerstodaymust contendwithinflation(whichincreasedevelopmentcosts)and higherinterestrates
(which reducerevenuefrom home sales).We respectfullyrecommend thattheCouncilcontinuethedialog
with affordablehousing developersto establishpoliciesand programsthatwillactuallyachieveyour
housing goals.Thank you foryour considerationand theopportunitytoprovidecomments.
SincefeT
Denise Iseri-Matsubara
Executive Director
^ UHERO Study,Apr 2022.Pg.4- https://uhero.hawaii.edu/wp-
conteiU/uploads/2022/04/MeasuringTheBurdenQfHousingRegulationlnH awaii.pdf

I 20You don'toftengetemailfrom aya.beloved@gmail.com.Learnwhy thisisimportant o"im-< mAloha,
My name isAya Iwasaki I'm a Ha'iku Resident.
I'mwritinginsupport ofthe Maui Community Water Authority.

7311
xm
Establishinga countywide community water authoritygives us the opportunityto bring in the rightexpertise
and acquire the funding to manage thisprecious resource wisely.As itstands, aging infrastructureand
mismanagement contributeto unacceptable amounts ofwasted water,with major impacts to localecosystems
watersheds, and communities.
GO
Oo
GO
A Maui County Community Water Authoritywould allowus to investinwatershed restorationand repairsand
maintenance to old wasteful infrastructure,develop transparent,efficient,and culturallyand environmentally
responsiblesystems of management, providelocaljobs inresource management, and ensure water security
forallof Maui’s currentand futureresidents.Regional community boards incorporatethe indispensable
knowledge from localcommunities who have an intimateunderstanding oftheirwatersheds and water
systems.
The alternativeisprivate,corporatecontrolover publictrustresourceswithno guaranteeofgood stewardship
or equitable distributionof water or water rates.
Today millionsof gallonsof water per day are being divertedfrom the EastMaui streamsforcommercialuse,for
some years leavinglocalfarmers without water.
Sadly,generationsof kalofarmersgot wiped out because theirwatersourcewas takenaway.Thisnotonlyeffected
theirlivelihood,plantingand farming halo isa Hawaiian practiceand birthright.
There has been a 30 year water leasein placegivingprivateentitiesrightsto divertwater from the EastMaui Rivers
held by Alexander & Baldwin and East Maui IrrigationCo (EMI.)
With the currentleasesetto expire,EMI along with Mahi Pono arenow attemptingto get another30 yearleasefor
our waters which would allow them to divertup to 88 milliongallonsa day from EastMaui Streams.
A8iB/EMI and Mahi Pono/PSP have caused greatharm to thecommunity (includinglocalkalofarmers& the
preservationof Indigenousways),as wellas ecologicalharm by alteringtheessentialmauka to makaiwaterflow.
We cannot affordto perpetuate the status quo. Maui County must do betterto ensure the sustainabilityof our
resources, our'aina, and our communities.
The Community Water Authority would bring the management of our waters back to the residentsof Maui.
Mahalo foryour time and effortsforour beloved island.
1
Aya Kamanakai Iwasaki

DEAR MAUI COUNTY COUNCILMEMBERS,
My brother,John Kaahui, and iare currentlyattending Seabury Halland are servingon
the Maui Youth Council. In our most recent Youth Council meeting, \A/eheard from
CouncilmemberTamara Paltin,who expressed to us brieflyher historyinthe Maui
community, her interestinthe Planning Department, and even how she rose to become
a Councilmember. Councilmember Paltin offered us a clearerunderstanding of the
formulation process of Billsand then the members of our Youth Council were
tasked/encouraged to perform researchon a Billwhich could benefitthe Maui
community.
Since our recent Youth Council meeting, my brother and Ihave learned about the
proposed Bill107, which touches on our one of our biggestconcerns forMaui's future:
affordablehousing.Severalmembers of the Youth Councilhave expressed an interestin
findingway{s) to improve on the criticalshortage of affordablehousing inMaui.
My brother and Iboth believethat thiswould be an idealBillto researchas a collective
inour Maui Youth Council.We, as youth lookingto our future inMaui, willbe most
impacted by the housing shortage which thisBilladdresses.
John and Irespectfullyask you, the Members of the Maui County Council,to allow the
Maui Youth Council the opportunity to research thisBilland present to you our findings
within the time designated by our Youth Counciltimeline.
Thank you for your serviceand for your guidance instructuringa good futureforMaui.

mean the retentionbasin needs to be empty or dry to functionproperly.Then thatwould also
mean “Fill” will be used to fillthe water table below and most likelythe springcavitiesbelow
that flows from Waiohuli mauka to Waiohuli makai. ACCOUNTABILITY isurgenthere when
thenaturalresource“The Wai” iscompromised and thecontinuityof natures“Circleof Life”is

Aloha-
My name isRussellKahikinaokala Akaka Kallstrom and in my personalcapacityas a member of the Molokai
Community Iwould liketo extend my heartfeltcondolences to the 'ohana of ColetteMachado. Colettewas an
activistand advocate for Molokai- she was a founding member of The Molokai Land Trustwhich has protectedover
1600 acres of Molokai coastline.She was a key advocate for the Papakilo Database projectspearheaded by OHA
that houses the largestIndigenouslanguage libraryinthe world,which isin'oleioHawai'i. Both have impacted me
greatly,but 1must admit when Iheard the news of her passing,my firstthought was not of these or any of her many
publicaccolades,but how much she loved my daughter. She was a woman of faithinJesus and she constantly
supported and prayed for my daughter and for us through some terriblyrough times and Ihonor and aloha that. I
recallthe Scripture,"Pomaika'ika po'e make, ke make iloko o ka Haku, ma keiahope aku: he 'oia'i'o,wahi a ka
'Uhane, iho'omaha lakou iko lakou luhi;a ke hahai nei no ho'ika lakou mau hana me lakou.''(Rev 14:13)<Blessed
are the dead, that hereafterdie inthe Lord. For truly,says the Spirit,they willrestfrom theirtoil;and theirdeeds
willfollowafterthem."> And that,rightlyso,iswhat today and thisresolutionisabout, allowingthose deeds to
finallycatch up to her. Iknow that inthe end, we are judged by how much we loved. And Itestifyshe loved greatly.
Aloha ke Akua. Mahalo Myron, allthe 'ohana,counciland testifiers.

Aloha e County CouncilMembers!
My name isCheryl King,a Seabird BiologyTechnicianwith the Maui Nui SeabirdRecovery Projectand 1alsorun the
Hawaiian HawksbillConservation projectunder the Hawai'iAssociationforMarine Education and Research. While
working as a marine biologistforover 25 years (inHawai'isince1999),I'vewitnessed firsthand countlessincidencesof
the negative effectslightinghas on sea turtlesand seabirdsspecifically.Human lightingoverridestheirnaturalinstincts
during many crucialstages of theirlives.Every death and injurycaused by human lightingshould have been preventable
with smarter lightingchoices.
There are so many impacts facingour naturalworld everyday,and making our lightingwildlifefriendlyisone ofthe
easiestactionsthat everyone can controlevery day. There are so many solutionsout thereand readilyavailable.
Sometimes it'sas easy as turningoffthe switch! It'sthe leastwe can do as we tryto co-existwith nativewildlife,who
inhabitedthese islandslong before humans and completely deserve protecting.
Ifullysupport Bill21 (CDl FDl) thataddresses harmful outdoor lightinginorderto protectour dark nightskiesforour
human health,astronomy and our nativewildlife.We trulyappreciateyour hardwork on thisand lookforwardto your
vote of support on July15th.
Mahalo nui loa!
~Cheryl King,MSc.

WrittenTestimony to OPPOSE the Hale Waipuilani
WaipuilaniRoad. KiheiReferto "CR22-61"
To:
. ,j
Subject:
You don'toftengetemailfrom ikuwa74@gmail.com.Learnwhv thisisimportant
Aloha we are Walter and OpuulanI Kaauamo Kuluhiwa who have resideat 75 East WaipuilaniRoad forthe past 50 years
datingback toAugust 1,1972.As residentsand propertyowners ofWaipuilaniwe totallyDISAGREE and OPPOSE the
Waipuilanihousing development at 16 E. WaipuilaniRd. We have seen the progressionof Kihei,as a whole to “no end".
Here are our concerns to the matter.
1. Flood, Zone, Area—We consider itthrough because we have seen the resultsaftera very rainyday, floodingthis
street,being mud/ filled,debris/choked and the onset of water retainment at the end of Waipuilani exitingto
South KiheiRoad. Untila couple of years ago, therehas been some correctionmade at thisparticulararea.
However, recently,when we experienced heavy rain,there’sstillthe settlementof water exitingto S. KiheiRd.
We even have picturesof a neighborsson who stood inknee deep water frontingtheirhome. Ittook days forthe
water to dry up. One of the residentsexitingWaipuilanito S. KiheiRd. also builta stone wall and a berm to keep
water out from theirpropertywhen Waipuilanigets flooded.Luckily,we liveon a higher levelenteringour
residence and propertyso when itgets flooded we are not affected.However, some of our neighbors were not so
fortunate because their homes were situated on the same levelas the road so whenever there was heavy rainor
floodthey had to deal with the clean up.
TrafficCongestion: How wonderful itwas to move to Waipuilaniin 1972. DefinitelyNo TrafficCongestion and also
none on S. KiheiRd. Presently,thisroad has been used fordiversionoftrafficwhenever there’sa fire,accidentor
event in Kihei.Sometimes vehicleslineup for4 or 5 hours to get to whatever destinationtheirheaded to when
thishappens, do we need to add to the situationby allowingto buildingthishousing development? It'san A’ole!!
NO to build itsomewhere else because itdoesn’t fithere on our street.This willcommence and add development
on thisroad. Dangerous as itiswhere and when many cars speed up the road (presently20mph) but,peopledo
not heed tothe signs.We know of a neighbors son who was ina hitand run accidentby a speeding car and the
driverdid not render help.The driverwas latercaught,intoxicatedand speeding. Fortunately,good neighbors
who look foreach other rendered to help the young boy and he was hospitalizedand releasedinsatisfactory
condition.
Bike Pathway and Sidewalk. There are none and many bikecyclistutilizethisroad forexercising,going to the
store, bank and etc.
2.
3.
Mahalo fortakingthe time to listento my concerns regardingthishousing development. We hope you listento our
experiences and concerns on thismatter and why we oppose thisproject.
Respectfully,
Walter & OpuulanI Kaauamo Kuluhiwa

Aloha Chair Lee and members,
My name isChrisLaidlaw and Iam writingto you today to ask you not to support bill107, CDl (2022).Ihave been
working inthe constructionindustryon Maui for17 years,and during thistime Ihave participatedinthe buildingof over
500 affordableor workforce homes. Iam a huge supporterof eitheraffordable,workforce or justreasonably priced
houses forour Maui residents.Unfortunatelyinthese unprecedented times Idon'tthinkthatwe can cut salespricesby
20-25%. Land costsand development costswith allthe permitting and red tape along with risinglaborcostsand the
unforeseen construction material hikes of 25-100% have made itchallengingfora developer/ builderto make a profit
with even the currentworkforce salespricing.Ido not want companies gettingrichoffof providingreasonablepriced
houses forour Maui people but 1know that nobody isgoing to go through years of tryingto develop workforce housing
ifthey are going to take a losson the project.Iwant the buildingof affordablehouses to be sustainablebut afterdoing
the numbers with current cost of land and construction versus proposed new salesprices,Ido not see thisworking. I
hope thatthe stateand county can help reduce land and development costs because 1do not see a way to reduce
construction costs by 20-25% while stillproviding a safe and well builthouse. Iliveinwaikapu gardens builtby Spencer
Homes and have seen and experienced firsthand what a differencebeing able to buy a affordablypricedhouse can
make on families.Iwant more people to experiencethe blessingof buying a home but am worried that developers and
builderswillpullout of the workforce market ifthe numbers don't make sense. 1ask you allto consider the long term
effects that this billcould have.
Thank you foryour time reading thisand foryour serviceto the Maui people.
Chris Laidlaw
47 west makahakaha loop
Wailuku HI 96793

4ello,
My father& Iare both residentsinthe area near the proposed Hale Waipuilani housing
development projectitem # CR22-61.
We are opposed tothisdevelopment formany safety& environmental reasons.
Please consider our followingconcerns as thisprojectwould put my father& HI wildlifeindanger.
● An ingress/egress road to Kauha’a willcreate more trafficon the already busy road.
● The area has an aging infrastructure,no sidewalks, and thisdevelopment willcreate more trafficand
put home owners walking inthe area at risk,especiallythe elderlypopulation,likemy 83 year old father
whom ishearing impaired.Also more dangerous forchildreninthe area that are more focused on
playing than lookingforcars.This oversized development ispoorly placed in a flood plainand notorious
flood zone.
● This high-densityprojectwillincreasetrafficproblems for localresidents and South Maui.
● Trafficproblems would decrease the area safety,by increasingthe number of residentsneeding
evacuation during naturalhazards, tsunamis, etc.
● Increased Trafficreduces the abilityof EMS to access these areas and reduces theirabilityto provide
timelyresponses. This could be lifethreateningformy 83 year old fatherwith a heart condition.
● exemptions should not be allowed to Zoning laws and SMA requirements.
● The projectdoes not solve housing problems forlocalpeople (50 percent or more could be sold to new
residentsfrom the mainland etc).
● Local residentshave informed the Builderabout the potentialharm to Archeological Sites (including
nearshore fishponds) etc.Residents have repeatedly informed Buildersabout floodingissues,traffic
problems, and pedestrian safety issues.
● This projectshould be moved to another area - land swap, 16 E. Waipuilani Road can become a
conservation area
Mahalo foryour consideration.

FW; CC22-62 AffordableHousing Bill107
From:
Sent:
To:
OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERK
Subject:
I You don'toftenget emailfrom m-matsuda@hawaii.rr.com.Learnwhy thisisimportant
Committee Report No. 22-62; Bill107, GDI (2022)
Chair Lee and Members,
Ido not support Bill107, GDI (2022), in itscurrentform. The lackof affordablehousing forsale isthe
biggest problem when tryingto purchase a home on Maui. From permittingdelays to increases inmaterial
and labor cost,it'snot easy to buildhomes on Maui. Builders have shown thatthey can buildaffordable
housing at the currentsales prices,but lowering the pricesthatthe builderscan sellhouses forwillnot
encourage them to buildmore affordablehousing and willlikelyreduce the number of homes built.To be
productive,regulationsshould be providingincentivesand not deterrents.Please consider leavingthe
affordablehousing sales pricesalone and make housing more attainableby providingfinancialassistanceto
individualbuyers.
Thank you
Mark Matsuda
1

From:

LMc <macca20015@gmail.com>
Wednesday, July13,2022 4:52 PM
County Clerk
Testimony opposing KaleWaipuilani(CR22-61)
TESTIMONY OPPOSING HALE WAIPUILANI.docx
To:
Subject:
Attachments:
I You don'toftengetemailfrom macca20015@gmail.com. Learnwhy thisisimportant
Attached, please find my testimony inopposition to thisegregious
development proposal.Iam alsocopying and pasting below in case you
can'topen the word doc.
Mahalo,
Lara McGlashan
TESTIMONY OPPOSING HALE WAIPUILANI
Iam writingthistestimony to vehemently oppose the Hale Waipuilani housing development
proposed at 16 E.Waipuilani Rd. (CR22-61).
Iwas not able to signthe petitionagainstthisdevelopment since Iwas out of town, but very much
wish toaddmy testimonytothedocket.Thisprojectwillbe horriblefor the community, the
environment and the infrastructure of Central KThei. Here iswhy:
Flooding
Since moving to thisarea, Ihave experienced severalfloods,the latestwhich happened as a result
of the storm inthe winter of 2021. Thisstorm brought massive amounts of rainwhich led to
severe floodingand runoff,and huge pilesof heavy mud and debris- some of which stillremain,
almost a year later,piledup likedirt-snow-driftsalong ditchesand ingullies.Entire
streets makua of S. KThei Rd. were covered with mud 1 to 2 feet high.This mud, standing water
and debrissat untended for nearlya week before emergency servicescould come inand remove
itwith plows and heavy constructionequipment. Roads were damaged, people were stranded
and landscapingwas destroyed.
At our home on Kaikane Place (makua of the proposed development site),the entireyard was
under water fora week. Ican only imagine how much longeritwould take the water to recede if
allthat open land was made unavailableto absorb and filterallthat rainfall.
1
Inadequate sewer/water systems
There was alsoa recent incidentwhere inadequate, old sewer and water systems broke and
flooded the entiretyof Waipuliani Rd. makai, largeparts of S. KTheiRd. and some of
Waipuliani makua. Thisflooding happened on and offfor more than two weeks, and caused traffic
issues,a glutof mosquitoes and dangerously muddy drivingconditions.Whether or not ithas
been remediated isunclear;workers did stop the flooding,but to my knowledge, no new pipes or
systems were installedunder the streetto prevent thisfrom happening again.
Which alsoleads to the concern of that many more people adding hundreds of gallonsof water
and sewer waste to the already stressed systems. These systems are clearlyin need of updating,
and itseems foolishto thinkthat adding 28 more homes, 28 more kitchensand possibly50 more
bathrooms willnot cause major problems underground. Burst sewer pipes willbe a health hazard
for people on land and inthe ocean, and a death sentence for adjacent reefsand sea life.
Traffic/Congestion
Currently,the trafficat the intersectionof S. KTheiand Waipuilani isvery dangerous and very fast.
There isa constant lineof carstryingto turn from Waipuilani onto S.KiheiRd.,with no break in
eitherdirectionfor up to 5 minutes. This causes a backup of cars onto Waipuilani,which ishighly
disruptiveto the neighborhood.
What's more, my 9-year-oldson and Iwalk our dog at the park by the ocean inthe evenings,and
at leasttwice a week, we are almost hitby a car whose driverisnot paying attentionto us,even if
we are directlyinthe middle of the streetinthe crosswalk and other cars are stopped to letus
pass. Inthe past year at thisintersection,Ihave personallywitnessed a girlbeing hiton a bicycle,
a touristin a rentalcar hittinga pickup truck,a dog jumping out of a car intotrafficand causing
several collisions,and a car hittinga homeless man's shopping cart as he crossed the road. This
then alsocaused a trafficjam as people triedto help him collectallhisbelongings and get them
out of the street.
Adding dozens more people to an already over-saturated area willmake thistrafficand transit
problem a hundred times worse. Backups on Waipuilaniwillgo allthe way up the streetto LTpoa,
and localpedestrianstryingto crossthe road willbe in even more danger with thisincreasein
trafficand people.
Parking
Even though the projecthas a number of proposed parkingspaces, there willobviously be many
times when there willbe more carsthan there isspace, especiallywhen itcomes to transientsand
tourists.
There willbe nowhere forthese people to park,and they willlikelycreate problems traffic-wise
and parking-wiseon both Kauha'a and Waipuilani.
Local Neighborhood
2
We bought our home inthislocal,residentialneighborhood fora reason.The peoplewho live
here are the workforce of Maui. We are the policeofficers,constructionworkers,gardeners,
writers,painters,trainers,grocery checkers- the infrastructureand the taxpayersthatmake Maui
work. This neighborhood consistsof singleand multi-familyhomes with multiplegenerationsof
Mauians livingand working on our island.
There are no transient accommodations or condos makuo of S. KThei Rd. in our area for dozens of
blocks,and we likeitthat way. We can raiseour familiesina safe,quietarea,and can get to know
our neighbors on a first-namebasis.We can easilylook out for one another and keep the
neighborhood free of crime and trash.
This development of (obvious)vacationrentals,none of which are meant (orpriced)for local
people to purchase, willstressour beloved neighborhood with noise,trash and traffic,and will
change itfrom a quiet,safearea to a placeof parties,transientsand filth.
Of very greatconcern isthe possibilitythatthe county willchange the zoning laws or willgive
these buildersexemptions to accommodate theirproject.Ifthat sortof waiver ispassed itwillset
a very dangerous precedent,and could very well givethe green lightto other such horrible
projects,eventuallyburying thisentireresidentialarea of KTheibeneath commercial,
consumeristic rentals.
Other options, questionable motives
Itismy understanding that these buildershave been given the option for a "land swap," so they
can buildtheirprojectinanother area with fewer issuesand no opposition - and that they have
refusedthisoption.But why? A landtradewillbe of benefitto allparties- we willmaintain our
local,quietneighborhood and the builderswillget to construct theirfor-profitrentals.
Theirmotives are clearlyquestionable,giventheirrefusalto even consider an alternativelocation,
and thiswarrantsfurtherinvestigation.
Itisalso my understanding that the buildersfileda 'counter-petition'to counteract the one drawn
up by the localresidents.What exactlyisthispetition,and who on earth would sign it- except
the builders,theirbankers and theirlawyers?Thisseems likea bunch of legalfluffthrown at the
issueto bury itin red tape, and whatever counter petitionthey conjured up should be discarded
and disregardedcompletely.
Please see the logic,not the $
Maui is overcrowded with transient accommodations. More than 120 flightscome in PER DAY,
and unload tens of thousands of visitorsevery week. New housing such as thisproject isnot
intended to benefitthe people who liveand work here; itisintended for tourism.
Thisarea isa well-known floodzone,and hardscapingseveralmore acres would exacerbate
thisissueby leapsand bounds.
3
Thisdevelopment willexacerbateand alreadybad trafficsituation.Italsowilllikely
breakdown of our already-fragilewater and sewer systems.
cause a
The onlypeople thisprojectwillbenefitare the developerswho are blindedwith greed.And
the only people who willbe harmed are residents.
Allthey see are dollarsigns.
We are trustingthat you willsee the logic.
Please do not allow thisdevelopment to continue.
4
RECEIVEDTESTIMONY OPPOSING HALE WAIPUILANI
Iam writingthistestimony to vehemently oppose the Hale Waipui
proposed at 16 E.Waipuilani Rd. (CR22-61).
OFFICE OF THE
Iwas not able to sign the petitionagainstthisdevelopment since1w^S^blffcIwft^SR^ry
much wish to add my testimony to the docket. This projectwillbe horribleforthe community,
the environment and the infrastructure of Central KThei. Here iswhy:
Flooding
Since moving to thisarea,Ihave experienced severalfloods,the latestwhich happened as a
result of the storm in the winter of 2021. This storm brought massive amounts of rainwhich led
to severefloodingand runoff,and huge pilesof heavy mud and debris- some of which still
remain, almost a year later,piledup likedirt-snow-driftsalong ditchesand ingullies.Entire
streets makua of S. KThei Rd. were covered with mud 1 to 2 feet high.This mud, standing water
and debrissatuntended for nearlya week before emergency servicescould come inand

At our home on Kaikane Place {makua of the proposed development site),the entireyard was
under water for a week. Ican only imagine how much longer itwould take the water to recede
ifallthat open land was made unavailableto absorb and filterallthat rainfall.
Inadequate sewer/water systems
There was also a recent incident where inadequate, old sewer and water systems broke and
flooded the entiretyofWaipuliani Rd. makai, largepartsof S.KTheiRd. and some of Waipuilani
makua. This flooding happened on and offfor more than two weeks, and caused trafficissues,
a glutof mosquitoes and dangerously muddy drivingconditions.Whether or not ithas been
remediated isunclear;workers did stop the flooding,but to my knowledge, no new pipes or
systems were installedunder the streetto prevent thisfrom happening again.
Which also leads to the concern of that many more people adding hundreds of gallonsof water
and sewer waste to the already stressed systems. These systems are clearlyinneed of
updating,and itseems foolishto thinkthat adding 28 more homes, 28 more kitchensand
possibly 50 more bathrooms willnot cause major problems underground. Burst sewer pipes will
be a health hazard for people on land and inthe ocean, and a death sentence foradjacentreefs
and sea life.
Traffic/Congestion
Currently,the trafficatthe intersectionof S.KTheiand WaipuilaniIsvery dangerous and very
fast.There isa constant lineof cars tryingto turn from Waipuilanionto S. KlheiRd.,with no
break in eitherdirectionfor up to 5 minutes. Thiscauses a backup of carsonto Waipuilani,
which ishighlydisruptiveto the neighborhood.
What's more, my 9-year-oldson and Iwalk our dog at the park by the ocean in the evenings,
and at least.twicea weeky we are almost hitby a car whose driverisnot paying attentionto us,
even ifwe aredirectlyinthe middle ofthe streetin the crosswalk and other cars are stopped to
letus pass,inthe past year atthisintersection,1have personallywitnessed a girlbeing hiton a
bicycle,a touristjn a rentalcar hittinga pickup truck,a dog jumping out of a car intotrafficand
causing seve'falcollisions,and a car hittinga homeless man's shopping cart as he crossed the
road.Thisthen alsocaused a trafficJam as people triedto help him collectallhisbelongings
and get them out of the street.
Adding dozens more people to an already over-saturated area willmake thistrafficand transit
problem a hundred times worse. Backups on Waipuilani willgo allthe way up the streetto
LTpoa,and localpedestrians tryingto cross the road willbe in even more danger with this
increase intrafficand people.
Parking
Even though the projecthas a number of proposed parking spaces, there willobviously be
many times when there willbe more cars than there isspace, especiallywhen itcomes to
transients and tourists.
There willbe nowhere forthese people to park,and they willlikelycreate problems traffic-wise
and parking-wiseon both Kauha'a and Waipuilani.
Local Neighborhood
We bought our home inthislocal,residentialneighborhood for a reason.The people who live
here are the workforce of Maui. We are the policeofficers,construction workers, gardeners,
writers,painters,trainers,grocery checkers - the infrastructureand the taxpayers that make
Maui work. This neighborhood consistsof singleand multi-familyhomes with multiple
generations of Mauians livingand working on our island.
There are no transient accommodations or condos makua of S. KThei Rd. in our area for dozens
of blocks,and we likeitthat way. We can raiseour familiesina safe,quietarea,and can get to
know our neighbors on a first-namebasis.We can easilylook out forone another and keep the
neighborhood free of crime and trash.
This development of (obvious)vacation rentals,none of which are meant (or priced)for local
people to purchase,willstressour beloved neighborhood with noise,trash and traffic,and will
change itfrom a quiet,safe area to a place of parties,transients and filth.
Of very great concern isthe possibilitythat the county willchange the zoning laws or willgive
these buildersexemptions to accommodate theirproject.Ifthat sort of waiver ispassed itwill
set a very dangerous precedent, and could very wellgive the green lightto other such horrible
projects,eventually burying this entire residentialarea of Kihei beneath commercial,
consumeristic rentals.
Other options, questionable motives
Itismy understanding that these buildershave been given the option for a "land swap/' so they
can buildtheirprojectin another area with fewer issuesand no opposition - and that they have
refused thisoption.But why? A land trade willbe of benefitto allparties- we willmaintain our
local;quietneighborhood and the builderswillget to construct theirfor-profitrentals.
Their motives are clearlyquestionable;given theirrefusalto even consider an alternative
location,and thiswarrants furtherinvestigation.
Itisalsomy understanding thatthe buildersfileda 'counter-petition'to counteractthe one
drawn up by the localresidents.What exactlyisthispetition;and who on earth would sign it-
except the builders,theirbankers and theirlawyers? This seems likea bunch of legalfluff
thrown atthe issueto bury itinred tape, and whatever counter petitionthey conjured up
should be discarded and disregardedcompletely.
Please see the logic,not the $
Maui is overcrowded with transient accommodations. More than 120 flightscome in PER DAY,
and unload tens of thousands of visitorsevery week. New housing such as thisprojectisnot
intended to benefitthe people who liveand work here; itisintended fortourism.
This area isa well-known flood zone, and hardscaping several more acres would exacerbate this
issue by leaps and bounds.
This development willexacerbate and already bad trafficsituation.Italsowilllikelycause a
breakdown of our already-fragilewater and sewer systems.
The only people thisprojectwillbenefitare the developers who are blinded with greed. And
the only people who willbe harmed are residents.
Allthey see are dollarsigns.
We are trustingthat you willsee the logic.
Please do not allow thisdevelopment to continue.

OhFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERK
RE: Committee Report No. 22-62;Bill107,CDl (2022)
Dear Chair Lee,
On behalfof my adultchildrenlivingathome who can’taffordany otherhousing,Iam writing
to ask thisCouncil topleaseconsiderdeferringBill107,CDl (2022),which initscurrentform is
suretobe the“nailinthecoffin”of any new effortsby theprivatesectortobuildaffordable
housing forMaui’s working families.
Maui clearlyhas a supplyand demand imbalance of housing thattheCouncilhas notbeen able
to move the needle on. Insteadof artificiallylowering the pricethe developercan sella house
forto well below the actualcostto build it,not includingany profit,Councilshouldbe lookingat
ways to subsizethecostof landand infrastructuretoactuallylowerthecostofthehouse. That is
theonlyway tolowerthepriceand stillgethousingbuilt.
Iknow thatyou know this,havingbeen a formerHousing DepartmentDirector,butwhat Isee
Councildoingisdrivingup thecostof infrastructureby legislatingunnecessaryregulationand
concurrentlyreducingtheinventoryof landavailableforaffordablehousingby moving itinto
open spacetothedelightof thosewho don’twant any growthand wouldjustassoonexportour
children who can’t afford to live here to the mainland.
The constructioncostof the most modest new home barelypencilsatthecurrentsales
guidelines.Reducing thepricewithoutreducingthecost,orwithoutsubsidizingthebuyer,isa
surerecipeforworseningtheimbalancebetween marketand affordablehousing,built.
Pleasereconsiderthe unintendedconsequences of Bill107,wellintentionedas itmay be,by
takingmore timetoconsiderthealternativesproposedby theDepartmentof Housing and
Human Concerns and the adviceand experienceof thosewho aretryinginearnesttobuild
affordablehousing forour children.
Sincerely.

Testimony regardingBill21,outdoor lighting
To:
OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERKSubject:
I You don'toftenget emailfrom arshakn@gmail.com.Learnwhy thisisimportant
Esteemed Members of the County Council,
Iwould liketo testifyinstrongsupportof the Bill21 to restrictthe use of outdoor lighting.According to Dr Scott Fisher
of Hawaii Land Trust,Hawaii has the dubious honors of being the placeon earth where speciesare going extinctthe
fastest,so we must act now.
There are numerous researchstudiesthat linklightpollutionto the demise of birds and insects.This isan issue
recognizedbytheUnitedNations,"Lightpollutioncan cause birdsto change theirmigrationpatterns,
foragingbehavioursand vocalcommunication,resultingindisorientationand collisions"statesthe
UN "Dim the LightsforBirdsat Night"publication.
One of the objectionsto thisbillisthatlightsprovidea feelingof safety inresidentialcommunities.
This concern can be easilymitigatedby the installationof a nightvisionoutdoor home security
camera thattypicallyretailsfor$100. This isa smallpriceto pay incomparison to the permanent
damage vyeare doingto our ecosystem with lightpollution.
1relocatedto Hawaii three years ago as a tech entrepreneurand moved my company here because I
appreciatethe naturalbeauty of Hawaii and would liketo see itpreserved.My company donates data
science resources to the Hawaii Land Trust to help create artificialintelligencemodels that can
mitigatetsunami damage to sensitivewetland areas.This isan issue 1care deeply about and hope
thatthe councilwillapprove thisbillto improve the plightof our nativespecies.
Best,
Arshak Navruzyan

Kihei,HI 96753

Testimony forHaleWaipuilaniCR 22-61
To:
Subject:
You don'toftenget emailfrom kokoroots@gmaii.com.Learnwhy thisisimportant
Hale Waipuilani Testimony
Aloha mai kakou, my name isCody Nemet Tuivaiti,and Iam a lifelong residentof
Kula Kai, a member of the Aha Moku council and a steward of hui o 'Ainakuko'a o
Waiohuli Kai,a restorationprojectin the vicinityof thisproposed development.
There isa very famous 'olelono'eau that says,He Wa'a He Moku, He Moku He Wa'a,
A canoe is an island, and an island is a canoe.
What isthe differencebetween Quantity of lifeas to the Qualityoflife?
We can learna lotfrom historyand from the stewards ofthese lands because they
survived formore than 2000 years completely sustainable,but yet,in a matter of
one lifetime,1myself have seen our canoe, our Moku, fillup with water.Western
Influencehas brought a new mindset to the islands,expansion.
As a steward and advocate for restoration, itis vital that our impacts to our
environment are held to the highestregard.This involveswater quality,marine
health,culturalawareness and mitigationmeasures in the terms of sea levelrise,
runoffand storm-surge.
As we have seen recently,storm surge isvery real,and so isfloodingand runoff.I
worry because there ismore emphasis on new growth and lesson the existingwell
being of our community and 'aina.
1would liketo entertainthe idea ofthisland being turned to conservationin a land
swap as was mentioned to me as a possibility.
Being thatthisarea isalready surrounded with homes, conservationland isexactly
what thisarea needs, not justforthe environment, but forqualityoflifein the
existing community.
As much as we need good homes we need a good qualityof life.What isthe sense
thatwe cram everyone together ifeveryone ismiserable,more traffic,more
flooding,lossof environment, and lossof moralitywithin our localcommunities.
● (Morality:when localsfeellikestrangersin theirown town]
As a member of the Aha Moku council, we have made ita mission to represent core
values when itcomes to stewardship of such conservation areas,a place that is
impactfulto our running waterways, our endemic and native plants,the
continuation of a healthy hydrology cyclethat permeates through to our oceans.
Overall,a betterqualityoflife.
1ask thatwe revisitother options proposed, and remember that while we look to
the futureforour much needed affordablehousing,thatthere are alreadythousands
of homes here that also need to be acknowledged. It'sPono planning.And as
someone who grew up hisentirelifein Kula Kai,1can tellyou firsthand thatwe are
experiencing too many changes too quickly.We have been impacted, we are
strangersin our own home, we have lostmany ofthe core values we grew up with
due to the heavy influxof misguided planning.Planning ismore than justmeeting
requirements, it'ssettingstandards.
. (According to county documents, the Hale Waipu'ilaniprojectisseeking the
county's fast-trackaffordablehousing process to create28 for-saleunits on
1.53 acres.Under the county's 2.97 residentialworkforce housing policy,
developers may request exemptions or modificationsto county code in order
to build 100% affordableprojectsquickly.]

Testimony itemCC22-62 Affordablehousing Bill107,CD1 2022 COUNTY CLERK
OFFICE OF THETo:
Subject:
I You don'toftenget emailfrom kenota11@gmail.com. Learnwhy thisisimportant
Dear Chair Lee and Members
1am a partnerin a 80 unit100% affordablehousing project(saleunits)inWailuku. Ido not support Bill107,
CD1 (2022) in itscurrentform. With interestratesrising,materialcosts increasing,and permittingdelays,itis
already extremely challengingto buildaffordablehomes under the currentsalesprices.Reducing the sales
prices,as proposed inthe billwould make our projectunfeasibleto build,thus stopping any housing inits
entirety.There isan existinglack of inventory,and cuttingthe salespricewould likelyfurtherreduce inventory
and potentiallykillexistingprojects.
Please consider subsidizingthe individualbuyers and leavingthe affordablehousing sales pricesintheir
currentform. The County could best assistthe builders’effortsto buildaffordablehousing by providingthe
necessary public infrastructurefor housing.
thank you
Ken Ota

County Clerk
Testimony -Bill21 LightingOrdinance
OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERKTo:
Subject:
[You don'toftengetemailfrom lpaci(S)mills-group.com.Learn why thisisimportant at
https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderldentification]
Council Members,
Iam testifyingagainstproposed Bill21, because itconflictswith the goal of sustainabilityand adverselyaffectsthe
people inthiscommunity. Imagine how many perfectlygood, working lightfixtureswillneed to be removed and
dumped because of thisproposed bill.Most sustainableconcepts would allow existinglightingto remain in place for the
lifeof the fixture.
For example, the drivingrange at the Dunes has new fixtures.Ifthisbillispassed, the affectsto the environment would
be detrimental.To comply with thisproposed bill,itwould require rippingout entirelightpoles,concrete bases, and
newly installedfixtures.Then a contractorwillhave to dig 6-8 feet deep along the length of both sidesof the range to
installnew concrete lightbases,poles,conduits,and fixtures.
This billwillrequireunnecessary ground disturbanceand wasteful dumping of useable fixtures.Isthe County landfill
ready to accept excess constructionwaste created by thisproposed bill?Please consider the unintended consequences
of thisbillwhich willhurt our island'ssustainabilityand vote "No."
Sincerely,
Leiane Paci
General Manager
The Dunes at Maui Lani

mi JUL 13 PM % 15Testimony of
PacificResource Partnership
OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERK
Council of the County of Maui
RegularMeeting
Friday,July15, 2022
Re: 1)Amend Resolution22-119,CDl, FDl to establisha Maui Nui Community Water Systems and an
East Maui RegionalCommunity Board;and 2) Resolution22-144, CDl, FDl establishplanning
commissions forallcommunity planareason Maui,and dissolvethe Maui PlanningCommission.
Aloha Chair Lee,Vice Chair Rawtins-Fernandez,and Members of the Council:
PacificResource Partnershipstronglyopposes 1) Resolution22-119,CDl, FDl and 2) Resolution22-144,
CDl, FDl.
We stronglyoppose Resolution22-119, CDl, FDl (proposes a Charter Amendment authorizingthe
County to establisha Maul Nui Community Water Systems and EastMaui RegionalCommunity Board)for
the followingreasons:
● The Maui Islandwater use and development plan adopted by the Councilspecificallystatesunder
exhibitA of Ordinance 5335, on page 270: thata studyshould be conducted to study "water
system ownership and management models, such as water authority..."There isno evidence
that a formal study by a reputable research company has been completed to investigateand
analyzethe implementation and impacts of a countywide water authorityforMaui County.
● Concerned thatthe ratepayerwillbe requiredto pay fora countywide water authorityat a time
when Maui residentsare strugglingwith Maui'shighcostof livingand lackof affordablehousing.
The followingincludessome of the coststhatthe ratepayeror Maui residentmay have to pay
for:
o Hiringof staff,salaries,and fringebenefits:
o Officespace and equipment,
o Maintenance and operationof water systems,
o Upgrades and the rebuildof water systems,
o Potentialacquisitionor condemnation of landsto retaincontrolof water systems
● Itispremature to pass thischarteramendment when the rolesand responsibilitiesbetween the
proposed water authorityand Maui's Department of Water Supply are unclear.
fitCttK PfSOucrCfe
THF POWER OF PARTNFRRHiP WWW.PRP-HAWAII.C9li)
WVW FACEBOOK.COM/
PACIFICRES0URCEPARTNFR5HIP
llOOALAKEASTREET/iTHFLOOR' IBJHONOI..UIU/HI96813 ^

laPRPHAWAII oPHONE --> 808.528.5557

(Continued From Page 1)
As sucb;we respectfullyrecommend thatthe Councildefer thismeasure.
YTHUCC-
We stronglyoppose Resolution22-144,CDl, FDl (proposesa CharterAmendment establishingplanning
commissions forallcommunity plan areason Maui, and dissolvesthe Maui PlanningCommission) forthe
followingreasons:
The Maui CharterCommission had previouslyincludeda similarproposal intheirJanuary 18, 2022 Draft
Report to the Council,which would have eliminatedthe Maui PlanningCommission and replaced itwith
sixnew PlanningCommissions foreach of the Community Plan areas on the islandof Maui; no changes
were proposed for Lanaiand Molokai PlanningCommissions.
In a January 20, 2022 memo to the Charter Commission, the Corporation Counsel identifiedlegaland
practicalissuesto be addressed forthe proposed new Planning Commissions (See page 5 of memo
dated January 20, 2022 attachment)
The Department of Planningsubmitted writtentestimony inoppositionand recommended an
alternativeproposalthatthe Maui PlanningCommission be comprised of at leastone member of
each of the Community Plan areas.(See Department of PlanningTestimony dated January 24, 2022).
The Charter Commission's FinalReport dated February 18, 2022 included a versionof the
Department of Planning-recommended alternative,which would requirethe Maui Planning
Commission to have representationfrom each Maui Community Plan district.(See attached).
o
o
o
As such, we respectfullyrecommend that the Councildefer thismeasure.

BillNo. 107,CDl (2022)—to establisha new method of determiningthe salespriceof an affordabledwelling
unitto includethe totalhousing costsassociatedwith home ownership such as principal,interest,taxes,
homeowner's insurance,privatemortgage insurance,and homeowner's associationdues.
Friday,July15, 2022
9:00 A.M.
Aloha Chair Lee, Vice Chair Rawlins-Fernandez, and Members of the Council.
PacificResource Partnership(PRP) isa non-profitmarket recoverytrustfund which representsapproximately
7,000 men and women union carpentersand 240 largeand smallcontractors.With our expertiseinresearch,
compliance, marketing,and projectadvocacy,we are committed to buildinga stronger,more sustainable
Hawaii ina way that promotes a vibranteconomy, createsjobs,and enhances the qualityof lifeforall
residents of Hawaii.
PRP writesinstrongoppositionto Bill107, CDl, establishinga new method ofdeterminingthe salespriceof
an affordablehousing dwellingunit.
We have witnessed and continueto see how Maui's overregulationof affordablehousingprojectshave kept
thiscounty initscurrenthousing crisis.Many industryexpertshave raisedmajorconcernsaboutthis
Council'stendency to rush legislationthrough the legislativeprocesswithoutlookingatthe financialand
practicalrealitiesof what ittakesto buildaffordablehousing.Ifpassed.Bill107 willbe another regulatory
barrierto the constructionof affordablehousing.
We respectfullyrecommend thatthe Councildeferthismeasure and continuethe dialoguewith
stakeholders,includingaffordablehousing developers,to establishpoliciesthatwillincentivizethe
constructionof more affordablehousing before the problem getsworse.
Thank you forthe opportunityto submit writtentestimony.

Committee Members
RE: Comments on Bill21 (2022) Seabird and Biodiversity Protection
Aloha Chair Lee and committee members,
The Maui Hotel & Lodging Association(MHLA) isthe legislativearm ofthe visitorindustryfor
Maui County.We representover 170 propertyand alliedbusinessmembers and over 12,000
employees.
MHLA would liketo offercomments on the proposed Bill21, relatingto outdoor lightingforthe
protection of seabirds.
This Billwould impact alloutdoor lighting— from outdoor restaurants,patios,seating,golf
drivingranges,outdoor shopping centers likeMaui Mall and Queen Ka'ahumanu Center,
privateswimming pools,privatelandscaping,county fairs,outdoor concerts,and allresidential
exteriorlighting.By includingailoutdoor lighting,thisBillisaffectinga broad rangeof anything
outdoor and defined to be outdoor.
Hawaii County, which has a similarstatute,has establisheda listof preapproved fixtures;we
recommend that ifthisbillisapproved that Maui County adopt a similarlistto helpsimplify
compliance with the County ordinance.
Additionally,MHLA isrespectfullyrequestinga five-yeargrace period,ratherthan threeyears
to be in compliance.
Thank you forthe opportunity to provide testimony.
Lisa Paulson
Executive Director,Maui Hotel & Lodging Association
85 N. Church Street,Wailuku HI 96793 | P.808-244-8625 F.808-244-3094|infof5)mauihla.ore|www.mauihla.ore

Committee Report No. 22-62;Bill107,CDl (2022)Re:
Aloha Chair Lee:
The WMTA board of directorsdoes not support Bill107, CDl (2022),initscurrentform.
The lackof housing for saleon Maui isalready drivingpricesup.Our localworkforceisnotabletofinda
basic single-familyhome or rentalsdue to lackof inventory.
Artificiallylowering the pricethat builders can sellthe affordablehousingwillfurtherreducethe supplyof
homes and increasethe pricesof existinghomes. This willmake thingsworse insteadofbetter.
Instead of mandating lowering the affordablehousing salespricespleaseconsiderinfrastructure
improvements and zoning changes that would encourage home builders.
The pre-constructioninfrastructurecosts and requirements currentlyexistingneed to be examined.New
homes contributeto property tax revenues and County investment into
Subsidizinginfrastructurecostswould allow lower pricedhomes inallcategories,especiallyinthe
affordable price ranges.

County Clerk
Insupport of a Water Authority
To:
Subject:
You don’toftengetemailfrom alohapowers@gmail.com.Learnwhy thisisimportant
Dear Mayor and Council,
Aloha,thisisMegan Loomis Powers from the Huelo / Haiku area.Igrew up on our familyland that borders Ho'olawa
stream.
My two childrenand my granddaughter were born there. Ihave witnessed the stream lifeebb and flow as EMI
would take most of the water most of the time, and then stormwater would clean out the stagnant pools,
mosquitoes and allow for a small window of time when lifewould return along the stream {an exquisitelybeautiful
occurrence)-only to be driedout again and againand again over the last50 years. Thisallowed forinvasivespecies
to get a strong-holdas they survived betteralong the dry stream-bed.
Iam testifyingto offermy resounding SUPPORT forthe charteramendment to createa Water Authorityforthe
County of Maui. No more research isrequired to givethe citizensof Maui an opportunityto vote on the creationof
the Water Authority.Once itisinplace,likeany livinginstitutionthere willbe opportunityto improve upon its
structureand function,in necessary.
But we have no time to waste. Itneeds to be established sooner than later.
For too long the precious waters that sustainlifefor Maui County have been controlledby corporationswhose
bottom lineisprofit.Our watersheds, nativefloraand fauna and nativeculturalpracticeshave nearlybeen snuffed
out due to the selfish,extractivepractices.
We are currentlyat the precipiceof long-term leasesgoing intothe hands of foreigncorporationswho would then
controlour water supply. (Whose managing partners by the way, have a checkered historyofwater controland
abuse inCalifornia.)
We cannot let our water resources be in those hands.
But even ifwe were not presentlyunder eminent threatby such leasesto such entities,itwould simplybe good
management to have such an Authority in place.
Thisisnot a new concept. Many placesinthe United Statesand around the worldalreadyhave Water Authoritiesin
place helpingto protectthe watershed and manage the many competing demands on the resource.
Itislong over due that Maui County controlit'sown PublicTrustwater resourcesforpresentand future
generations.
Sincerely,
Megan Loomis Powers
1

My name isLindsey Rex, Hawaii residentsince'82
Iam infavorof HMI waterauthoritybeingmanaged atthe county level.Iam againstthe
privatizationofwaterby a foreigncorporationorany corporationforthatmatter.This isMaui and
Maui'spreciousresourcesneed tobe stuardedand manged here,county level.
In February of 2019 Mauians met with a Senator Panel regardingrightsto water between Maui
Farmers & PSP investments of Canada aka Mahi Pono.
A quote from the Maui New coverage of thatmeeting
“Urging theprotectionof East Maui’s water forNative Hawaiian use,Joyclynn Costa of Haiku saidthe
fightover EastMaui water isnothingnew and thatshe’ssaddened areafamiliesmust continuallybattle
forrights.'Thishas been going on sinceIwas born,'saidCosta,whose 83-year-oldfatherfrom Nahiku
alsotestified.“And yet we stillcome hereto thesame tablewiththesame songs.It’swhat theywould
equate to insanity.’’
This isisnot a new fight.
Historicallywe seeHawaii fightingforitslanguage,land,culture,and water.Itdoesnotneed to
be a fight.Representthe peoplewell,electedcouncilmembers.
Checks and balances arc needed or Maui... Hawaii as we know itwill not be what ithas been
so famouslyknown for.ItisnotedthatMahi Pono has invested600 millionitsfarmingoperationhere.
Ithas d ili'^’^r'^iO'^dnmpg hm:ti-o«J^niii-.inipmvpftEMI infrastructure,and currentlyisnot
using nearlytheamount of water A&B used with sugar cane.We can recognizeand honorthisbutby

around.This isMaui's terf,not a foreigncorporation's.A cohesivesymbioticrelationshipcan be

authority

between A&B and Mahi Pono. In reference to the Rebate section2.7 Ifthe seller(A&B) isunable to
legallydeliverirrigationwater sufficientforbuyer (Mahi Pono)tofullyimplementit'sfarmingplan,
then rebates in the amount of $62 million are owed from the sellerto the buyer.

Re: Committee Report No. 22-62
FirstReading of Bill107, CDl (2022)
Aloha Chair Lee,
1am an architectand do not support Bill107, CDl (2022)as proposed.Ihave participatedinseveralaffordable
housing projectsas an architectand see the struggleto make the projectsaffordable.Itisa struggleto buildaffordable
housing on Maui under the current priceguidelines,with the currentupfrontcost.Today buildersarefacingthe highest
increased costsfor materials,financingand permittingtime,as wellas infrastructurerequirementsthatdriveup the cost
of the homes. Lowering the salespricesdoesn't lower the buildingcost,or provideany path to buildersto balancethe
cost of a projectwith it'ssalesprice.By lowering the salesprices,very few or no homes willbe builtwith current
requirements and construction costs. Please leave the salespricesintheircurrentform or considertradingany sales
pricereductionwith the county or home buyers fundingthe infrastructurewitha specialbond/salestaxjustforthat
project.The County could best assisteffortsto buildaffordablehousingby providingthe necessarypublicinfrastructure
for housing or by providingsubsidiesto the Buyers.
Both my daughter moved to Oregon inpartdue to the costof livingon Maui.That opened my understandingof
how other placesfund housing development. They were ableto by used homes as young adults.The salespriceisvery
low compared to Maui, but the salestax isvery high. The end costisnot thatfarapart,but what isdifferentisthatthe
loan amount they need to qualifyfor iswithin reach,and the taxesthey pay fund what many inMaui have to includein
theirhome priceupfront. Their tax billsvary from neighborhood to neighborhood,and detailina breakdown ofthe
bonds that paid forthe water, sewer, road,school,or other publicpartsassociatedwith theirproject.The advantageof
that isalsothat the publicpartswere funded by a bond witha good interestratenotavailableto individuals,and fully
designed and integratedintothe overalltown's system.
Passinga billthatmandates a lower salespricewithout a way to make up the differencefrom actualcostwill
just resultinanother stop to housing being built.Likewhen Tavarespasseda mandated a 40% affordablecomponent to
projects.Afterthat very littlegot built.
Aloha, Anthony Riecke-Gonzales
Riecke Sunniand Kono Architects, Ltd.
Mailing Address: P.O.Box 1627, Kahului HI 96733-1627
Street Address; 2145 Wells St..Suite 302, Wailuku HI 96793
Cell;(808) 281 -1934 Desk; (808) 872-3602 Main: (808) 877-7688

Hale WaipuilaniHousing Development Testimony
To:
OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY CLERK
Subject:
I You don'toftenget emailfromjussrodi@yahoo.com. Learnwhy thisisimportant
Dear Council,
Iown a home thatispaid for(and expect to liveinforthe restof my life)inthe Medowlands HOA, directlyacross from the
proposed Hale Waipuilanihousing development. Idon'tbelievethatthisisa good idea forour community. Any new road
enteringKauha'a thatclose to South KiheiRoad willcompound the danger Iface when tryingto make my way from
Laumaewa Loop to Kauha'a then on to South KiheiRoad. There have been accidentsatthislocation.One driverhitthe
residenton the corner'smailbox and nearby electricpole.Another drivertook out a palm treeinthe frontyard of a
homeowner directlyacross from the proposed ingress/regressroad at the cornerof Laumaewa Loop and Kauha'a.The
danger iseven worse when Iam walking or ridingmy bike as there isno sidewalkor bikelane.Plentyof people walk their
dogs along thisroute and itisonly a matter of time before some tragicaccidentoccurs.Inadditiontothis,the area floods
whenever we have realrainin Kihei.1can'ttellyou how many timesthisintersectionhas been closeddown due to rain,
but I'vecome to expect itaftera good rain.This isusuallyaccompanied by a trafficjam on Kauha'a as people tryto make
theirway around Kihei'sflooded streets.
1am infavorof low cost housing, but thisprojectcould benefitbuyersfrom the mainland and would onlyincreasethe
populationof our islandinan area thatisalreadydensely populatedand difficulttogetaround safelyand ina timely
manner. Finally,given thatthe projectneeds exemptions to SMA requirementsand zoning laws,itclearlyneeds to be
relocated where these exemptions do not need to be granted.
Please keep thisarea of Maui, where my home is,safe and freefrom overcrowding by votingto notapprove the Hale
Waipuilanihousing development. It'ssimply a bad idea at thislocation.
Thank you,
Russell Riley
42 Laumaewa Loop, Kihei

Forwarded message
From: David Dorn <daviddorn808@gmail.com>
Date: Thu, Jul 14, 2022, 7:14 AM
Subject:Updated testimony:Hale Waipuilanisiteshould remain as is,and be designated as "conservation".
To: <countv.clerk(S)mauicountv.us>
Update 7/14/2022:
The Hale Waipuilaniprojectshould not be approved; We should not allow new housing to be builtinthe
floodzone. This area has multipleriskfactorsand there are many betterlocationsto buildaffordable
homes.
There are 2500 Affordablehomes inthe pipeline:Maui currentlyhas 36 AffordableHousing projectsinthe
works and according to Mayor Victorino(editorialreAffordableHousing Maui News 4/30/22),there are
2,500 affordablehomes inthe pipeline.
Disapproving willhave a negligibleimpact on AffordableHousing: Disapproving this28-unitprojectwill
have a negligibleimpact on Maui’s affordablehousing and represents only about 1 percent of affordable
housing inthe pipeline.
A land swap deal isthe best option: Maui County currentlydoesn't have the 133 milliondollarbudget to fix
drainage Infrastructureto make Kiheisafe from flooding.But the County does have land.The County has
land available at the Kihei Police Station,at Maui Lani,and inthe centralvalleynear Pu’unene. Any of
these areas are farbettersuitedforhousing, and are freefrom the flooding,and coastalhazards of
tsunami, sea levelrise,and climate change.
The Developer has been offered a Land Swap: The Maui County has offeredthe developer the opportunity
to discuss a land swap forcounty-owned land,ina more suitablelocation.Although the developer initially
agreed, they are now saying itwould take too long forthatto happen.
The County needs to protectKihei: Kiheicannot accommodate more traffic,or more housing inflood
zones, so we need to reduce development on the floodplain,and encourage a retreatfrom the shoreline.
Urban growth areas and new housing should be located on higherground, and farfrom known floodzones.
Only the County has the power to make the decisionsnecessary to make thishappen.
The best use of 16 E WaipuilaniRoad isConservation:
● This siteshould be placed intoconservation.This isone ofthe area’slastremainingopen spaces.
And itispartofthe localwatershed and wetland nexus.
1
● Keeping thislotas conservationhelps the County to meet itsgoals of Open Space preservation,
from the MIP, and KMCP Community Plan, as well as achieve the goals of shorelineretreatas
recommended inthe CARE Committee and Multiplestudies.
● Also, thisaction helps us to depopulate and relocatethe Kiheifloodzone, as was recommended by
former planning directorDavid Goode,
●fe Conserving thislotat 16 E Waipuilani Road willhelp to stabilizethe degraded watershed, and will
help preserve the site’sbeneficialfunctionas the area's de factodrainage basin.
● This also helps the County towards itsgoal of designating and acquiringland for“green drainage
infrastructure”,as recommended in the SMWP, and the KDMP.
-
Keep the property as is: Preserving thislotas is.has many benefitsand willnot overburden the local
community or itsaging infrastructure.The site’soriginalhistorichouse can remain, and continueto provide
housing. The open space and undeveloped land,can continue to provide beneficialservices.The site's
uncompacted Jaucus and Loamy sand soilscan continue to functionas a settlingarea forneighborhood
floodwaters,and the site'snativevegetation can be encouraged to provide ground cover and habitatfor
native species. Other possible uses could include becoming an urban nature park or a community garden.

SUBJECT: Bill107, CDl (2022) - Suggested Amendment
Dear Chair Johnson and Committee Members,
Iam submittingwrittentestimony to express my concerns over Bill107, CDl (2022)
Please amend Bill107 to create the mortgage subsidy component contemplated in the Maui
County Comprehensive Affordable Housing Plan.
Artificiallylowering salespricesto make housing more accessibleto Maui residentsdoes not
produce more homes. Council should establishmore tools to bridgethe gap between costto
buildand accessiblepricing.One toolwould be a mortgage subsidy to the buyers so that they
can buy the homes.
Builderscannot buildhomes ifthe salespricesare lowered to the Bill107 levels.There are
many variablesto the cost of buildinga home, one-sizedoes not fitallwhen itcomes to
construction costs. Construction costs, labor costs, interestrates,supply chain delays are not
controlledby the County or Builders.Today, we received news that cement alone will
increase 65-85% beginning next year. That means the price for concrete slabs for homes will
increase. My Wailuku Apartment project alone has increased pricing by 30% since 2019.
Costs have not historically gone down in Hawaii, only up.
IfBill107 passes,itwillineffectstop projectsfrom feasibilityand no homes willbe built
because there isno subsidy program attached to it.Rents willthen continue to rise.The Maui
County Comprehensive Affordable Housing Plan,PolicyPriority8, Recommended Action 3,
makes clearthat adjustingthe Sales PriceGuidelines was intended to be done concurrently
with a mortgage subsidy.
We feelthat the mortgage subsidy should be given directlyto the homebuyer. IfCouncil
wants to incentivizebuilders,they should assistbuilderswith separate subsidiesfor public
infrastructureand comprehensive zoning changes to encourage building.

OFFIttE OF THE
COUNTY CLERK
July 12, 2022
Council of the County of Maui
Re: Hale Waipu'ilaniWorkforce Housing Project(Kihei)(AH-17)
Dear Councilmembers:
The severe floodinginSouth Maui in December of lastyear demonstrated the
problems of poor planningdecisionsinthe past.South Maui isthe drainage basin
forthe southwest slopesof Haleakala,but itsfunctionto absorb flood waters has
been drasticallyimpaired by impermeable surfaces.
Homeowners and businesses elsewhere have sued local regulatoryagencies for
permitting construction invulnerable locations,that were then damaged by storm
and flood activity.Given what we know of climatechange and more severe
weather events, itisrisky,ifnot criminal,to buildinthe path of stormwaters.
Even ifmeasures are taken to limitflooding insubjectareas,the waters willbe
shunted to adjacent properties.Anyone buildinginwetlands and on shorelinesis
working on the "greaterfool" principlethat they can sellbefore the next disaster.
Do not put the County injeopardy of lawsuitsfrom outraged homeowners and
businessesthat have been impacted by unsound planningdecisions.
Iurge you to pursue a land swap solution to thisissueand leave the lotunder
discussionas drainage.
Thank you.
Diane E Shepherd DVM
3329 Kehala Dr.,Kihei,HI 96753
Count^GeH<

Aloha Mike:
Subject: Bill107 (2022)
Reviewing the contents of Bill107 (2022),Ifind ithard to.b^Ueve that theOrr»CE Or TK£
County Councilwould supportsuch provisionsthatwc^(d(j)j^-^^t(Hi[r^^^ltothe
affordable housing program.
The guidelines provided by HUD isused to determine the feasibilityof any
affordable housing project. Alteringthe guidelineswould cause an imbalance of
the affordability,which willresultin eliminatingthose who need
homeownership the most.
We have experienced a seriesof events and inequities,particularlyinthe past
three years.These events have created huge adversities,most notably inthe
housing industry. Risingcosts for every commodity, together with inflationand
other factors(i.e.risinginterestrates)that have deterred developers from
creating new projects. The most challenging obstacle isuncertainty of avoiding
bankruptcy.
Istronglysuggest the following:
The County should provide the infrastructurefor any affordable housing
projects.Water, sewer, roads, fireprotection,curbs,gutters,sidewalks
utilities,etc.,then, create a specialassessment districtthrough the real
property tax system to pay for these improvements over a period of time.
This can be calculatedequitably for each new home owner.
2) Defer Bill107 as itreads currently.
3) Amend the Billso that developers and contractors willhave an
opportunity and an incentive to avoid losses. This normally resultsin a
significantcontribution to our community by providing bona fide
affordable housing projects.
4) Another option isprovidingsubsidiesto qualifiedbuyers who meet the
established criteriaby the Department of Housing and Human Concerns.
Mahalo for your successfulpassage of the realproperty tax billto benefit
veterans. Ifyou would liketo join our Maui Korean War Veterans general
membership meeting on Wednesday, July20, 9:00 AM at Hale Nanea, please let
me know.
1)
Respectfully,
Lloyd K. Sodetani
